docs.title: DC-CM-INS-001 Insurance Integration Specification v1
docs.text:
DC-CM-INS-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. Insurance Integration Specification

Document ID DC-CM-INS-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — SR&ED: novel data product Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Technical SR&ED Eligibility Strong — novel actuarial data pipeline from community safety infrastructure with privacy-preserving consent management Related Documents LEGAL-CM-014

| Executive Summary This specification defines the technical architecture for CivicMesh's insurance data products. Three tiers: aggregate anonymised corridor and community data for actuarial modelling (no consent required beyond community agreement), consent-gated individual driving record API for underwriting (explicit policyholder opt-in), and incident evidence package retrieval for claims and subrogation (individual consent or legal authority). The challenge is building a data pipeline that satisfies both the insurer's need for high-quality behavioural data and PIPEDA's consent requirements — simultaneously. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Insurance Data Product Architecture
docs.text: Product API Endpoint Data Returned Consent Layer Pricing Tier 1 — Aggregate Feed GET /v1/insurance/aggregate/{region}/{date_range} Corridor risk scores, violation density heatmaps by type/time/season, road condition index by segment, infrastructure asset condition, collision hotspot analysis — all aggregated to census-tract minimum Community data federation agreement (no individual consent) Annual subscription per region Tier 2 — Individual Record GET /v1/insurance/record/{policyholderDID} 12-month driving behaviour summary: lane discipline score, following distance percentile, time-of-day risk profile, validated incident history, clean record periods — for consented policyholders only Individual CCSC member opt-in through Member App — insurer-specific consent per query Per-query fee at underwriting renewal Tier 3 — Incident Evidence GET /v1/insurance/evidence/{packageId} Video clip, GPS coordinates, timestamp, violation classification, confidence score, node credential chain, chain of custody documentation — for specific identified claims Individual consent (claims process) OR legal authority (subrogation) Per-package flat fee Tier 4 — Premium Reduction Certification POST /v1/insurance/certify/{communityId} Community coverage density report, node uptime statistics, evidence quality metrics — confirms community meets premium reduction threshold Community manager certification request Annual certification fee

docs.title: 2. Individual Record API — Consent Flow
docs.text:
• Insurer submits Tier 2 query with: policyholderDID, insurer DID, query purpose, policy renewal date;
  • Platform verifies: insurer DID has active Insurance Data Sharing Agreement (LEGAL-CM-014);
  • Platform checks member consent store: does this member have an active, non-revoked consent for this specific insurer's Tier 2 access?
  • If consent absent or revoked: return 403 Forbidden with reason CONSENT_NOT_FOUND or CONSENT_REVOKED;
  • If consent valid: generate 12-month behaviour summary from member's evidence package history → anonymise to remove specific location patterns → return summary;
  • Log query to member's consent history (visible in Member App → Privacy → Data Accesses);
  • Log query to insurer's audit log (available for annual audit under LEGAL-CM-014 Section 5).

docs.title: 3. UBI (Usage-Based Insurance) Backbone
docs.text: Technical Challenge (SR&ED) Existing UBI products (Intact MyDriving, Desjardins Ajusto) collect self-reported telematics data from the insured driver's own device. CivicMesh collects third-party validated data from the road infrastructure. The challenge is: how to construct a behavioural risk score from enforcement-adjacent data (violations detected near a vehicle) without creating adverse selection problems or proxy discrimination. Data Sources for UBI (1) Violations attributed to the insured vehicle (from opted-in enforcement data); (2) Following distance events (from TrafficMesh node footage when insured vehicle is visible); (3) Speed percentile (from OBD-II data for opted-in node-owning drivers); (4) Time-of-day pattern (from node location data for opted-in drivers). Privacy Constraint UBI data is available ONLY for drivers who have explicitly opted in through the Member App. Drivers who have not opted in receive no UBI pricing — their standard premium applies. No inference about opted-out drivers from network data. Anti-Discrimination Design Risk scoring uses only behaviour attributes, not geographic proxies. Corridor risk scores are not applied to individual drivers based on where they live — only where they drive and how they behave there.

docs.title: DC-CM-MSSP-001 MSSP Operator Programme v1
docs.text:
DC-CM-MSSP-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh MSSP Operator Programme

Document ID DC-CM-MSSP-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Technical / Business SR&ED Eligibility Minimal — service program document, not primary R&D Related Documents LEGAL-CM-010

| Executive Summary The CivicMesh MSSP Operator Programme defines the service catalog, SLA framework, operational requirements, and certification path for MSSP operators. D-Central Shield Inc. is the founding MSSP operator and the operational template from which all external MSSP certifications are derived. The programme is designed to allow D-Central Shield to hand off clients to certified external MSSPs as geographic coverage expands, while maintaining platform quality standards. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Service Catalog
docs.text: Service Description SLA Tier Required Infrastructure Operations — Platform Uptime Evidence pipeline, officer portal, NOC dashboard availability 99.9% monthly uptime. <1hr P1 incident response. All tiers Infrastructure Operations — Node Fleet Management Firmware update deployment, node health monitoring, remote diagnostics, hardware replacement coordination Firmware updates deployed within 7 days of release. Node health alerts within 5 minutes. All tiers Security Operations — SOC Monitoring 24/7 security event monitoring, chain of custody integrity, credential fraud detection, anomalous data pattern detection SOC staffed 24/7. Security incident response within 30 minutes. Silver, Gold Security Operations — Penetration Testing Annual penetration test by approved third party Annual, report delivered within 30 days. Critical findings remediated within 14 days. All tiers AI/Model Operations — Continuous Retraining Violation detection model performance monitoring, false positive rate tracking, retraining on new violation footage False positive rate reviewed monthly. Retraining initiated if FP rate exceeds threshold. All tiers Evidence and Legal Support — Court-Ready Packages Production of certified evidence packages for contested citations. Chain of custody documentation for legal proceedings. Evidence packages produced within 4 hours of request. Court appearance support documentation within 48 hours. All tiers Compliance and Audit — PIPEDA Reporting Annual privacy compliance report, breach notification management, privacy impact assessments for new deployments Annual report within 90 days of fiscal year end. Breach notification within 24 hours. All tiers Partner Support — Onboarding and Training New client onboarding, community manager training, officer portal training for municipal clients Onboarding completed within 10 business days. Training sessions scheduled within 5 days of request. All tiers

docs.title: 2. MSSP Certification Tiers
docs.text: Tier Requirements Revenue Sharing Territory Bronze — Entry CivicMesh MSSP Operator Track certification (2 staff minimum). Basic infrastructure (dedicated NOC workstation, 24/7 on-call rotation). E&O insurance $2M. Cyber insurance $2M. Standard platform fee schedule. No cooperative patronage. Non-exclusive. Single city or region. Silver — Established All Bronze requirements. SOC 2 Type II equivalent. 24/7 NOC staffing (minimum 2 concurrent). E&O $5M. Cyber $5M. Annual CivicMesh audit passed. Standard fees + 5% cooperative patronage return on net platform revenue from managed communities. Non-exclusive. Up to 2 cities or regions. Gold — Premier All Silver requirements. Dedicated CivicMesh NOC team (minimum 4 FTE). MSSP Cooperative Gold membership. D-Central endorsement for municipal RFPs. Standard fees + 10% cooperative patronage + co-branding rights + early feature access. Exclusive territory option available. National scope eligible.

docs.title: 3. D-Central Shield — Founding MSSP
docs.text: Role D-Central Shield operates as MSSP for all D-Central-direct clients (OC Transpo pilot, first HOA deployments) and as the operational proof of concept from which the MSSP programme standards are derived. Transition Plan As external MSSPs are certified, D-Central Shield transitions clients in mature markets to external MSSPs and focuses on: (a) highest-complexity or highest-sensitivity clients, (b) new market entry (first client in a new city), (c) MSSP certification oversight and auditing. Conflict Management D-Central Shield competes with external MSSPs on equal terms within the MSSP Cooperative governance structure. D-Central retains a permanent founding member vote in the MSSP Cooperative to prevent hostile governance by any single large MSSP.

DC-CM-MSSP-CERT-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. CivicMesh MSSP Certification Programme

Document ID DC-CM-MSSP-CERT-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Technical SR&ED Eligibility Minimal — programme management document Related Documents DC-CM-MSSP-001

docs.title: 1. Certification Tier Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Bronze Silver Gold Certified MSSP Operator Track staff Minimum 2 Minimum 4 Minimum 8 (dedicated CivicMesh team) NOC staffing Business hours (8am–8pm M–F) Extended hours (6am–10pm 7 days) 24/7 dedicated NOC Security certification Annual pen test by approved firm SOC 2 Type II equivalent SOC 2 Type II + annual D-Central audit E&O insurance $2M CAD $5M CAD $10M CAD Cyber liability insurance $2M CAD $5M CAD $10M CAD Platform uptime SLA 99.5% 99.9% 99.95% P1 incident response 4 hours 1 hour 30 minutes MSSP Cooperative membership Required Required Required — Gold membership Annual audit Self-assessment D-Central desk review D-Central on-site audit

docs.title: 2. Certification Process
docs.text:
• MSSP submits application with: company profile, insurance certificates, security certification evidence, staff credential inventory, technical infrastructure overview
  • D-Central reviews documentation — 15 business days
  • For Silver/Gold: D-Central conducts technical infrastructure assessment (remote or on-site)
  • Certification decision communicated with detailed findings
  • On approval: MSSPOperatorCredential issued to MSSP DID, on-chain registration, MSSP Partner Agreement (LEGAL-CM-010) executed
  • Annual renewal: submit updated documentation → abbreviated desk review → credential renewed

docs.title: 3. Ongoing Compliance Monitoring
docs.text: Automated Monitoring D-Central NOC monitors all certified MSSP deployments: uptime vs SLA, incident response times, evidence pipeline integrity, federation protocol compliance. Automatic alerts on SLA breach. Quarterly Review D-Central reviews MSSP performance report quarterly. Persistent SLA breaches trigger corrective action plan. Credential Suspension Immediate credential suspension triggers: security breach affecting community data, unauthorized data sharing, AGPL licence violation, loss of required insurance. Suspension logged on-chain within 24 hours.

docs.title: FIN-CM-001 Founder Cash Flow Model v1
docs.text:
FIN-CM-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. Founder Cash Flow Model — 18-Month Pre-Investor Build

Document ID FIN-CM-001 Version 1.0 Status PRIORITY — complete before any spending or salary draw Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Financial Purpose Month-by-month cash flow projection for 18-month development period. Governs salary draw, IRAP timing, SR&ED refund planning, credit card float management. Related Documents IRAP-CM-001

| Executive Summary This document is a framework for the founder cash flow model. The actual model should be built in a spreadsheet (FIN-CM-001.xlsx — to be created) with monthly columns for 18 months. The purpose is to ensure the company never runs out of cash during the development phase, to understand the IRAP reimbursement timing, to plan the SR&ED annual refund, and to manage the business credit card float between spending and reimbursement. The framework below provides the structure and key assumptions. | |---|

Complete the spreadsheet version of this model (FIN-CM-001.xlsx) before drawing any salary, before submitting the IRAP application, and before making any R&D expenditure. The cash flow model is the instrument panel for the entire funding strategy.

docs.title: 1. Revenue Sources — Monthly Timing
docs.text: Revenue Source Amount Timing Notes IRAP salary reimbursement — founder 80% of eligible monthly salary Monthly — approximately 30–45 days after milestone report submission Depends on ITA approval and monthly report acceptance. First payment typically Month 2–3. IRAP salary reimbursement — additional staff 80% of eligible monthly salary per staff member Monthly — same lag as above Add to model when each staff member is hired IRAP contractor reimbursement 50% of eligible contractor invoices Monthly — with regular milestone report Contractor invoices must be submitted with milestone report SR&ED federal ITC refund Up to 35% of net eligible expenditures (after IRAP reduction) Annual — typically June/July after April filing Budget conservatively: assume 90 days after filing Ontario SR&ED top-up Up to 30% of net eligible expenditures Annual — with federal filing Some lag from federal; consolidate in same month estimate CommunityShield HOA NOC fees $[Monthly fee per HOA] × number of HOA clients Monthly — from first HOA client signing Model conservatively: 3 HOA clients by Month 6, 5 by Month 9 Training certification fees $299–$1,200 per candidate × candidates per month Monthly — as candidates enroll Conservative: 5 candidates/month from Month 2, 15/month by Month 6 Mitacs Accelerate contribution return $7,500 per unit (company contribution required) One-time per unit — company pays out, does not receive This is a NET COST — but generates $15,000 in research value ISC Phase 1 — tranche 1 50% of award (up to $75,000) On contract signing — approximately Month 10–12 if applied Month 6 Only if ISC challenge matches and application is accepted ISC Phase 1 — tranche 2 50% of award (up to $75,000) On PoC acceptance — approximately Month 16–18

docs.title: 2. Expenditure Categories — Monthly Timing
docs.text: Expenditure Monthly Amount Payment Method SR&ED? IRAP? Founder salary — eligible R&D portion $5,833–$6,667 (= $70K–$80K/yr) Payroll — must be through payroll, not ad hoc transfer Yes — reduced by IRAP Yes — 80% reimbursed Founder salary — non-eligible portion (admin, sales) $0–$1,000 estimate Payroll No No Corporate income tax remittances (payroll deductions) ~25–30% of gross salary CRA payroll account — due 15th of following month N/A N/A HST remittances (if revenues exceed $30K/year) Collect and remit quarterly CRA HST account N/A N/A Cloud computing — R&D (AWS/GCP training instances) $300–$800/month (varies with training runs) Business credit card Yes Yes (as materials) Software subscriptions (GitHub Teams, Linear, Harvest) ~$100–200/month Business credit card Partial Partial Hardware purchases — R&D prototypes $2,000–$5,000 in Months 1–6; $1,000/month thereafter Business credit card Yes Yes (as materials) SR&ED consultant — Year 1 engagement $3,000–$8,000 total (typically on contingency at 15–25% of refund) Invoice Yes — as contractor No Legal fees — incorporation, USA, IP assignment $3,000–$8,000 in Month 1 Invoice — or DIY where possible No No Trademark filing fees — CIPO $458/mark × 8 marks = ~$3,664 CIPO online No No Domain registration — 15 domains × 2 years ~$300 total Business credit card No No Mitacs partner contributions (if pursuing) $7,500 per unit — one-time Invoice to Mitacs No — contribution not SR&ED eligible (research output is) No HOA installation costs (when CommunityShield deploying) $1,500–$3,000 per HOA site (hardware + install) Business credit card / invoice Partial — prototype only No

docs.title: 3. Business Credit Card Float Management
docs.text: The Float Problem Expenses are charged to the business credit card when incurred. IRAP reimburses approximately 30–45 days after the monthly milestone report. SR&ED refunds after filing. The business credit card carries a float of roughly 45–60 days of eligible expenses at any time. Float Size Estimate At $5,000–$8,000/month in eligible expenses (hardware, cloud, contractors), the float is approximately $10,000–$16,000 at any given time. The credit card limit must be at least this amount. Recommended Card RBC Avion Business or TD Business Travel Visa. Apply at incorporation. Request $20,000–$25,000 limit. Pay balance in full monthly from payroll account after IRAP reimbursements arrive. Cash Buffer Maintain a minimum $15,000 CAD cash buffer in the business bank account at all times. This covers: payroll tax remittances due on the 15th, credit card balance payment, and unexpected expenses. Do not draw below this buffer. Alert Threshold If cash balance drops below $20,000, trigger a review: check IRAP milestone report status, check SR&ED refund ETA, check HOA client billing, and consider whether to reduce expenses temporarily.

docs.title: 4. Salary Strategy
docs.text: Defensible Salary Range $70,000–$80,000 per year is defensible for a technical founder with IRAP. At $75,000, IRAP covers $60,000 (80%) and you pay $15,000. After SR&ED on the remaining 20%, net cost is approximately $12,000–$14,000/year. This is the most capital-efficient way to pay yourself. Why Not Higher $100,000 is possible but requires IRAP to accept it as market-rate for a student founder. $75,000–$80,000 is market-rate for a senior developer in Ottawa without founder markup. Discuss with your ITA before setting the salary. Why Not Lower Paying yourself less than $60,000 is a false economy. IRAP covers 80% — the incremental cost of an additional $10,000 salary is only $2,000 out of pocket (20%). Take the market rate. Payroll Setup Must be set up through a formal payroll system (QuickBooks Payroll, Wagepoint, or similar) before the first paycheque. Payroll deductions (CPP, EI, income tax) must be remitted to CRA by the 15th of the following month. This is non-negotiable — late remittances attract penalties. Mitacs Entrepreneur Stipend If Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur is approved ($7,500/unit), this is paid as a stipend — it is not a salary and does not reduce IRAP-eligible salary. It supplements the salary draw.

docs.title: 5. 18-Month Summary (Framework — Populate in Spreadsheet)
docs.text: Month Key Financial Event Approximate Cash In Approximate Cash Out Cumulative Position 1 Incorporation, payroll setup, first salary draw, hardware purchases $0 (no IRAP yet) $8,000–$12,000 (legal, hardware, salary) ($8,000–$12,000) 2 First IRAP milestone report submitted. Training certification revenue begins. $3,000–$5,000 (training fees) $7,000–$9,000 (salary, cloud, subscriptions) Improving 3 First IRAP reimbursement received (~$4,800 = 80% of $6,000 eligible salary for Month 1) $7,000–$10,000 (IRAP + training) $7,000–$9,000 Stabilizing 4–6 IRAP running monthly. First HOA client signed. $10,000–$15,000/month $8,000–$12,000/month Positive or break-even 7–9 Additional technical staff hired (if funded). 3 HOA clients. IRAP expanded to cover staff. $15,000–$20,000/month $15,000–$18,000/month Positive 10–12 ISC Phase 1 tranche 1 if awarded ($75,000). Mitacs research partner active. $20,000–$30,000/month $15,000–$20,000/month Strong positive 13–18 SR&ED Year 1 refund received ($25,000–$35,000). OC Transpo pilot contract signed. ISC Phase 1 tranche 2. $30,000–$50,000/month $20,000–$25,000/month Investor-ready

docs.title: FIN-CM-002 CivicMesh Unit Economics Model v1
docs.text:
FIN-CM-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Unit Economics Model

Document ID FIN-CM-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Financial Purpose Per-node economics, per-community model, municipal contract model, path to profitability Related Documents FIN-CM-001

docs.title: 1. Per-Node Economics
docs.text:
| Executive Summary The unit economics model answers the fundamental question: what does a single CivicMesh node cost to deploy and operate, and what revenue does it generate? The answer determines: (1) the minimum viable network density for profitability, (2) the MSSP operator margin, (3) the civilian opt-in incentive that drives adoption, and (4) the insurance data value per node-observation-day. | |---|

Economic Item Amount (CAD) Notes HARDWARE — One-Time Per Node Node hardware cost (DCMC wholesale) $800–$2,500 Tier 1 (Jetson Orin NX + full sensor suite): $2,500. Tier 3 (contractor kit): $800. Tier 2: ~$1,800 Installation labour (certified technician) $150–$500 Tier 1 (permanent): $500 (4-hour install + credential ceremony). Tier 3 (5-min install): $150. Tier 2: $350 Total hardware + install $950–$3,000 Amortized over 3-year node life: $26–$83/month OPERATING COSTS — Monthly Per Node MSSP NOC management fee $15–$30 Includes: health monitoring, firmware updates, evidence pipeline, NOC support. Volume discounts at 100+ nodes. Cellular data (LTE SIM) $8–$15 Dependent on evidence upload volume. Bus route node: ~$12/month at average violation rate. Cloud infrastructure (S3, compute, DB) $3–$8 Per-node share of platform infrastructure costs. Decreases with scale. Total monthly operating cost $26–$53 Before amortized hardware. Full cost incl. amortization: $52–$136/month REVENUE — Monthly Per Node Citation revenue (city fleet node) $80–$400 Highly variable: bus stop obstruction on Transitway corridor: high. Residential plow route: lower. Average target: $150/month at steady state. Citation revenue — MSSP share (20%) $16–$80 MSSP operator retains 20% of gross citation revenue attributed to managed nodes. Digital twin data subscription contribution $2–$8 Per-node share of insurer/municipal data subscription revenue. Delivery network nodes: higher share. Training certification (indirect) $0 direct Technicians certified to install the node → training revenue flows to CTC. Not per-node but driven by node count. Total MSSP revenue per node per month $18–$88 At steady-state citation volume NODE ECONOMICS SUMMARY MSSP break-even (months from activation) 6–18 months Varies by node tier and enforcement corridor. Tier 1 bus route: 8–12 months. Tier 3 contractor kit: 3–6 months (lower hardware cost). MSSP NPV per node (3-year life, 15% discount) $400–$2,200 Wide range reflects corridor variability. Portfolio approach required — high-value corridors cross-subsidize low-value coverage.

docs.title: 2. Per-Community Economics (HOA/Condo)
docs.text: Economic Item Small HOA (20 nodes) Medium Condo (60 nodes) Large Campus (150 nodes) Hardware + install (one-time) $20,000–$40,000 $60,000–$120,000 $150,000–$300,000 Monthly platform fee (NOC management) $600–$800 $1,800–$2,400 $4,500–$6,000 Monthly citation revenue — community share (60%) $1,000–$3,000 $3,000–$9,000 $7,500–$22,500 Annual insurance premium reduction (est.) $2,000–$5,000 $6,000–$15,000 $15,000–$40,000 Annual net benefit (revenue + savings - fees) $6,400–$30,600 $19,200–$91,800 $48,000–$229,500 Payback period on hardware 4–12 months 4–12 months 4–12 months

docs.title: 3. Municipal Contract Economics (OC Transpo Pilot → City Fleet)
4. Path to Profitability
docs.text: D-Central Shield break-even D-Central Shield (founding MSSP) breaks even when: Monthly MSSP revenue (20% citation share + NOC fees across managed nodes) exceeds: D-Central Shield operating costs (NOC staff, infrastructure, legal, admin). Estimated break-even: 150–200 nodes under management. OC Transpo pilot alone (30 nodes) does not achieve break-even — city fleet Phase 2A does. CivicMesh Inc. platform break-even CivicMesh Inc. (platform IP layer) earns: platform fees from D-Central Shield and external MSSPs, insurance API subscriptions, data marketplace subscriptions. Platform break-even estimated at: 3 active MSSP operators + 1 insurance subscriber + 500 active nodes under management. Cooperative patronage sustainability CCSC distributes 80% of citation revenue share to members. CCSC retains 20% for operations. CCSC becomes operationally self-sustaining when: monthly citation revenue share exceeds $15,000 (operations threshold). At 500 managed nodes generating average $150/month: $75,000 gross × 15% CCSC share = $11,250/month — approaching threshold.

docs.title: FIN-CM-003 Investor Pitch Financial Model v1
docs.text:
FIN-CM-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Investor Pitch Financial Model

Document ID FIN-CM-003 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Financial Purpose Government funding coverage, equity ask, use of funds, Series A milestones, comparable valuations Audience Seed and Series A investors Related Documents FIN-CM-001

docs.title: 1. The Government Funding Advantage
docs.text:
| Executive Summary Before walking into an investor meeting, D-Central Group will have received approximately $300,000–$600,000 in non-dilutive government funding covering 70–85% of development costs. The investor is not being asked to fund development risk — they are being asked to fund scaling a working, government-validated product. This is a categorically different investment proposition. | |---|

Programme Amount Timing Investor Dilution NRC IRAP — Salary reimbursement (18 months) $126,000–$144,000 Monthly (cash flow positive from Month 2) Zero — non-dilutive grant SR&ED — Annual refund (Year 1) $25,000–$45,000 Annual (Month 14–18) Zero — tax credit Mitacs Accelerate (3 units) $45,000 in research labour value Per internship unit Zero — research partnership Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur $7,500–$15,000 stipend Per internship unit Zero — personal stipend ISC Phase 1 (if awarded) Up to $150,000 On award and PoC delivery Zero — competitive grant Training revenue (pre-investor) $30,000–$75,000 Monthly from Month 2 Zero — earned revenue Total non-dilutive before investor meeting $233,500–$429,000 Before investor closes Zero equity given

docs.title: 2. Seed Round Ask
docs.text: Item Detail Amount $500,000–$750,000 CAD Instrument SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity) or convertible note — typical for pre-revenue seed Valuation cap $4,000,000–$6,000,000 CAD pre-money (justified by: government validation, live OC Transpo pilot, insurance partner LOI, comparable valuations) Equity dilution at conversion 10–15% on a standard conversion scenario Target close Month 12–15 — after OC Transpo pilot live and first citation revenue flowing Use of funds (1) Additional technical staff — 1 ML engineer, 1 backend engineer: $180,000/year; (2) Phase 2 city fleet hardware: $100,000; (3) Legal — franchise disclosure, provincial regulatory work: $40,000; (4) Marketing and business development: $50,000; (5) Working capital buffer: $30,000–$130,000

docs.title: 3. Series A Readiness Milestones
docs.text: Milestone Target Timing Why It Matters to Series A Investors OC Transpo pilot live and generating revenue Month 8–10 Proof that municipal clients pay. Real revenue, not projected. City of Ottawa fleet expansion contract signed Month 14–18 Second client. Replicability demonstrated. Revenue scaling. First external MSSP certified and operating Month 15–20 Channel model proven. Revenue not dependent on D-Central Shield alone. Insurance API — first Tier 1 subscriber Month 18–24 Data product revenue demonstrated. Insurance market validation. CCSC cooperative membership — 500+ members Month 18–24 Network density milestone. Community ownership moat quantified. SR&ED + IRAP documented and filed Month 12–14 Government validation of technical merit. De-risking evidence for institutional investors. Series A ask Month 20–28 $3M–$5M for national expansion, full team build-out, insurance API scale, DECU regulatory path

docs.title: 4. Comparable Valuations
docs.text: Company Description Valuation / Exit Relevance Flock Safety US fixed ALPR + community safety network — 5,000+ cities, 20B scans/month $7.5B (2024 Series E) Direct competitor. CivicMesh's community-owned, privacy-preserving model addresses Flock's primary vulnerability. Nexar Crowdsourced digital twin + road intelligence $500M+ (2023) CivicMesh's digital twin module is a superior version of Nexar's core product (systematic city fleet coverage vs. random civilian coverage). ShotSpotter / SoundThinking Acoustic detection network — 150+ cities $300M acquisition (2023) MeshEar is a privacy-preserving, community-owned alternative to ShotSpotter's exact market. Waze Crowdsourced navigation $1.3B acquisition by Google (2013) MeshNav is a cooperative-owned, infrastructure-data-backed alternative to Waze's model. Moovit (transit data) Public transit data and routing $900M acquisition by Intel (2020) MeshNav's transit integration and OC Transpo data products address this market.

docs.title: 5. The Investor Framing
docs.text: Wrong framing (avoid) 'We are building a traffic camera company in Ottawa.' Right framing 'We are building the protocol layer for community-owned civic safety infrastructure — the platform that Flock Safety, Ring, and ShotSpotter wish they had built, positioned at the intersection of a $38M Ottawa revenue gap, a $7.5B market category, and a cooperative model that makes our users into our most powerful political advocates.' Government validation argument 'The NRC's Industrial Technology Advisor has reviewed our technical uncertainty and approved IRAP funding. CRA has accepted our SR&ED claim structure. An ISC Phase 1 application is pending with Transport Canada. When the federal government funds your R&D, validates your technical merit, and your first client is a publicly-funded transit authority — you are not a startup anymore. You are a growth-stage company that hasn't needed outside capital yet.' The ask in context '$500,000 to accelerate what the government has already validated. Not to build something new — to scale something proven.'

Year 2 — Algonquin onboarded Algonquin Continuing Education delivers Track 1 L1 and L2 to their security integrator network. Estimated 250 certifications/year at $299/$499 average: ~$98,750 gross × 60% = $59,250 to Algonquin. Algonquin's ROI $59,250 revenue in Year 2 for a programme that requires: instructor training (D-Central provides), LMS setup (existing Brightspace infrastructure), curriculum materials (provided by CTC). Marginal cost to Algonquin is minimal. ROI is very high. Mitacs leverage Algonquin as accredited CTC training provider + Algonquin as Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur incubator = two simultaneous Algonquin relationships. Both are referenced in the Mitacs application. Combined, they make the application significantly more competitive.

docs.title: FIN-CM-005 MSSP Cooperative Revenue Model v1
docs.text:
FIN-CM-005 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh MSSP Cooperative Revenue Model

Document ID FIN-CM-005 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Financial Purpose Per-partner economics, platform fee structure, patronage return formula, Year 1–5 projections for MSSP partners Related Documents FIN-CM-002

docs.title: 1. MSSP Partner Revenue Model
docs.text: Revenue Stream How It Works MSSP Margin Scale Driver Citation revenue share MSSP retains 20% of gross citation revenue from all nodes under management. City receives 65-70%, CCSC 10-15%. Gross: 20% of citation revenue. Net after NOC costs: 8–12% of gross citation revenue. Linear with node count and enforcement corridor density. NOC management fees MSSP charges clients $20–$40/node/month. MSSP pays CivicMesh platform $8–$15/node/month. Margin: $5–$25/node/month. $5–$25/node/month depending on tier and volume. Linear with node count. Installation fees MSSP charges clients $500–$2,500/node installation. MSSP pays technician $150–$500. Margin: $200–$2,000 per installation. $200–$2,000 per installation (one-time). Linear with new deployments. Declines as market matures. MSSP Cooperative patronage return Members receive X% of net platform fee revenue (post-D-Central operating costs) pro-rata by nodes under management. Additional 5–10% uplift on platform revenue proportional to managed node count. Scales with total platform revenue — compounds as platform grows. Training certification fees MSSP Staff Track 2 certification: MSSP pays $1,200/staff member. MSSP can charge this to clients as part of implementation fees. Pass-through — no direct margin but enables client billing. Linear with MSSP headcount growth.

docs.title: 2. MSSP Economics at Scale — Illustrative Example
3. MSSP Cooperative Patronage Model
docs.text: Patronage pool After D-Central operating costs, [X]% of net platform fee revenue is pooled for cooperative distribution. Distributed to MSSP Cooperative members pro-rata by their nodes under management as a percentage of total managed network. Example at 1,000 total managed nodes, MSSP A has 200 MSSP A's share: 200/1,000 = 20% of cooperative patronage pool. If pool is $50,000/year: MSSP A receives $10,000 cooperative patronage in addition to their direct revenue. Why this matters for MSSP adoption The cooperative patronage means a small MSSP with 50 nodes under management benefits from the growth of the entire network — not just their own deployments. This incentivizes all MSSP operators to grow the platform, not just their own client base.

docs.text: Incident Type Current Cost (Ottawa Police / Bylaw) TrafficMesh + Guard Cost Savings Per Incident Noise complaint — patrol car dispatch $250–$500 (officer time + vehicle) $75–$150 (Guard dispatch) $100–$350 per incident Trespassing — bylaw response $150–$300 (officer time) $50–$100 (Guard dispatch) $50–$200 per incident Parking violation — bylaw officer $80–$150 (officer time) $0 (platform citation, no dispatch) $80–$150 per incident Snow route violation — bylaw $80–$150 (officer time to locate and document) $0 (automated platform citation) $80–$150 per incident

docs.title: 3. Private Investigation Integration
docs.text: What this is D-Central Guard Cooperative members who also hold private investigator licences (PSIA dual licence) can receive referrals for follow-up investigation work from D-Central Forensics. Revenue model PI referral fee: $0 to Guard Cooperative (relationship-based). PI investigation fees: $75–$150/hour billed directly by PI to client (insurance company, HOA, municipality). D-Central Forensics earns an evidence package preparation fee separately. Why it matters Many enforcement and community safety incidents lead to insurance claims or legal proceedings that require investigation beyond the CivicMesh evidence package. Having PI capability in the Guard Cooperative creates a full-service response capability that no competitor can offer.

docs.title: FIN-COOP-001 Cooperative Patronage Distribution Model v1
docs.text:
FIN-COOP-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. Cooperative Patronage Distribution Model

Document ID FIN-COOP-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Financial Purpose CCSC formula, D-Credit vs CAD payout options, tax treatment of patronage returns, annual distribution schedule Related Documents LEGAL-CCSC-001

docs.title: 1. CCSC Patronage Distribution Formula
docs.text: Revenue Source CCSC Receives Members Receive (80%) CCSC Retains (20%) TrafficMesh citation revenue share Per commercial agreement — 15% of gross citations from civilian/community nodes 80% distributed to contributing node owners pro-rata by attributed citations 20% to CCSC operating fund and reserve CommunityShield fine revenue share 25% of community fines from CCSC-member nodes 70% to contributing node owners; 10% pro-rata to all active members 20% to CCSC fund Government node owner stipends 100% pass-through — municipality pays stipend through CCSC as administrator 100% — CCSC retains nothing on stipend pass-through 0% Insurance rebates 100% pass-through — insurer pays directly to opted-in member accounts 100% — individual negotiation between member and insurer 0% Data marketplace allocation Per data product revenue share agreement with CivicMesh Inc. 60% to active node owners pro-rata by node uptime hours 40% to CCSC fund

docs.title: 2. Patronage Payout Options and Tax Treatment
docs.text: Option Description Tax Treatment When Available CAD Direct Deposit Patronage amount transferred to member's registered bank account. Taxable income in year received. CCSC issues T4A for amounts >$500/year. Monthly for amounts >$50. Annual for smaller amounts (December). D-Credit Patronage converted to D-Credit at par (1 D-Credit = $1 CAD). Spendable within D-Central ecosystem at 5% bonus rate. Tax position: D-Credit received as patronage should be taxable on receipt at par value. Consult tax counsel before large D-Credit elections — CRA has not issued specific guidance on cooperative digital currencies. Monthly for all amounts. Platform Fee Credit Patronage applied as credit against CCSC membership fee, CivicMesh platform fees, or training certification fees. Reduces taxable patronage — credit reduces a deductible expense. Net tax effect: tax neutral vs. receiving CAD and paying the fee separately. Monthly — applied before next fee cycle. Transit Credit Patronage donated as OC Transpo Presto credit (where municipal agreement active). Charitable donation equivalent? — legal opinion required before offering this option. CRA position unclear. Monthly — if municipal agreement active.

docs.title: 3. Annual Distribution Schedule
docs.text: Activity Timing Responsible Monthly patronage calculation 1st of each month for prior month CivicMesh platform revenue accounting system Monthly D-Credit and fee credit distributions 5th of each month Automated via Member App Monthly CAD distributions (>$50) 10th of each month CCSC treasury — direct deposit batch Annual CAD distribution (annual amounts <$50/month accumulated) December 15 CCSC treasury — annual batch T4A slip generation (patronage >$500) January 31 for prior year CCSC accounting — distributed via Member App and mailed Annual patronage statement (all members) February 15 for prior year Member App — downloadable PDF CCSC AGM — patronage summary presented to members Within 6 months of fiscal year end (by June 30) CCSC board + Manager Dashboard report generation

docs.title: 4. D-Credit Velocity Incentive
docs.text: What it is Members who spend D-Credit within the D-Central ecosystem (on platform fees, training certifications, cooperative membership fees, D-Central partner merchant purchases) receive a 5% velocity bonus on the amount spent. Example Member earns $100 in patronage → elects D-Credit → receives 100 D-Credit (= $100 value). Member spends 50 D-Credit on L1 technician certification renewal. Velocity bonus: 2.5 D-Credit (5% of 50). Member's D-Credit balance: 52.5 D-Credit remaining. Economic effect The velocity incentive creates a local circular economy: patronage earned from community safety contributions is spent within the cooperative ecosystem, generating more activity, which generates more patronage. This is the cooperative economic flywheel D-Credit is designed to create. Tax note The 5% velocity bonus is additional patronage — taxable as income in the year the bonus D-Credit is issued. For small amounts this is de minimis — at scale, members should be advised to track D-Credit bonuses for T4A purposes.

docs.title: 1. Revenue Phase Architecture

docs.title: 2. Phase 1 — OC Transpo Pilot Revenue Detail
docs.text: Citation volume projection Based on NYC MTA ACE data: 30 buses on high-density corridors generate approximately 50–200 validated citations per day across the fleet. At $150 average fine: $7,500–$30,000 gross citation revenue per day. Monthly: $225,000–$900,000. TrafficMesh MSSP revenue (20%) $45,000–$180,000 per month gross citation revenue × 20% = $9,000–$36,000/month. Minus operating costs (~$4,500/month for 30 nodes): net $4,500–$31,500/month. NOC management fees $450/month (30 nodes × $15/node). Modest but recurring. Conservative projection for planning Use $5,000–$15,000/month net MSSP revenue from Phase 1 pilot for financial planning. Do not plan on upper bound — use 30th percentile for cash flow. Timing Revenue begins approximately Month 8 (after 6-month software MVP + 2-month installation and soft launch). First full revenue month: approximately Month 10.

docs.title: MKT-CCSC-001 CCSC Resident Join Package v1
docs.text:
MKT-CCSC-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CCSC Resident Join Package

Document ID MKT-CCSC-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format Direct mail, digital, and door-to-door resident recruitment materials Purpose Drive civilian opt-in for the CivicMesh Community Safety Cooperative Related Documents LEGAL-CM-013

docs.title: 1. Headline Messages by Channel
docs.text: Channel Headline Sub-headline Call to Action Digital (social, display) Your dashcam can earn you money Help keep Ottawa roads safe and earn a share of enforcement revenue. Join the CivicMesh Community Safety Cooperative. Download the Member App Direct mail (letter to Ottawa residents) Ottawa just lost $38M in road safety funding. Here's how residents can help replace it — and earn money doing it. CivicMesh turns existing dashcams into community safety infrastructure. Every validated enforcement action earns you revenue. Scan QR code to join Community bulletin board Your neighbour's dashcam keeps this street safer. Yours can too. Join the CivicMesh Cooperative. Connect your dashcam. Earn monthly income. Ottawa roads, community-owned. QR code to Member App Invest Ottawa / entrepreneurship events The cooperative that pays residents for road safety Community-owned safety infrastructure generating real income for Ottawa residents. Currently enrolling founding members. Business card with QR code

docs.title: 2. The Plain-Language Value Statement
docs.text: | What you get by joining CCSC • A share of enforcement revenue when your dashcam or doorbell camera helps document a road safety violation — typically $30–$100/month for an active Ottawa driver. • A reduction on your auto insurance premium if you opt in to the verified driving record programme — typically 8–15% annually. • A vote in how Ottawa's community safety infrastructure is governed — one member, one vote. • The knowledge that your dashcam is contributing to safer roads, not just sitting in your glove box. • The right to opt out at any time. No penalty. No minimum commitment. | |---|

docs.title: 3. What CCSC Is NOT
docs.text: Misconception Truth 'It's a surveillance programme' CivicMesh never stores continuous footage. Your camera only captures short clips when an AI model detects a potential violation. Faces are blurred on your device before any footage leaves it. 'The government is watching you' CivicMesh is a cooperative — it is community-owned, not government-owned. Law enforcement can only access your footage through a formal legal request, and you are notified within 48 hours if that happens. 'You're reporting on your neighbours' You are contributing to community safety infrastructure the same way a witness provides a statement. The violation is independently documented — you don't decide who is cited, an officer does. 'It's like Ring/Amazon' Ring is owned by Amazon. Your footage is stored on Amazon's servers. Your data is used by Amazon. CivicMesh is owned by the cooperative — your data is stored on community-owned infrastructure and is yours.

docs.title: MKT-CCSC-002 Community Dividend Programme Explainer v1
docs.text:
MKT-CCSC-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Community Dividend Programme Explainer

Document ID MKT-CCSC-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format One-page public-facing explainer — plain language, grade 8 reading level Purpose The anti-surveillance narrative anchor — positions CivicMesh as community benefit, not community monitoring Related Documents MKT-CCSC-001

docs.title: 1. The Community Dividend Concept
docs.text: | Executive Summary CivicMesh Community Dividend: Your contribution to community road safety earns you money. Every validated enforcement citation attributed to your node generates a patronage payment to your account. This is not a government programme — it is a cooperative economic model where the infrastructure you contribute to pays you back. | |---|

docs.title: 2. How the Dividend Works — Plain Language
docs.text:
• You connect your dashcam to the CivicMesh network through the Member App. Takes 10 minutes.
  • Your dashcam captures a short clip when an AI model detects a potential bus stop violation, parking violation, or other enforceable offence near your location.
  • A trained officer reviews the clip and decides whether to issue a citation. Your dashcam footage is one input among others — the officer makes the decision, not the AI, and not you.
  • When a citation is issued and collected, a share of that revenue flows to the CivicMesh Community Safety Cooperative.
  • The Cooperative distributes 80% of the revenue it receives to member node owners like you — proportional to how many validated citations your node contributed to.
  • You see your earnings in the Member App. You choose: receive as a bank transfer, spend as D-Credit in the cooperative ecosystem, or donate as a transit credit.

docs.title: 3. The Numbers — What to Expect
docs.text: Scenario Monthly Citations Attributed Monthly Patronage Estimate Commuter driver — Ottawa downtown, 30 min each way 3–8 citations/month $15–$60/month Rideshare driver — active 20 hrs/week on bus corridors 10–30 citations/month $50–$200/month HOA resident with doorbell camera — active parking area 5–15 citations/month $25–$100/month Delivery driver — routes covering all of Ottawa 15–40 citations/month $75–$280/month

docs.title: 4. The Political Argument in One Paragraph
docs.text: Ontario banned automated speed cameras because communities had no stake in the outcome. CivicMesh inverts this entirely. When community members own the network and earn from it, banning CivicMesh means taking money out of their pockets. The cooperative structure is not just ethically correct — it is the mechanism that makes the programme politically permanent rather than politically vulnerable.

docs.title: MKT-CCSC-003 Public Consultation Toolkit v1
docs.text:
MKT-CCSC-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Public Consultation Toolkit

Document ID MKT-CCSC-003 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format Toolkit for community managers and municipal partners preparing for public consultation Purpose Prevent DeFlock-style backlash by proactively establishing transparency and community benefit narrative Related Documents MKT-CCSC-001

docs.title: 1. Consultation Preparation Checklist
docs.text: Item Timing Who Prepares Purpose Camera location map — printed and digital Before any public announcement CivicMesh platform auto-generates Show residents exactly where cameras will be before they are installed Plain-language privacy notice Before installation Template from CivicMesh — customized by community manager Inform residents of their rights before they can ask Community dividend programme explainer (MKT-CCSC-002) At announcement CivicMesh provides Address 'what do we get?' before it's asked FAQ document — top 10 questions At announcement Template from CivicMesh — reviewed by community manager Anticipate and answer every obvious question in writing Law enforcement request log — initial empty At launch Auto-generated in Manager Dashboard 'Here is the log that shows us that zero law enforcement requests have been made.' Annual transparency report structure preview At announcement Template from DC-CM-GOV-003 'Here is exactly what we will publish every year and where you can find it.'

docs.title: 2. Public Meeting Agenda Template
docs.text:
• Welcome and introduction: who is presenting and in what capacity (HOA manager, city staff, D-Central representative).
  • The problem: what safety or enforcement challenge this deployment addresses.
  • The solution: 5-minute explanation of CommunityShield/TrafficMesh — what it captures, what it does not capture.
  • Your privacy: walk through the privacy protections — on-device blurring, no continuous recording, formal request requirement, notification within 48 hours, right to see and delete your data.
  • Your benefit: explain the revenue share programme and insurance discount.
  • Your control: show the camera map. Explain opt-out. Explain the annual transparency report.
  • Questions — allocate 30 minutes minimum. Do not rush.
  • Next steps: pilot start date, how to opt in, where to find ongoing information.

docs.title: 3. Hard Questions — Prepared Answers
docs.text: Hard Question Prepared Answer 'Who decides if a footage request from police is approved?' The MSSP NOC operator reviews the request against legal authority requirements. The community manager is notified of every request and every decision within 48 hours. No footage is shared without a case number and documented legal authority. 'What if there's a data breach?' All community footage is stored on community-owned hardware — your HOA's NAS device, not in a cloud company's server. A breach would affect your building's network — not a national database. If a breach occurs, you are notified within 48 hours per PIPEDA requirements. 'What if I don't want cameras in my community?' This decision is made by the community through its normal governance process. For condos, the board votes. For HOAs, the rules may require a membership vote. We require that governance process to be completed before any installation. 'Can Amazon or Google access this footage?' No. CivicMesh is not affiliated with Amazon, Google, or any US technology company. The footage is stored on your community's own NAS device. We have no ability to access it without your community manager's explicit action through the platform. 'What happens to the cameras if we want to stop?' You can cancel at any time with 60 days notice. We remove the cameras at no charge. All your footage is deleted or returned to you in standard format. CivicMesh retains no copies.

docs.title: MKT-CCSC-004 CivicMesh Privacy Promise v1
docs.text:
MKT-CCSC-004 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Privacy Promise

Document ID MKT-CCSC-004 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format Public document — plain language, published openly at civicmesh.ca/privacy Purpose The public-facing privacy commitment — must be impeccable and honest Related Documents DC-CM-PRV-001

| Our Privacy Promise — Plain Language • WE COLLECT: Short video clips when our AI model detects a potential road safety violation. GPS coordinates of the capture location. A timestamp. The AI's confidence score. A hash (scrambled version) of the license plate of vehicles involved. • WE DO NOT COLLECT: Continuous video footage. Your face or anyone else's face in an identifiable form — faces are blurred on the camera before any footage leaves the device. Your driving route for advertising purposes. Any data you haven't specifically consented to share. • YOUR DATA BELONGS TO YOU: You can download everything we hold about you, anytime. You can correct any inaccurate data. You can delete everything and leave the network — no penalty, no minimum commitment. • LAW ENFORCEMENT: Police and government agencies can only access your data through a formal written request with documented legal authority. Every such request is logged publicly (case number visible, case details protected). You are notified within 48 hours of any access to your data. • WHO HAS ACCESS: Your community manager, for safety events in your community. Officer review portal users (municipal bylaw officers), for enforcement evidence. Our technical team, for platform operations — they see metadata, not your footage content. • WE WILL NEVER: Sell your data. Share it with advertisers. Connect it to a national private database. Share it with immigration authorities without a court order. Use it to build a profile of your daily movements. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Technical Privacy Controls
docs.text: Privacy Feature Technical Implementation Independently Verifiable? On-device face blurring Face detection model runs on the camera hardware. GaussianBlur applied to all detected faces before any frame is transmitted or stored off-device. Yes — open source firmware code published. Anyone can verify the blurring runs before transmission. Hash-before-store ALPR License plates are converted to SHA-256 hashes before storage. The original plate is held in device memory only during the capture event — never written to storage or transmitted in plaintext. Yes — open source code shows the hashing pipeline. No continuous recording Non-violation frames are discarded on the device. The camera processes every frame but only stores a clip when an AI model detects a violation candidate. Yes — open source firmware confirms the discard logic. Data minimization Only the minimum data necessary for the specific enforcement purpose is included in each evidence package. Yes — evidence package schema is publicly documented (DC-CM-ARCH-001). PIPEDA compliance Privacy Impact Assessment completed and available on request. Individual access, correction, and deletion rights technically implemented in Member App. PIA available on request. Member App access functions can be demonstrated.

docs.title: 2. Your Rights — How to Exercise Them
docs.text: See your data Member App → Settings → Privacy → Download My Data. Fulfilled within 30 days. Correct your data Email privacy@civicmesh.ca. Corrections fulfilled within 30 days. Delete your data Member App → Settings → Privacy → Delete My Account and Data. Deleted within 30 days (except data required by law or active law enforcement hold). See who accessed your data Member App → Privacy → Data Access History. Shows every time your data was accessed, by whom (role, not name), for what purpose. File a complaint If you believe your privacy rights have been violated: email privacy@civicmesh.ca first. If unsatisfied: file with the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (priv.gc.ca) or your provincial privacy commissioner.

MKT-CM-GOV-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Municipal Buyer Profile

Document ID MKT-CM-GOV-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format Internal sales reference — decision-maker map for all departments, all municipalities Purpose Universal municipal sales tool for expansion beyond Ottawa Related Documents MKT-TM-GOV-006

docs.title: 1. Universal Municipal Buyer Profile
docs.text: Buyer Type Primary Pain CivicMesh Solution Budget Source Decision Authority Transit Authority CEO/VP Ops Revenue gap from enforcement ban. Bus lane compliance degrading service quality. TrafficMesh on bus fleet — enforcement revenue + service improvement data. Transit authority operating + capital budget Board of directors approval for pilot. CEO/COO for ongoing contract. City CAO / City Manager Multiple department problems (enforcement revenue, inspection costs, transit) with no unified solution. CivicMesh as city-wide infrastructure that solves multiple problems from one deployment. Executive office discretionary + departmental budgets CAO signs off on multi-department initiative. Mayor politically sponsors. CFO / Finance Director $38.7M+ revenue gap. Limited capital for new infrastructure. Mobile enforcement with no capital infrastructure cost. ROI positive within 3–5 months. Revenue replacement budget CFO approval for revenue-generating programme (lower threshold than expenditure programme). Bylaw Services Chief Under-staffed enforcement. Officers spending time on passive monitoring rather than active enforcement. Passive enforcement on fleet vehicles frees officers for complex cases. Technology handles high-volume routine violations. Bylaw operating budget + enforcement revenue Bylaw Chief recommends. City Manager approves. CIO / Digital Officer Technology modernization mandate. Smart city programme. Open source and data sovereignty requirements. Open source, privacy-preserving, Canadian-hosted, community-owned smart city infrastructure. Digital transformation budget CIO recommends. Budget committee approves.

docs.title: 2. Objection Matrix — Universal
docs.text: Objection Source Answer Supporting Document 'It's surveillance' Civil liberties advocates, opposition councillors Human review before every citation. Open source. Auditable. Community-owned. Public camera map. Annual transparency report. No continuous recording. MKT-CM-003 + REG-CM-001 'Data privacy concerns' Privacy advocates, provincial privacy commissioner PIPEDA PIA completed. On-device blurring. Canadian data residency. No commercial data broker connection. Community controls their own data. REG-CM-001 + DC-CM-PRV-001 'Vendor lock-in' Procurement officers, IT departments Open source — city can self-host, fork, or switch operators. AGPL licence prevents proprietary lock-in. All data in standard formats. LEGAL-CM-016 + DC-CM-ARCH-001 'We already have Flock Safety / Ring' IT departments familiar with incumbents Flock: US company, private database, no community benefit, DeFlock backlash in 30+ cities. CivicMesh: Canadian, community-owned, cooperative revenue share, no national private database. MKT-CM-GOV-002 + DC-STRAT-005 'Too expensive' Finance departments No capital infrastructure. Node hardware at cost price for city clients. ROI positive within 3–5 months based on OC Transpo pilot data. FIN-TM-001 + FIN-CM-002 'What if the province bans it too?' Risk-averse procurement officers Human review requirement is the legal distinction. Ontario's ASE ban targeted automated enforcement — not officer-assisted mobile evidence. Legal opinion on file. REG-TM-001

docs.title: MKT-CM-GOV-002 CivicMesh Anti-Flock Positioning Brief v1
docs.text:
MKT-CM-GOV-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Anti-Flock Positioning Brief

Document ID MKT-CM-GOV-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format 2-page competitive positioning for municipal procurement conversations Purpose Answer the 'why not just use Flock Safety or Ring' question definitively Related Documents DC-STRAT-005

docs.title: 1. Why Flock Safety Is a Political Risk
docs.text: Flock Safety Issue Documented Evidence Political Risk for Adopting Municipality National private ALPR database Flock operates a national database of plate reads shared across all client communities. Data accessible to law enforcement without individual community consent or notification. Municipalities face 'secret surveillance' accusations. DeFlock.me has 30+ organized community groups fighting Flock installations. Federal immigration enforcement data sharing Investigative reporting confirms Flock data has been accessed by ICE and immigration authorities. Sanctuary city municipalities face direct political contradiction. Legal exposure in jurisdictions with anti-cooperation ordinances. Unpermitted installations Forbes reported in 2024 that Flock installed hundreds of devices on public roads without required permits. Procurement liability. If the same practice occurs in your city, council faces questions about oversight. Zero community benefit Flock extracts data from communities and returns nothing. No revenue share, no community ownership, no cooperative governance. In a post-ASE-ban environment where the community benefit argument matters, Flock has no answer. Proprietary and unauditable No one outside Flock can verify what their system does or doesn't do with your community's data. Open source requirement policies in progressive municipalities explicitly exclude Flock.

docs.title: 2. The CivicMesh Alternative — Five Structural Differences
docs.text: Dimension Flock Safety CivicMesh Data ownership Flock owns the data. National private database. Community owns the data. No national private database. Hash-before-store. Community benefit Zero — no revenue share, no governance rights. CCSC cooperative revenue share. Member governance rights. Annual transparency report. Open source Proprietary — unauditable. AGPL v3 — full source code published. Anyone can audit. Canadian compliance US company — CLOUD Act exposure for Canadian data. No specific Canadian privacy compliance. Canadian CCPC. PIPEDA PIA completed. Canadian data residency. No CLOUD Act exposure. Political resilience 30+ organized community groups opposing Flock nationally. Flock cameras being removed in growing number of US cities. Community owns the network. Community earns from it. Political constituency for continuation, not removal.

docs.title: 3. The One-Line Pitch for Council
docs.text: CivicMesh is what Flock Safety would be if the community owned it, the data stayed in Canada, and the enforcement revenue came back to residents.

docs.title: MKT-CM-GOV-003 Government Incentive Programme Brief v1
docs.text:
MKT-CM-GOV-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Government Incentive Programme Brief

Document ID MKT-CM-GOV-003 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format Policy brief for municipal council and provincial policy staff Purpose Describe the government incentive structure that makes widespread CivicMesh adoption politically and fiscally attractive Related Documents DC-CM-INC-001

docs.title: 1. The Incentive Stack — What Government Gets and Gives
docs.text: What municipalities get (1) Enforcement revenue without capital infrastructure cost; (2) Road condition inspection cost savings; (3) Digital twin data for urban planning; (4) Reduced police dispatch costs (private security handles sub-criminal incidents); (5) Transit performance data at no additional cost; (6) Community goodwill from revenue share programme. What municipalities give (1) Hardware rebate programme: $[TBD] per node installed by residents (funded from first 3 months of citation revenue — self-funding); (2) Annual node owner stipend: $[TBD] per active node in participating communities (funded from enforcement revenue share); (3) Fleet access for installation (already budgeted city vehicles). The fiscal argument The municipality's incentive expenditure is funded entirely from the enforcement revenue the programme generates. The net cost to the municipality is zero — the incentive programme is self-financing.

docs.title: 2. Provincial Incentive Opportunities
docs.text: Programme Opportunity How to Access Ontario Smart Communities Programme CivicMesh as privacy-preserving smart city infrastructure. Ottawa pilot as reference deployment. City of Ottawa applies on behalf of programme. TrafficMesh as technology partner named in application. Ontario Municipal Cybersecurity Programme CivicMesh's open source, auditable architecture as a cybersecurity-compliant alternative to proprietary surveillance. MSSP certification and SOC 2 Type II provide compliance evidence. Federal Smart Cities Challenge Infrastructure Canada challenge funding for connected, smart city infrastructure. City of Ottawa as applicant, CivicMesh as technology partner. Year 2–3 opportunity after pilot is proven.

docs.title: 3. Insurance Incentive Integration
docs.text: For HOA and condo deployments CivicMesh facilitates introductions between community organizations and insurance partners. Coverage density certification issued after 90 days of operation. Insurer offers premium reduction to certified communities. For individual residents Residents who opt in to the enforcement network receive premium reductions from participating insurers. Government can amplify this by publicizing the insurance programme alongside the hardware rebate. For commercial fleet operators Fleet operators (Enterprise, Communauto, delivery companies) receive fleet-level premium reductions. Government can recognize CivicMesh-certified fleet operators in commercial vehicle inspection programmes.

docs.title: MKT-CS-COM-001 CommunityShield HOA Pitch Deck Narrative v1
docs.text:
MKT-CS-COM-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CommunityShield HOA Pitch Deck — Narrative

Document ID MKT-CS-COM-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format 10-slide deck narrative for HOA board meetings Audience HOA board members, strata councils, community association executives Related Documents DEPLOY-CS-001

docs.title: 1. Slide-by-Slide Narrative
docs.text: Slide Title Key Message Speaker Notes 1 Your Community Has a Safety Problem We Can Solve Parking violations, access violations, package theft — costs you money and time to enforce. Open with the specific problems this community faces. Research before the meeting: how many parking violations per month? Has there been recent vandalism or trespassing? 2 CommunityShield — Community-Owned Safety Infrastructure Not Ring. Not Flock. Owned by your community, run for your community, revenue back to your community. Stress: your HOA owns the data. The footage never goes to Amazon or any other cloud company without your manager's explicit action. 3 What It Captures Event-triggered clips only. No continuous recording. Faces blurred on-device before anything leaves the camera. Your residents' privacy is architecturally protected. Show the on-device blurring diagram. 'The camera blurs faces before any footage leaves the device. There is no technical way for us to see unblurred footage of your residents.' 4 What Your Community Earns Parking fine revenue: 60% to HOA operating fund. Node owner residents earn 25%. Platform processing fee: 15%. Make it concrete: 'If your parking lot generates 10 violations per month at $75 each: $750 gross. Your HOA keeps $450. Residents with dashcams earn the rest.' 5 Insurance Premium Reduction After 90 days of operation, we issue a coverage density certificate to your insurer. Expect 5–15% premium reduction on your commercial property insurance. Give a dollar amount if you know their premium. '5% on a $20,000 premium is $1,000 back annually.' 6 The Installation Process Certified technicians. One day on-site. Cameras at entrances and parking areas. NAS in management office. You're live within 10 business days of signing. Address concerns about disruption. 'Your residents won't know we were there.' 7 What Your Manager Sees Manager Dashboard: active nodes, incident queue, revenue, annual report. 10 minutes a day to manage. Show the Manager Dashboard screenshot. It should look simple and unintimidating. 8 Resident Privacy Rights Any resident can see where every camera is. Any resident can request their data. Any resident can opt out. Law enforcement can only access footage through a formal legal request — and your manager is notified within 48 hours. This is the slide that addresses board member concerns about 'turning the condo into a surveillance state.' 9 What It Costs Monthly platform fee: $[TBD] for your community size. Installation: $[TBD]. Payback: [X] months based on your enforcement revenue projection. Have the numbers ready for this specific community before you walk in. 10 Recommended Next Steps Pilot with 2 cameras for 90 days. No long-term commitment. Cancel anytime. Make the ask as small as possible. 'Try 2 cameras for 3 months. If you don't like it, we remove them at no charge.'

docs.text:
MKT-CS-COM-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CommunityShield Condo Corporation Pitch Package

Document ID MKT-CS-COM-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format Property manager pitch — NOC dashboard demo, resident consent workflow, insurance programme Audience Property management companies, condo board presidents Related Documents DEPLOY-CS-001

docs.title: 1. The Property Manager Value Proposition
docs.text: Problem CommunityShield Solution Time Saved Per Month Parking enforcement requires staff to physically document violations CommunityShield captures violations passively. Manager reviews clips and clicks confirm. No physical documentation. 4–8 hours/month Residents complain about safety incidents — no documentation Every incident captured with timestamp, GPS, and clip. Manager can review and respond with evidence. 2–4 hours/month on incident disputes Insurance premium reviews require documentation of security measures Annual coverage density report auto-generated by platform. Submitted to insurer directly. 3–5 hours/year Annual general meeting prep requires security summary Transparency report auto-generated from audit log. AGM slide deck auto-generated from report. 4–8 hours/year Bylaw enforcement requires resident complaints before action Passive detection means violations are captured regardless of whether a resident reports them. 1–3 hours/month on complaint intake

docs.title: 2. The Resident Consent Workflow — What to Tell the Board
docs.text: What the board needs to know 'The Condominium Act requires notice to owners before installing cameras. We handle the notice template and the 30-day notice period. You distribute it. We activate after the period expires.' What residents are told 'Your building is deploying CommunityShield. Here is where every camera will be. Here is what it captures. Here is how to see your data. Here is how to opt in to the revenue share programme. Here is how to opt out of everything.' What residents get Residents who opt in their own doorbell cameras to the network earn a monthly patronage payment proportional to citations attributed to their device. Average: $20–$60/month for active Ottawa community. What the board approves A board resolution authorizing CommunityShield deployment under the Condominium Act. Template resolution provided. Legal counsel review recommended before vote.

docs.title: MKT-INS-001 Insurance Partner Pitch Deck Narrative v1
docs.text:
MKT-INS-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Insurance Partner Pitch Deck — Narrative

Document ID MKT-INS-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format 12-slide deck narrative for insurance actuarial and innovation teams Audience Intact, Desjardins, Aviva, SGI, and other Canadian P&C insurers Related Documents DC-CM-INS-001

docs.title: 1. Slide-by-Slide Narrative
docs.text: Slide Title Key Message Speaker Notes 1 Your Telematics Data Has a Problem Self-reported telematics data: 40% of drivers modify their behaviour during the telematics period. Your pricing is based on observed performance, not actual risk. Open with the insurer's pain, not your product. Every actuary in the room knows the self-reported telematics problem. 2 CivicMesh Is Third-Party Validated Telematics Evidence-grade driving behaviour data from the same infrastructure that issues enforcement citations. Not self-reported — verified. 'When we say a driver has clean behaviour, we mean no violations attributed to their vehicle in 12 months of continuous monitoring by city fleet nodes, civilian dashcams, and delivery vehicles covering every road in Ottawa.' 3 The Data Product — Three Tiers Tier 1: Aggregate corridor risk for actuarial modelling. Tier 2: Consent-gated individual driving record API for underwriting. Tier 3: Incident evidence for claims and subrogation. Show the API documentation. Make it concrete — they should be able to imagine how their underwriting team would use Tier 2. 4 Tier 1: Actuarial Gold Corridor risk scores. Violation density heatmaps. Road condition index. Every road in Ottawa, continuously updated. 'Your Ottawa actuarial team currently uses postal code as a proxy for risk. CivicMesh gives you the actual risk — by corridor, by time of day, by season, by weather condition.' 5 Tier 2: Individual Record at Renewal With policyholder consent, pull a 12-month verified driving record — lane discipline, following distance, time-of-day profile, clean period confirmation. 'No more relying on the customer's self-reported history. No more relying on your own dongle that the customer knows is there. Third-party validated data they opted in to share.' 6 Tier 3: Claims and Subrogation Incident evidence packages — video, GPS, timestamp, chain of custody — for specific identified claims. 'How many subrogation cases fail because you can't establish fault? Every CivicMesh-network vehicle is a potential witness.' 7 The Premium Discount Flywheel Offer discounts → more customers opt in → more data → better pricing → larger discounts → more customers opt in. 'Waze for insurance. Your customers opt in because they get cheaper insurance. You benefit because your models get better.' 8 The Fraud Reduction Opportunity Staged collisions, false claims, exaggerated claims — all become much harder when the incident is documented by CivicMesh nodes. Give a dollar figure for fraud costs in Ontario auto insurance. 9 Competitive Positioning Insurer who partners with CivicMesh gets: better data, lower fraud, better pricing accuracy, marketing differentiation, community goodwill. 'First insurer in Ottawa to offer the CivicMesh discount wins the opt-in market.' 10 Privacy and Compliance PIPEDA compliant. Consent-gated. No adverse use. Annual audit. All data stays in Canada. Address the compliance question before legal raises it. Show the PIA summary. 11 Pricing Tier 1 annual subscription: $50K–$150K/year for Ottawa data. Tier 2 per-query: $15–$25 at renewal. Tier 3 per-incident: $50–$75. 'Less than one avoided fraud case pays for a year of Tier 3 access.' 12 Next Steps Tier 1 pilot: Ottawa aggregate data for your actuarial team for 90 days. No commitment. See the data before you sign anything. Make the first ask free. Get the data in front of their actuarial team and let the value speak.

docs.text:
MKT-INS-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Insurance Data Product Catalogue

Document ID MKT-INS-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Marketing Format Product catalogue for insurance actuarial and commercial lines teams Purpose Detailed description of each data product with pricing, access model, and sample metrics Related Documents DC-CM-INS-001

docs.title: 1. Product Catalogue
docs.text: Product Description Data Included Access Model Pricing Tier 1 — Ottawa Corridor Risk Feed Aggregate anonymised road intelligence for actuarial and risk teams. No PII. Updated weekly. Corridor risk scores (0–100) by postal code and road segment. Violation density heatmaps by type, time of day, day of week, season. Road condition index by segment. Infrastructure quality scores. Collision hotspot analysis. Annual subscription. API access or CSV download. No individual consent required — aggregate only. $50,000–$150,000/year depending on data depth and update frequency. Volume pricing for multi-city agreements. Tier 2 — Individual Driving Record API Consent-gated verified driving record for opted-in policyholders at renewal. Per-query billing. 12-month behaviour summary: lane discipline score (percentile vs Ottawa drivers), following distance score, time-of-day risk profile, hard braking frequency, clean period confirmation, violation count by type. Per-query REST API call. Policyholder opt-in required per insurer (managed through CivicMesh Member App). Query rejected if no active consent. $15–$25 per query. Query charged on submission regardless of result. Tier 3 — Incident Evidence Package Specific incident footage for identified claims — subrogation, fraud investigation, liability disputes. Video clip (original, unmodified, chain-of-custody sealed). GPS coordinates. Timestamp. Violation/incident classification. Confidence score. Node credential chain (full provenance proof). Per-incident request via secure portal. Individual consent (from claims process) or legal authority required. Per-package flat fee. $50–$75 per package retrieved. Tier 4 — Premium Reduction Certification Annual community coverage density certification for HOA/fleet clients seeking premium reductions. Community node count, coverage density map, average node uptime, evidence pipeline reliability score, evidence quality metrics. Annual report submitted directly to insurer's underwriting team with community manager authorization. $500–$1,000 per community per year.

docs.title: 2. Premium Discount Tier Schedule
docs.text: Participation Level Discount Range Notes Hardware installed, community enforcement opt-in active, no individual data share 3–5% Community safety contribution discount — applies at the household/vehicle level if community is certified Full opt-in: verified driving record shared with this insurer 8–12% Verified safe driver discount — applies to policyholders who opt in via Member App Clean enforcement record 12 months (no violations attributed to vehicle) Additional 5–8% Clean record bonus — policyholders with zero attributed violations receive additional reduction Opted-in driver in high-coverage community (>80% street coverage) Additional 2–3% High-coverage community bonus — network density reward Commercial fleet — CivicMesh certified, >80% fleet enrolled 8–15% fleet premium Commercial lines product — applicable to rental, car share, delivery fleet clients
