docs.title: DC-CS-FED-001 CommunityShield Federation Protocol v1
docs.text:
DC-CS-FED-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. CommunityShield Federation Protocol

Document ID DC-CS-FED-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Technical SR&ED Eligibility Moderate — community-level federation protocol implementation Related Documents DC-CM-FED-001

docs.title: 1. Three Clip Sharing Pathways
docs.text: Pathway Trigger Who Can Initiate Audit Entry Community Notification Manual Submission Community manager reviews incident in NOC dashboard and decides to share clip with municipal authority or law enforcement Community manager only — no automated submission Immediate — manager DID signature on submission Summary in monthly NOC report AI-Triggered Consent-Gated Share Detection event exceeds threshold AND community manager has pre-configured automatic sharing for this event type AND this municipal partner Automatic (pre-authorized by manager) Immediate on submission Real-time NOC dashboard alert Formal Investigation Request Law enforcement submits formal request through Officer Portal with legal authority documentation Law enforcement only Immediate — full detail logged Community notification within 24 hours (may delay up to 30 days with judicial authorization)

docs.title: 2. Law Enforcement Request Handling Procedure
docs.text:
• Request received in CivicMesh Officer Portal: case number, legal authority type, clip sought (time window, node ID or GPS area), submitting officer DID
  • Platform verifies: officer DID is active, officer holds valid OfficerAuthorizationCredential from a municipal partner with a federation agreement
  • Audit log entry created immediately: timestamp, officer DID, legal authority type, data scope, community IDs affected
  • MSSP legal review triggered for: REASONABLE_GROUNDS requests, requests covering residential doorbell footage, requests for footage older than 30 days
  • Approved request: clip package assembled with full chain of custody, delivered to officer's evidence workspace
  • Community notification dispatched within 24 hours: 'A [legal authority type] data request was received and [approved/denied] for [event type] footage from [date range]. Reference: [case number (redacted for active investigations).]'

docs.title: 3. What CommunityShield Federation Does NOT Allow
docs.text:
• Bulk historical footage requests without case reference — every request must be tied to a specific investigation
  • Requests from agencies without a valid Municipal Data Federation Agreement (LEGAL-CM-011)
  • Immigration enforcement agencies (CBSA, IRCC) — explicitly prohibited without court order
  • Civil litigation parties — law enforcement only (criminal and regulatory matters)
  • Requests from MSSPs themselves — MSSPs are operators, not requestors

docs.title: DC-MP-FED-001 MeshPlate Federation Protocol v1
docs.text:
DC-MP-FED-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. MeshPlate Federation Protocol

Document ID DC-MP-FED-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Technical SR&ED Eligibility Moderate — novel hash-based cross-community query protocol Related Documents DC-MP-ARCH-001

docs.title: 1. Standard LPR Federation (Community → Municipal)
docs.text: What Federates Automatically Plates of vehicles confirmed as violating a city bylaw (snow route, accessible parking) — confirmed citation evidence packages only. These are treated identically to TrafficMesh enforcement evidence. What Does NOT Federate Automatically Trusted vehicle registry (resident plates — never shared), visitor log raw data (entry/exit records for private communities), or any plate read not associated with a confirmed violation. Municipal LPR Integration If a municipal partner operates their own ALPR network (e.g. Ottawa Police cruiser ALPR), MeshPlate can federate anonymised violation data (hash + timestamp + GPS) to supplement the municipal network. No plaintext plates in automatic federation.

docs.title: 2. Investigation-Specific Cross-Community Query
docs.text:
• Requesting officer submits: specific plate (plaintext), case number, legal authority (WARRANT required for historical cross-community queries), time window, justification
  • Platform hashes the submitted plate using each relevant community's salt
  • Hash query executed against each community's MeshPlate event records — returns matching event records (timestamp, GPS, clip hash) without exposing the community's plate database
  • Match results assembled into evidence package with chain of custody: which community, which node, which timestamp, confidence score
  • Request logged to public audit trail: case number (visible), plate (not visible), communities queried (aggregate count only), results found (count only)
  • Each queried community manager notified: '[Count] plate query executed against your community's MeshPlate records. Reference: [case number]. [Match found / No match] — specific plate not disclosed per privacy protocol.'
  • Post-case purge: all plaintext plate data derived from the query purged from investigative access within 30 days of case closure

docs.title: 3. No-Data-Broker Covenant
docs.text: The Covenant MeshPlate data — whether hashed or plaintext — will never be sold to, shared with, or accessed by any commercial data broker, insurance data aggregator (other than direct insurer relationships under LEGAL-CM-014), immigration enforcement agency, or political campaign. Technical Enforcement The covenant is enforced at the API level: MeshPlate query API endpoints are accessible only to: (a) municipal law enforcement with active federation agreements, (b) community managers for their own community's data, (c) CCSC members for their own vehicle's data. No bulk export API exists. Open Source Guarantee The no-data-broker covenant is written into the AGPL v3 Community Edition licence terms. Any deployment of MeshPlate must comply with the covenant or they are in breach of the licence.

docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. docs.title: DC-STRAT-001 D-Central Master Strategy v1
docs.text:
DC-STRAT-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP Master Strategy Document Vision · Mission · Ecosystem · Roadmap · Investment Thesis

Document ID DC-STRAT-001 Version 1.0 — Initial Release Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential Ecosystem D-Central Group — Full Ecosystem Related Documents DC-STRAT-002

| Executive Summary D-Central Group is a cooperative technology ecosystem building privacy-preserving, community-owned digital infrastructure across five strategic verticals: civic safety enforcement (CivicMesh), distributed connectivity (MeshTelecom), cooperative housing and property management (D-Central Housing), cooperative financial services (D-Central Credit Union), and open-source security infrastructure (OpenSecure). The Group's flagship product, CivicMesh, is a federated distributed enforcement platform that replaces private surveillance infrastructure with community-owned, auditable, privacy-preserving technology — generating revenue for communities while reducing government costs. All entities are structured as cooperatives or cooperative-linked corporations, anchored by a decentralized identity and credentialing system built on W3C DIDs and Verifiable Credentials recorded on the Polygon blockchain. The Group's development is designed to be 70–85% funded by non-dilutive Canadian government programmes (SR&ED, IRAP, ISC, Mitacs) before any investor capital is required. | |---|

docs.title: 1.1 Vision
docs.text: A world where the digital infrastructure communities depend on for safety, connectivity, housing, and finance is owned, governed, and operated by those communities — not extracted from them by private corporations.

docs.title: 1.2 Mission
docs.text: D-Central Group builds open-source, privacy-preserving, cooperative technology infrastructure that returns control, revenue, and governance to the communities it serves. Every platform we build follows the same principle: participants are members, not users; stakeholders, not subjects; beneficiaries, not products.

docs.title: 1.3 The Founding Problem
docs.text:
Every major technology category that communities depend on has been captured by private corporations whose incentives are structurally misaligned with community welfare:

Category Private Incumbent Problem D-Central Alternative Community Safety Ring/Flock: community data owned by private corporations, shared with law enforcement without consent, monetized without resident benefit CivicMesh: community-owned, privacy-preserving, federated, revenue returned to participants Navigation & Road Intelligence Waze/Google: surveillance capitalism model, no compensation to contributors, data sold to advertisers MeshNav: cooperative data model, contributors compensated, road intelligence community-owned Connectivity Rogers/Bell: extractive pricing, rural and low-income communities underserved MeshTelecom: cooperative ISP, D-Central DePIN mesh, community-owned infrastructure Housing Tech Yardi/RealPage: landlord-side tools that extract from tenants, algorithmic rent coordination DHC: tenant and member cooperative tools, community benefit embedded in architecture Financial Services Big 5 banks: fees extracted from low-income and newcomer communities DECU: education-linked credit union, D-Credit cooperative currency, community financial infrastructure

docs.title: 2.1 The Five Verticals
docs.text:
D-Central Group operates across five strategic verticals, each implemented as a cooperative entity or cooperative-linked corporation, each generating independent revenue while feeding the shared platform ecosystem.

Vertical Entity Flagship Product Revenue Model Status Civic Safety & Enforcement CivicMesh Inc. / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. / CommunityShield Inc. CivicMesh Platform (TrafficMesh, CommunityShield, MeshPlate, MeshEar, MeshNav) MSSP platform fees, citation revenue share, insurance data API, training certification Active — Building Distributed Connectivity MeshTelecom Cooperative / D-Central ISP DePIN mesh network, cooperative ISP, community broadband Monthly ISP subscription, node operator rewards, D-Credit integration Planning Cooperative Housing & Property D-Central Housing Cooperative (DHC) Cooperative property management platform, community land trust tools, SHI integration Management fees, cooperative membership, government housing grants Planning Cooperative Financial Services D-Central Education Credit Union (DECU) D-Credit cooperative currency, education-linked lending, newcomer financial services Interest income, transaction fees, D-Credit velocity Regulatory path Open Source Security Infrastructure OpenSecure Platform / D-Central Shield / D-Central Guard OS-PATROL, OS-GUARDIAN, CivicMesh security layer, MSSP/SOC/NOC services MSSP contracts, SOC-as-a-service, government security contracts Active — Building

docs.title: 2.2 The Cooperative Structure
docs.text:
Every participant-facing entity in the D-Central ecosystem is structured as, or directly linked to, a cooperative. This is not incidental — it is the structural mechanism by which the Group's mission is embedded into every transaction, every governance decision, and every revenue distribution.

Cooperative Principle Implementation in D-Central Open and Voluntary Membership Any resident, technician, MSSP operator, or community organization can join the relevant cooperative. Membership is gated by credential, not by capital. Democratic Member Control One member, one vote on cooperative governance. CCSC members vote on community safety policies. MSSP Cooperative members vote on platform standards. Training Cooperative members co-develop curriculum. Member Economic Participation Patronage returns flow to members proportional to their contribution. Citation revenue flows to node owners. Platform fee revenue flows to MSSP cooperative members. Training revenue flows to accredited providers. Autonomy and Independence Cooperatives are autonomous legal entities. D-Central Group provides the platform and technical infrastructure but does not control cooperative governance. Education, Training, Information D-Central Training Institute is itself a cooperative. Knowledge and certification are treated as community assets, not proprietary advantages. Cooperation Among Cooperatives All D-Central cooperatives are designed to interoperate. D-Credit is the shared economic layer. DID/VC credentials are portable across all entities. Data governance standards are shared. Concern for Community Every platform decision is evaluated against community benefit. The DeFlock risk — technology becoming surveillance infrastructure — is addressed structurally through open source, auditability, and community ownership.

docs.title: 3. D-Credit — The Cooperative Economic Layer
docs.text:
D-Credit is the cooperative currency that unifies revenue flows across all D-Central entities. It is not a speculative cryptocurrency — it is a community accounting unit, redeemable within the D-Central ecosystem, convertible to CAD at a fixed rate, and earned by contributing to the network.

D-Credit Function Description Patronage Distribution Citation revenue shares, MSSP cooperative patronage, training cooperative revenue, and government stipends can all be paid in D-Credit. Ecosystem Spending D-Credit is accepted for CivicMesh training certification fees, cooperative membership fees, MSSP platform fees (discounted rate), and D-Central Housing cooperative contributions. CAD Conversion Members can convert D-Credit to CAD through DECU at a fixed rate. This creates a cash-out pathway without requiring D-Credit to have speculative market value. Velocity Incentive Spending D-Credit within the ecosystem earns a velocity bonus — members who circulate D-Credit within the cooperative network receive a small additional patronage return, increasing local economic density. Government Alignment D-Credit is designed to be compatible with Canadian community currency regulations and is structured to avoid securities classification. Legal opinion commissioned in LEGAL-CM-014.

docs.title: 4. CivicMesh — Flagship Vertical
docs.text: CivicMesh is D-Central Group's first fully developed vertical and the proof of concept for the cooperative technology infrastructure model. It is described in full in DC-CM-BIZ-001. The summary below situates CivicMesh within the Group strategy.

docs.title: 4.1 Why CivicMesh is First
docs.text: CivicMesh was chosen as the first vertical for five reasons: - Market urgency: Ontario's November 2025 ASE camera ban created an immediate, documented $38.7M annual revenue gap for Ottawa alone. Municipalities need an alternative now. - Non-dilutive funding alignment: Edge AI, privacy-preserving data pipelines, and novel evidence chain-of-custody systems are all SR&ED and IRAP eligible. Up to 88% of R&D salaries can be government-subsidized during development. - Regulatory path clarity: Vehicle-mounted enforcement on city fleet vehicles operates under existing HTA authority. No new legislation is required for the OC Transpo pilot. A legal opinion confirming this is the first Track A deliverable. - Private sector flywheel: Every new node improves coverage, improving enforcement accuracy, improving insurance data quality, attracting more insurance subscribers, funding more civilian opt-in incentives, driving more node deployment. - Competitive gap: No existing player combines mobile nodes, civilian opt-in with revenue share, open source, community ownership, and insurance API. The gap is real and confirmed by competitive analysis (DC-CM-STRAT-001).

docs.title: 4.2 The Five CivicMesh Modules
docs.text: Module Replaces Community Benefit SR&ED Eligibility TrafficMesh Fixed ASE cameras, Flock Safety Citation revenue share, digital twin, transit improvement Primary — edge AI, ALPR, digital twin R&D CommunityShield Ring Neighbors Compensation for node contribution, data ownership, opt-out rights Strong — privacy-preserving AI, on-device blurring MeshPlate Flock/Motorola Vigilant (national DB) No private database, community owns LPR data, legal query only Novel — hash-before-store privacy architecture MeshEar ShotSpotter/SoundThinking Community triage before dispatch, private security integration, no audio retention Primary — on-device acoustic AI model development MeshNav Waze/Google Maps Better 24/7 data from city fleet, enforcement zone transparency, digital twin layer Strong — navigation data fusion pipeline

docs.title: 5. Government Funding Strategy
docs.text:
D-Central Group is designed from the ground up to maximize non-dilutive Canadian government funding. The goal is to cover 70–85% of development costs through government programmes before any equity investor capital is required. This changes the investor conversation from funding development risk to funding scaling a validated product.

Programme What It Funds Amount Timing SR&ED R&D salaries and materials — edge AI, ALPR, digital twin, DID/VC pipeline Up to 65% salary refund (CCPC refundable) Annual, after fiscal year close NRC IRAP R&D labour (80%) and contractor costs (50%) — dual-use/public safety stream Up to 80% of eligible salaries, monthly Monthly during approved project Mitacs Accelerate Graduate student researchers — computer vision, law, distributed systems $7,500 partner → $15,000 research labour per unit Per 4–6 month internship Mitacs Entrepreneur Founder stipend for student entrepreneur at approved incubator Up to $7,500 per internship unit Per internship, requires incubator ISC Phase 1 PoC development grant — Public Safety Canada / Transport Canada challenge Up to $150,000 6-month sprint ISC Phase 2 Full platform build — RCMP or smart city challenge response Up to $1,000,000 2-year build FedDev Ontario Regional technology growth for Ottawa-based digital/AI company Variable, project-based Project application Smart Cities Challenge CivicMesh as community-owned smart city infrastructure Variable Annual challenge cycle

The IRAP + SR&ED combination is the most powerful: IRAP reimburses up to 80% of eligible salaries monthly during the project. SR&ED then refunds a portion of the remaining 20% annually. Stacked correctly, the founder can draw a market-rate salary while the government covers the majority of that cost. Action priority: SR&ED ledger setup and IRAP application are Day-1 blockers. IRAP does not fund work already completed. Both programmes require contemporaneous documentation — GitHub commit logs, Linear/Jira tasks, and technical decision logs created on the day R&D occurs.

docs.title: 6. Ten-Year Roadmap
docs.text: Phase Timeline Milestones Revenue Target Phase 1 — Foundation Months 1–6 Incorporate. SR&ED/IRAP active. Training Institute launched. First 5 CommunityShield HOA deployments. Software-only MVP built. Legal opinions received. Training certification fees. HOA NOC revenue. Phase 2 — OC Transpo Pilot Months 6–12 First TrafficMesh node on personal vehicle. OC Transpo pilot proposal submitted and accepted. Officer review portal demo-ready. First Mitacs research partner engaged. MSSP pilot revenue. IRAP reimbursements flowing. Phase 3 — City Fleet Expansion Year 1–2 OC Transpo pilot live. City of Ottawa fleet deployment begins. Contractor node kit programme. First external MSSP certified. ISC Phase 1 award. Enforcement citation revenue. Expanded MSSP fees. Phase 4 — Civilian Opt-In Year 2–3 Civilian opt-in programme launch. Insurance API partnerships. MeshNav public beta. CCSC cooperative open for membership. First patronage distributions. Insurance API subscriptions. Civilian node patronage. Phase 5 — Multi-City Expansion Year 3–5 Hamilton, Mississauga, Toronto, Gatineau pilots. Multiple MSSP partners nationally. Series A investment (scaling, not development). MeshTelecom cooperative launched. Multi-city MSSP fees. National data marketplace. Phase 6 — Platform Maturity Year 5–10 Full five-module CivicMesh live. DECU credit union operational. DHC cooperative housing live. D-Credit accepted across all verticals. International expansion (US, UK, Australia). Full cooperative economy generating self-sustaining revenue.

docs.title: 7.1 The Market Opportunity
docs.text: The addressable market for CivicMesh alone spans municipal enforcement infrastructure, community safety technology, navigation data, and insurance telematics — a combined global market exceeding $50 billion annually. Comparable companies: Flock Safety ($7.5B valuation), Nexar ($500M+), ShotSpotter ($300M acquisition). None of them have the community-owned, privacy-preserving, open source positioning that eliminates the political and regulatory risk those companies face.

docs.title: 7.2 The Cooperative Moat
docs.text: The cooperative structure creates switching costs that no conventional competitor can replicate. When participants are members — when their node earns them patronage, when their cooperative membership gives them governance rights, when their D-Credit circulates within the local economy — leaving the network means losing a financial relationship, not just switching software. This is a structurally different retention mechanism than a subscription or a hardware lock-in.

docs.title: 7.3 The Government Validation Premium
docs.text: When D-Central walks into an investor meeting, it will carry: IRAP approval (NRC has validated the technical merit), SR&ED claims (CRA has validated the R&D expenditure), an ISC award (a federal department has validated the commercial potential), a legal opinion (confirming HTA compliance for the OC Transpo pilot), and a live municipal pilot with revenue data. That is not a startup asking an investor to fund an idea. That is a company asking an investor to fund the scaling of a government-validated product. The investor risk profile is categorically different — and the valuation should reflect that.

docs.title: 7.4 The Network Effect Compounding
docs.text: Every new node improves coverage. Better coverage improves enforcement accuracy. Better enforcement accuracy improves insurance data quality. Better insurance data attracts more insurer subscriptions. More insurer subscription revenue funds more civilian opt-in incentives. More civilian opt-in adds more nodes. The flywheel accelerates with scale, and it cannot be easily replicated by a new entrant once it is running — because the data asset, the coverage density, and the community relationships all compound simultaneously.

docs.title: 8. Document Control
docs.text:

This document is confidential and intended for internal strategic planning, investor briefings, and board review. Do not distribute without authorization from Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste.

DC-STRAT-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP Ecosystem Map Entity Relationships · Revenue Flows · Cooperative Linkages · Data Federation

Document ID DC-STRAT-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential Document Type Visual specification — source document for designed one-pager Related Documents DC-STRAT-001

The Ecosystem Map is not a document; it is a designed visual asset (one-pager / A3 poster / pitch slide) produced from the specification contained here. This document defines every entity, every relationship arrow, every revenue flow, and every cooperative linkage that must appear in the final designed asset. | |---|

CCPC — IP Holding Centre anchor node Layer 0 — Core D-Central Work Cooperative (DWC) Worker Cooperative Labour and technical workforce Layer 1 — People D-Central Education Credit Union (DECU) Credit Union (regulatory path) Financial services, D-Credit issuance Layer 1 — Finance D-Central Manufacturing Coop (DCMC) Manufacturing Cooperative Open hardware node production Layer 1 — Hardware

CCPC — Operating Traffic enforcement, OC Transpo pilot, city fleet Citation share + MSSP fees CommunityShield Inc. docs.title: 2.3 Connected Ecosystem Participants
docs.text: Participant Type Relationship Data Flow Revenue Flow Municipalities (Ottawa, etc.) Data federation agreement Citation data → city; road condition → city City pays MSSP fee; city receives citation revenue share OC Transpo Pilot client — bus fleet nodes Evidence packages → officer portal OC Transpo pays node kit + MSSP fee; receives citation revenue Insurance Companies (Intact, Desjardins, Aviva) Data subscriber — API agreement Anonymised aggregate + consent-gated individual records → insurer Insurer pays API subscription; insurer reduces premiums for members HOAs / Condo Corporations Community network participant CommunityShield footage → community NOC HOA pays platform fee; HOA receives fine revenue; residents get patronage University Research Partners (Carleton, uOttawa, Algonquin) Mitacs Accelerate partnership Research outputs → platform improvement Mitacs funds 75% of research labour Certified MSSP Partners (future) Franchise operators Node data → NOC → officer portal MSSP pays platform fee; earns client revenue; receives cooperative patronage

docs.text: Flow Description Node → CCSC → Member Citation revenue share flows from the enforcement pipeline through CCSC to member node owners as patronage (D-Credit or CAD) City → TrafficMesh → D-Central Shield Municipalities pay MSSP fees to D-Central Shield (first MSSP operator); Shield pays platform fee to CivicMesh Inc. Insurer → CivicMesh Inc. Insurance companies pay API subscription fees to CivicMesh Inc. for data products; portion flows to CCSC as additional member revenue HOA → CommunityShield → CCSC HOAs pay NOC platform fees; fine revenue flows through CommunityShield to CCSC member distribution Training fees → CTC → Training providers Certification fees flow through CTC (60% to delivering provider, 30% to CTC common fund, 10% to curriculum development) Government → CivicMesh entities IRAP monthly reimbursements (80% of eligible salaries); SR&ED annual refunds (up to 65%); ISC Phase 1/2 grants; Mitacs contributions

docs.text: Flow Description Node → Ingestion API Encrypted evidence packages (clip + GPS + timestamp + violation type + confidence) uploaded via LTE from edge compute module Ingestion API → Officer Portal Validated evidence packages placed in human review queue; officer reviews and confirms or dismisses before any enforcement action Officer Portal → Citation → City Revenue Confirmed citations flow to municipal payment system; revenue distributed per revenue share agreement Community Layer → Municipal Layer Upward federation only on explicit consent, with legal authority verification, audit log entry, and community notification CivicMesh → Insurance API Anonymised aggregate corridor data and (consent-gated) individual driving records flow to insurance subscribers Digital Twin → City Departments Road condition data, asset condition registry, pothole locations, sign/signal status flow to Public Works, Planning, Emergency Management

docs.title: 4. Visual Design Specification
docs.text:
The following specification is for the graphic designer who will produce the final Ecosystem Map asset. This document is the content source; the designer produces the visual.

Design Parameter Specification Format A3 landscape (420mm × 297mm) for print; 2480 × 3508px at 300dpi. Also export as 1920 × 1080px 16:9 slide version. Layer 1: five verticals. Layer 2: cooperative entities. Layer 3: external participants (municipalities, insurers, HOAs, universities). Revenue flows as solid arrows. Data flows as dashed arrows. Legend Bottom-right corner: solid arrow = revenue flow, dashed arrow = data flow, green badge = cooperative entity, D-Credit symbol = cooperative currency flow Produced By [Graphic designer to be engaged — Canva Pro, Adobe Illustrator, or Figma acceptable]

docs.title: DC-STRAT-003 Founder Story Vision Narrative v1
docs.text:
DC-STRAT-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP Founder Story & Vision Narrative For Media · Investor Introductions · Community Engagement · Grant Applications

Document ID DC-STRAT-003 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Internal / Selectively Public Document Type Narrative — multiple format versions specified within Audiences Media, investors, government, community, academic partners Related Documents DC-STRAT-001

docs.title: 1. The Full Founder Narrative (Long Form — 800 words)
docs.text: For grant applications, feature articles, and long-form investor materials. I grew up watching technology be built for communities without being built by them. The surveillance cameras that appeared in my neighbourhood were not installed because the community asked for them. The data they generated did not belong to the community. The revenue from the fines they enabled did not return to the community. And when residents objected — to the optics, to the privacy implications, to the feeling of being watched rather than protected — there was no mechanism to change any of it. The infrastructure was owned by a company in another city, the data flowed to a police department, and the community had no voice and no stake. I started D-Central Group because I believed there was a different way to build this — and because the tools to build it differently had finally arrived. The W3C Decentralized Identifier specification means a technician's credentials do not have to live in a private company's database. The Verifiable Credential standard means a community organization can issue a membership card that any institution can verify without calling a central authority. The open hardware movement means a camera node can be built from published designs, manufactured by any certified shop, and owned by the community it serves. The cooperative legal framework means the revenue from that camera does not have to flow to a shareholder — it can flow back to the resident whose doorbell is part of the network. CivicMesh is the first full expression of this approach. It is a distributed traffic and community safety enforcement network — but it is built on the principle that the community owns the infrastructure, controls the data, and receives the revenue. When a city bus equipped with a CivicMesh node captures a bus lane violation, the citation revenue is shared between the city and the cooperative. When a resident's dashcam contributes to a validated enforcement action, they receive a patronage payment. When a law enforcement agency needs access to footage for an investigation, they submit a formal request that is logged, audited, and visible to the community — not silently routed through a private company's back end. The Province of Ontario banned automated speed enforcement cameras in November 2025. The political reason was that residents saw them as a government cash grab with no community benefit. I think that analysis is correct — and I think it points directly to the solution. The reason fixed cameras failed politically is not that enforcement is wrong. It is that the community had no stake in the outcome. TrafficMesh gives them a stake. The community co-owns the network. The community receives the revenue. The community governs the data. When a politician proposes banning TrafficMesh, they are proposing to take money out of residents' pockets. That is a completely different political dynamic. I am a student at Algonquin College and I am building this in Ottawa — the city that has the most to gain from an alternative to the enforcement infrastructure that was just banned. I am structuring every entity as a cooperative because I believe that cooperative structure is not just ethically correct; it is strategically superior. The switching costs are higher. The community advocacy is built in. The political alignment is structural. And the government funding available to a student founder building privacy-preserving, open-source, community-owned safety infrastructure in Ottawa is, as it turns out, very substantial. D-Central Group is not a startup that will be acquired by the company it is disrupting. It is a cooperative ecosystem that will be difficult to acquire because its members own it, and impossible to replicate because the data, the relationships, and the trust are all embedded in the community infrastructure it has built. That is the company I am building. And Ottawa is where I am starting.

docs.title: 2.1 Elevator Pitch (60 seconds / ~150 words)
docs.text: Ontario just banned speed cameras, and Ottawa lost $38 million a year in enforcement revenue. I'm building the replacement — but I'm building it differently. CivicMesh is a distributed traffic enforcement network that runs on city buses, garbage trucks, and snowplows. Every violation it captures, the community gets a cut. The data stays with the city, not a private company. And every piece of it is open source and verifiable on a public blockchain. I'm a student at Algonquin, I'm incorporating a cooperative this month, and I'm applying for IRAP funding next week. The government is going to pay for most of this build — and when I walk into an investor meeting in 18 months, I'll have a live Ottawa pilot and $150,000 in federal grants already in hand.

docs.title: 2.2 Investor Introduction (2 minutes / ~300 words)
docs.text:
For warm introductions, first investor meetings, and pitch competition openers. Emphasizes market size, competitive gap, and government de-risking.

| Flock Safety is worth $7.5 billion. They put license plate readers on telephone poles across 5,000 American cities and sell the data to police departments — without the community's consent and without returning any revenue to the communities that host their hardware. DeFlock.me now has 30 organized community groups fighting to remove Flock cameras. Ring has the same problem. ShotSpotter was cancelled by Chicago for the same reason. I'm building the alternative they created the demand for. CivicMesh is a distributed community safety network — traffic enforcement, community cameras, acoustic detection, license plate recognition, and navigation data — but built on a cooperative model where the community owns the infrastructure, controls the data, and receives the revenue. Every node is open source hardware. Every credential is verifiable on a public blockchain. Every data federation request is logged and visible to the community. The market is real and urgent. Ottawa just lost $38 million a year when Ontario banned speed cameras. OC Transpo needs a bus lane enforcement solution. The City has a $10 million red light camera programme with no mobile equivalent. I have a legal opinion confirming that vehicle-mounted enforcement operates under existing HTA authority — no new legislation required. I have an IRAP application filed, SR&ED documentation running from day one, and an ISC Phase 1 application targeting a Public Safety Canada challenge. The government is covering 70–85% of my development cost. I'm looking for capital to cover the remaining 15% and to fund the scaling conversation once the Ottawa pilot is live. | |---|

docs.title: 2.3 Media / Journalist Version (one paragraph)
docs.text: An Algonquin College student is building an open-source, community-owned alternative to the private surveillance networks that have been banned or protested across North America. CivicMesh uses city buses, snowplows, and garbage trucks as mobile sensor nodes — capturing traffic violations and road condition data — but unlike Ring or Flock Safety, the community owns the hardware, controls the data, and receives a share of enforcement revenue. The platform is built on W3C Decentralized Identifiers and Verifiable Credentials, giving communities cryptographic proof of who installed each camera, who can access the footage, and when that access occurred.

docs.title: 2.4 Government / Grant Version (formal)
docs.text: D-Central Group is an Ottawa-based cooperative technology company developing privacy-preserving, community-owned digital safety infrastructure. The Group's flagship product, CivicMesh, is a federated distributed enforcement platform that addresses the enforcement revenue gap created by Ontario's November 2025 automated speed enforcement ban, while providing municipalities with a constitutionally compliant, PIPEDA-compliant, open-source alternative to proprietary surveillance infrastructure. The platform generates technical uncertainty in five areas qualifying for SR&ED: edge AI model development for violation detection, ALPR optimization for Canadian weather conditions, privacy-preserving federated data architecture, novel cryptographic chain-of-custody pipeline, and on-device acoustic event classification. The company is led by a student founder at Algonquin College, satisfying the Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur programme eligibility criteria, and is incorporated as a CCPC to access all available federal and provincial innovation funding programmes.

docs.title: 3. Key Messages by Audience
docs.text: Audience Lead Message Supporting Points What NOT to Say Investors Government is funding 70–85% of this build before you write a cheque IRAP, SR&ED, ISC, Mitacs stacked. Flock = $7.5B comp. Ottawa pilot = proof of concept. Cooperative structure = moat. Don't lead with cooperative ideology — lead with returns and de-risking Municipality We fill the revenue hole the ASE ban left, without fixed infrastructure Mobile = harder to avoid. Human review = legally defensible. Open source = no vendor lock-in. Community revenue share = political support. Don't mention blockchain in a first municipal meeting — lead with revenue and legal compliance Community / Residents You own it, you govern it, you get paid Revenue share, cooperative membership, opt-in controls, public camera map, annual transparency report. Don't downplay the enforcement aspect — residents generally support traffic safety enforcement when they benefit from it Media The privacy-preserving anti-Flock — built by a student in Ottawa Community ownership, open source auditability, no national database, mandatory human review, residents compensated. Don't use 'surveillance' — use 'community safety infrastructure' Academic Partners Genuine open research questions with publication potential Edge AI, privacy-preserving LPR, acoustic classification, evidence law, cooperative governance. Don't oversell commercialization — academics need research freedom

docs.title: DC-STRAT-004 Annual Report Template v1

DC-STRAT-004 D-CENTRAL GROUP Annual Report Template Public Transparency · Cooperative Distributions · Community Impact · Financial Summary

docs.title: DC-STRAT-005 Competitive Moat Analysis v1
docs.text:
DC-STRAT-005 D-CENTRAL GROUP Competitive Moat Analysis Why the Full-Stack Cooperative + Open Source + DID/VC + D-Credit Model Cannot Be Replicated

Document ID DC-STRAT-005 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential Primary Audience Sophisticated investors, strategic partners, board Related Documents DC-STRAT-001

| Executive Summary This document analyzes the structural competitive advantages of D-Central Group's cooperative + open source + DID/VC + D-Credit architecture. The thesis is that these four elements, combined, create a moat that a traditional corporation cannot replicate — because replicating it requires becoming a cooperative, which eliminates the extractive business model that makes the competitor profitable in the first place. The document also analyzes the vulnerabilities of each major incumbent and why their structural position prevents them from effectively responding to D-Central's model. | |---|

docs.title: 1.1 Cooperative Structure — The Governance Moat
docs.text:
A cooperative structure creates switching costs that subscription software, hardware lock-in, and network effects cannot match. When a participant is a member — when their node earns them patronage, when their cooperative membership gives them governance rights, when their D-Credit circulates within the local economy — leaving the network means losing a financial and governance relationship, not just changing software.

Moat Dimension Why Competitors Cannot Replicate Patronage economics Flock Safety, Ring, and ShotSpotter are Delaware C-Corps whose fiduciary duty is to shareholders. Returning citation revenue to community members would reduce shareholder returns. They cannot structurally compete on this dimension without abandoning their business model. Governance rights A community that has governance rights over their data infrastructure has a fundamentally different relationship with the platform than a customer who can be repriced, recontracted, or sold to an acquirer. No private competitor can offer governance rights without diluting investor control. Political immunity When Ford's government banned speed cameras, every fixed camera operator lost their market overnight. CivicMesh communities, by contrast, have a financial stake in the platform continuing. Their political advocacy is built into the economic structure. This is a regulatory moat that no private competitor can replicate. Community advocacy DeFlock.me has 30+ organized groups fighting to remove Flock cameras. No equivalent movement will target CivicMesh because the community that would organize against it is the community that owns it and receives its revenue.

docs.title: 1.2 Open Source — The Trust and Adoption Moat
docs.text:
Open source creates a moat in govtech that is the inverse of the typical technology moat. In consumer software, open source reduces lock-in. In municipal technology, where procurement requires auditability and where political risk is existential, open source is the lock-in mechanism — because municipalities that have adopted and integrated open source infrastructure are far less likely to replace it with proprietary alternatives.

Moat Dimension Why Competitors Cannot Replicate Procurement advantage Many municipalities now have open source preference policies. Flock and Ring are explicitly excluded from consideration in some jurisdictions because they are proprietary and unauditable. D-Central passes every open source procurement requirement by design. Community model improvement The more cities deploy the open source platform, the more violation detection models improve, the more edge cases are documented, and the more the codebase matures. This improvement compounds across every deployment. A proprietary competitor improves only from its own data. Anti-competitive fork protection AGPL licensing means any organization that deploys D-Central's platform as a service must open source their modifications. This prevents a large vendor from forking the codebase and competing with a proprietary version — the mechanism that killed HashiCorp's open source moat when it pivoted to BSL. Academic integration Open source enables Carleton and uOttawa research partnerships that generate publications, credibility, and continuous model improvement. Private competitors cannot offer professors research freedom on proprietary code.

docs.title: 1.3 DID/VC Credentialing — The Trust Infrastructure Moat
docs.text:
The W3C DID and Verifiable Credential architecture creates a portable, interoperable trust layer that becomes more valuable as more entities issue and accept credentials within the ecosystem. This is a classic network effect — but applied to institutional trust rather than user attention.

Moat Dimension Why Competitors Cannot Replicate Court-admissible evidence chain No existing enforcement camera system can produce a cryptographic provenance chain showing that specific evidence was captured by hardware manufactured by a certified factory, installed by a certified technician, operated by a certified MSSP, and untampered since installation — verifiable on a public blockchain without calling the vendor. This is a qualitative leap in evidence defensibility that takes years of credential issuance history to build. Trust registry depth The value of the D-Central trust registry compounds with every credential issued. After three years of operation, the registry contains hundreds of certified technicians, dozens of MSSP operators, and thousands of certified nodes. A new entrant starts with zero — and cannot claim the same evidentiary standing without rebuilding the entire credential chain from scratch. Portable credentials A technician whose credentials are issued as W3C VCs on the Polygon blockchain is not locked into D-Central's platform. They can take their credentials to any W3C-compatible system. This portability, counterintuitively, increases adoption — because participants are not being locked into a proprietary credential that becomes worthless if D-Central fails. Regulatory alignment Canadian courts are beginning to grapple with blockchain-anchored evidence. Building the credential framework now positions D-Central to define the standard that others will have to meet, rather than meeting a standard set by a regulator or competitor.

docs.title: 1.4 D-Credit — The Economic Velocity Moat
docs.text:
D-Credit creates a local economic velocity effect: money earned in the D-Central ecosystem is most efficiently spent within the D-Central ecosystem, which drives further adoption, which drives further D-Credit circulation, which deepens the cooperative economy.

Moat Dimension Why Competitors Cannot Replicate Circular economy A resident who earns D-Credit from citation patronage and spends it on CivicMesh training certification is participating in a circular cooperative economy. No private competitor can create this loop because their revenue extraction model requires money to flow out of the community to shareholders. Fee discount incentive D-Credit holders who spend within the ecosystem receive a discount on platform fees. This creates a permanent pricing advantage for D-Central over competitors — loyal cooperative members always pay less than new customers of competing services. Non-speculative design By anchoring D-Credit to a fixed CAD conversion rate through DECU and explicitly avoiding speculative market listing, D-Credit avoids the volatility and regulatory risk that has undermined other community currencies. This makes it safe for municipalities and insurance companies to accept it as a payment mechanism — which no competitor's loyalty token can claim.

docs.title: 2. Incumbent Vulnerability Analysis
docs.text: Incumbent Core Vulnerability D-Central Exploit Can They Respond? Flock Safety DeFlock backlash, no community benefit, no Canadian privacy law compliance, data broker integration generating lawsuits Community ownership, open source auditability, no national DB, PIPEDA compliance built-in, revenue to communities No — their investor return model requires extracting data value, which is incompatible with community ownership Ring / Amazon Amazon extracts value from community data; no resident compensation; Ring data shared with police without transparency CCSC member compensation, transparent federation logging, community-owned storage, no Amazon dependency No — Amazon's incentive is to monetize data centrally; cooperative redistribution contradicts their core model ShotSpotter Alert fatigue, low arrest rate, racial bias in deployment patterns, private company owns all data Community triage before dispatch, on-device processing only, no audio retention, community-governed deployment decisions Limited — they could add community triage but cannot change data ownership without losing their core data product revenue Rekor/Waycare Government contract model with no civilian participation layer, no revenue share, proprietary data Civilian opt-in with compensation, open source platform, community cooperative as client structure Partial — they could open source but cannot add revenue share without restructuring their entire margin model Waze / Google Surveillance capitalism model, contributor data sold to advertisers, no compensation, Google account required Cooperative data ownership, contributor compensation via D-Credit, no advertising monetization, anonymous participation option No — Google's fundamental business model is advertising supported by data aggregation; removing that removes their revenue

docs.title: 3. The Replication Paradox
docs.text: The deepest moat in the D-Central model is what might be called the replication paradox: in order for a private competitor to replicate D-Central's community ownership, open source governance, and cooperative revenue distribution, they would have to become a cooperative. And becoming a cooperative means returning revenue to members rather than to shareholders — which eliminates the business model that makes the competitor attractive to private investment in the first place. This is not a temporary competitive advantage that can be overcome with enough capital. It is a structural incompatibility between the extractive model of private capital and the redistributive model of cooperative ownership. Flock Safety cannot offer community revenue share without reducing investor returns. Ring cannot offer community data ownership without reducing Amazon's data monetization. ShotSpotter cannot offer on-device-only processing without eliminating their data product. D-Central's cooperative structure is therefore both its mission and its moat. The two are identical.

docs.title: 4. The Network Effect Compounding Model
docs.text: Stage What Compounds Year 1 — Ottawa Node density in Ottawa improves enforcement accuracy. Insurance data quality improves. Training Institute establishes certification standard. First MSSP certified. Cooperative membership open. Year 2 — Ontario Ottawa revenue data used to pitch Hamilton, Mississauga, Gatineau. Each new city adds nodes. More nodes improve AI models. Better models reduce false positive rate. Lower false positive rate improves officer trust. More officer trust drives more citation volume. More citation volume drives more cooperative revenue. Year 3 — Canada National data asset emerges. Insurers with national books need national data. D-Central is the only provider of community-owned, privacy-preserving, auditable road and community safety data at national scale. Data product revenue becomes self-sustaining. Year 5 — Platform D-Central sets the standard for community safety infrastructure in Canada. Provincial and federal procurement policies begin referencing D-Central's open source credential framework as the baseline. New entrants must meet D-Central's standard to compete — a standard D-Central wrote and continues to govern. Year 10 — Ecosystem D-Credit circulates across all five verticals. DECU provides financial services to cooperative members. DHC manages cooperative housing for communities with CivicMesh deployments. MeshTelecom provides connectivity that the node network depends on. Every vertical reinforces every other vertical. The switching cost is no longer 'change software' — it is 'rebuild your community's entire cooperative economy.'

docs.text:
1. Start with MVP priorities:

  5. Officer Portal

  6. For each feature, follow the pattern:

  7. Read 00_OVERVIEW.md
  8. Study test requirements
  9. Implement test suite (unit → integration → E2E)
  10. Run tests, measure coverage

  11. Move to next feature

  12. Track progress:

  13. Update status (📋 → 🔄 → ✓)
  14. Record test coverage %
  15. Note known issues
  16. Update timeline

docs.title: CivicMesh Feature Testing Guide
docs.text: Master guide for testing all CivicMesh platform features as independent modules.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This guide provides a standardized approach to testing each feature in isolation, then integrating them into the complete platform.

Testing Philosophy: - Test each feature module independently first - Verify subfeatures within each module - Use mock data / simulators where hardware isn't available - Create repeatable, automated test suites - Document acceptance criteria for each feature

docs.title: Directory Structure

docs.title: Feature Test Document Template
docs.text: Each feature test document follows this structure:

docs.title: 1. Feature Overview
docs.text:
• What is it?
  • Why is it important?
  • Where does it fit in the platform?

docs.title: 2. Subfeatures
docs.text:
• List all subfeatures
  • Interdependencies
  • Acceptance criteria for each

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• Languages (Python, Node.js, etc.)
  • Libraries/dependencies
  • Version requirements

docs.title: Data/Media
docs.text:
• Sample videos (dashcam footage)
  • Test data (OBD logs, GPS coordinates)
  • Ground truth datasets

docs.title: Hardware (if applicable)
docs.text:
• Physical components needed
  • Simulation alternatives

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• OS requirements
  • Database requirements
  • Network requirements

docs.title: Unit Testing
docs.text:
• Test individual functions
  • Mock external dependencies
  • Target: >80% code coverage

docs.title: Integration Testing
docs.text:
• Test subfeature interactions
  • Simulate realistic data flows
  • Mock upstream/downstream systems

docs.title: End-to-End Testing
docs.text:
• Full feature from start to finish
  • Real data where possible
  • Acceptance criteria verification

docs.title: Performance Testing
docs.text:
• Latency requirements
  • Throughput targets
  • Resource usage (CPU, memory)

docs.title: Test Data
docs.text:
• Sample inputs
  • Expected outputs
  • Edge cases

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria Checklist
docs.text:
• Clear pass/fail criteria
  • Measurable thresholds

docs.title: Test Code
docs.text:
• Python test class (pytest)
  • Setup/teardown
  • Individual test methods

docs.title: Week 1: Unit Tests
docs.text:
• Create test data generators
  • Write unit tests for each module
  • Achieve >80% code coverage
  • Run daily

docs.title: Week 2: Integration Tests
docs.text:
• Test feature interactions
  • Mock external systems
  • Verify data flows
  • Daily test runs

docs.title: Week 3: End-to-End Tests
docs.text:
• Full feature workflows
  • Use realistic test data
  • Verify acceptance criteria
  • Manual + automated testing

docs.title: Week 4: Performance Tests
docs.text:
• Measure latency
  • Verify throughput
  • Optimize bottlenecks
  • Load test (if applicable)

docs.title: Week 5: Module Integration
docs.text:
• Combine 2–3 features
  • Test interactions
  • Identify missing pieces
  • Refine architecture

docs.title: Video Assets
docs.text:
Store in features/test-data/videos/: - dashcam_normal_traffic.mp4 (1 min) - dashcam_speeding.mp4 (30 sec) - dashcam_lane_violation.mp4 (30 sec) - dashcam_night_vision.mp4 (30 sec) - dashcam_rain_weather.mp4 (30 sec)

Where to get: - YouTube dashcam clips (creative commons) - Cityscapes dataset (academic) - Your own phone camera

docs.title: OBD Test Data
docs.text: Store in features/test-data/obd/: - speeding_scenario.json — Vehicle goes 65 in 50 zone - normal_traffic.json — Steady speed, no violations - multiple_events.json — Several violations in sequence

docs.title: Expected Results
docs.text: Store in features/test-data/results/: - speeding_expected.json — Ground truth detections - lane_violation_expected.json — Expected violations - Confidence scores, timestamps, etc.

docs.title: All Features
docs.text: bash cd features pytest -v --cov=. --cov-report=html

docs.title: Single Feature
docs.text: bash cd features/violation-detection pytest 01_SPEEDING.md -v

docs.title: With Coverage Report
docs.text: bash pytest --cov=features --cov-report=term-missing

docs.title: Continuous Testing
docs.text: ```bash

docs.title: Watch for file changes, re-run tests
docs.text: pytest-watch ```

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria (Global)
docs.text:
Every feature must pass these tests before moving to next phase:

  • All unit tests pass (100% of test suite)
  • Code coverage ≥80%
  • Integration tests pass (all subfeatures)
  • No known bugs (critical/high severity)
  • Performance targets met
  • API latency <200ms
  • Inference <200ms (edge compute)
  • Upload success >99%
  • Security requirements met
  • Encryption in transit (TLS 1.3)
  • Signed audit logs
  • No hardcoded secrets
  • Documentation complete
  • Feature overview
  • API documentation
  • User guide (if applicable)
  • Code review approved
  • Peer review completed
  • Architecture sound
  • No technical debt introduced

docs.title: Common Issues & Solutions
docs.text: Issue Cause Solution ALPR confidence too low Poor image quality Use better dashcam footage, adjust model threshold Lane detection misses violations Model trained on sunny days Add rainy/night test videos Dedup doesn't merge duplicates Geofence too small Increase radius to 100m, adjust time window False positives high Model overfitting Retrain with more diverse data Upload failures Mock API down Check mock server logs, restart service

docs.title: Generate fake dashcam footage for testing

import cv2 import numpy as np

def generate_test_video(filename, duration_sec=30): """Generate synthetic traffic video.""" fourcc = cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'mp4v') out = cv2.VideoWriter(filename, fourcc, 30.0, (1920, 1440))

for frame_num in range(duration_sec * 30):
    # Create blank frame
    frame = np.ones((1440, 1920, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 100

    # Draw road elements
    # - Lane markings (white lines)
    # - Road surface
    # - Sky
    # - Vehicles
    # - Signs

    out.write(frame)

out.release()

```

Create a test report template:

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Review this guide (30 min)
  2. Create feature test docs (one per feature, 4–6 hours total)
  3. Set up test infrastructure (mock APIs, test data generators, 2 hours)
  4. Write unit tests (2 weeks)
  5. Write integration tests (1 week)
  6. Run full test suite (ongoing)

Start with: Violation detection (speeding) → Enforcement workflow → Data pipeline

These three features cover the MVP and are independent enough to test in parallel.

docs.title: Data Pipeline Module - Overview
docs.text: End-to-end incident data flow from edge node to cloud ingestion.

docs.title: Feature Summary
docs.text:
The data pipeline is the glue connecting edge detection to cloud storage and officer review. It handles: - Edge capture & compression (on node) - Encryption & signing (security) - Buffering & retry logic (reliability) - Cloud upload (connectivity) - Ingestion & deduplication (backend) - Digital twin fusion (parallel processing)

Key Characteristics: - End-to-end encrypted - Survives 24-hour offline - Automatic retry with backoff - Deduplication across multiple nodes - <5 sec ingestion latency - 99%+ upload success rate

docs.title: Subfeatures
docs.text: Subfeature Description Priority Phase Frame Capture Grab dashcam frames at 30 fps CRITICAL MVP Metadata Collection Assemble timestamp, GPS, OBD, violation data CRITICAL MVP Clip Extraction Extract 5-sec context (pre + post) CRITICAL MVP Compression H.264 video codec, ~500 KB per clip HIGH MVP Encryption AES-256-GCM for confidentiality HIGH MVP Signing HMAC-SHA256 for authenticity HIGH MVP Local Buffer Ring buffer (100 incidents, 50 MB) MEDIUM MVP Cellular Upload LTE/5G with retry logic CRITICAL MVP Cloud Ingestion FastAPI validation + storage CRITICAL MVP Deduplication Merge duplicate incidents from multiple nodes HIGH MVP Digital Twin Fusion Extract asset data, update twin MEDIUM Phase 2

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Edge Node (Capture) ├── Frame Buffer (rolling 10 sec) ├── Violation Detection (async) └── Incident Packaging ├── Clip extraction ├── Metadata assembly ├── Compression (H.264) ├── Encryption (AES-256) └── Signing (HMAC) ↓ Local Buffer ├── Ring buffer (100 incidents) ├── Persistent storage (SD card) ├── Retry queue └── Status tracking ↓ LTE/Cellular Uplink ├── Encrypt packet ├── POST to cloud API ├── Automatic retry (exponential backoff) └── Fallback to Wi-Fi (depot) ↓ Cloud Ingestion API ├── Signature verification (node auth) ├── Packet decryption ├── Evidence storage (S3/MinIO) ├── Database persistence (PostgreSQL) ├── Deduplication └── Review queue routing ├→ Fast-track queue (95%+ confidence) ├→ Manual review (80-95%) ├→ Discard (<80%) └→ Digital Twin fusion

docs.title: Data Flow Diagram
docs.text:
``` INCIDENT GENERATION ├─ Violation detected (speeding, lane, etc.) ├─ Type + confidence logged └─ Incident ID assigned

CLIP EXTRACTION ├─ 2 sec pre-violation from buffer ├─ Violation event frame(s) └─ 3 sec post-violation from buffer Total: 5-10 sec clip

METADATA ASSEMBLY ├─ Timestamp (frame-level) ├─ GPS (lat, lon, accuracy) ├─ OBD (speed, heading, RPM) ├─ Violation details (type, speed, limit, excess) ├─ ALPR results (if applicable) ├─ Node ID (which vehicle) └─ Confidence score (violation confidence)

COMPRESSION & ENCRYPTION ├─ H.264 video compression │ └─ ~500 KB per 10-sec clip ├─ Metadata JSON (~1 KB) └─ Package size: ~501 KB total

SIGNING & ENCRYPTION ├─ HMAC-SHA256 signature (node secret key) ├─ AES-256-GCM encryption (city public key) └─ Final encrypted package: ~510 KB

LOCAL BUFFERING ├─ Ring buffer: 100 incidents max (50 MB) ├─ If buffer full: oldest incident dropped ├─ If upload fails: retry queue (up to 24 hrs) └─ Status: pending, uploading, success, failed

CELLULAR UPLOAD ├─ Check LTE connectivity ├─ If connected: upload immediately ├─ If no connection: queue locally ├─ Retry strategy: │ ├─ 1st retry: +10 sec delay │ ├─ 2nd retry: +30 sec delay │ ├─ 3rd retry: +60 sec delay │ └─ After 3 failures: try next hour ├─ Depot Wi-Fi offload: │ ├─ Vehicle returns at night │ ├─ Connect to depot Wi-Fi │ └─ Offload queued incidents (cost savings) └─ Success: HTTP 200 + incident_id returned

CLOUD INGESTION ├─ Receive POST /incidents/submit ├─ Verify signature (node authentication) ├─ Decrypt payload (city private key) ├─ Validate schema (Pydantic) ├─ Store evidence clip (S3/MinIO) ├─ Store metadata (PostgreSQL) └─ Return: HTTP 200 + incident_id

DEDUPLICATION ├─ Query: "Is this (plate, violation, location) already reported?" ├─ Geofence check: Within 50m? ├─ Time check: Within last 5 min? ├─ Decision: │ ├─ New incident: Create record │ ├─ Duplicate (lower confidence): Ignore │ └─ Duplicate (higher confidence): Replace └─ Result: Single incident per violation

REVIEW QUEUE ROUTING ├─ Fast-track (≥95% confidence): │ ├─ Auto-issue citation │ └─ Officer minimal review ├─ Manual review (80-95%): │ ├─ Officer watches clip │ └─ Approves/rejects └─ Discard (<80%): └─ Not pursued ```

docs.title: Software
docs.text: Python 3.8+ ├── opencv-python # Frame capture & compression ├── cryptography # AES-256, HMAC ├── requests # HTTP uploads ├── fastapi # Cloud API ├── sqlalchemy # Database ORM ├── boto3 (or minio) # S3-compatible storage ├── pydantic # Schema validation └── pytest # Testing

  • Encryption latency <100 ms

docs.title: 2. Annual Comprehensive Report
docs.text:
Frequency: December 31st annually (automated)

Output: civicmesh.ca/reports/annual/2026.pdf (rendered from same JSON template as monthly)

8-section template (from GOV-003):

  1. Network Overview
  2. Node count by jurisdiction
  3. Vehicle types (bus, city vehicle, contractor kit)
  4. All violations by type (table + sparklines)

  5. Total revenue collected

  6. Enforcement Metrics

  7. Citations issued vs. dismissed
  8. Breakdowns per violation type
  9. False positive rate (target: <5%, actual: report true value)

  10. Appeal outcomes (upheld vs. overturned)

  11. Law Enforcement Access

  12. Requests by authority type: WARRANT, REASONABLE_GROUNDS, EXIGENT, ROUTINE
  13. Approval/denial rate
  14. Notification timeline (avg. hours to notify community)

  15. Any redaction requests (if applicable)

  16. AI Model Performance

  17. Models deployed (name, version, date deployed)
  18. False positive rate per model
  19. Retraining events (dates, reasons, outcomes)
  20. Demographic/environmental bias audit results

  21. Appeal rate (% of citations contested due to AI error)

  22. Privacy Compliance

  23. Data access complaints: received / resolved
  24. Subject access requests: received / response time (avg.)
  25. Data deletion requests: received / compliance rate

  26. Breach incidents (if any, else: "0 breaches")

  27. Revenue Distribution

  28. Gross revenue by source (enforcement fines only)
  29. Percentage breakdown (72% municipal, 15% platform, 8% CCSC, 5% operator)
  30. CCSC patronage fund: recipients and amounts

  31. Civilian rewards pool: total distributed

  32. Governance Activity

  33. RFC submissions this year
  34. RFC outcomes (approved/rejected)
  35. TSC voting record (supermajority decisions)
  36. Contributor role changes

  37. Community audit petitions filed + outcomes

  38. Corrective Actions

  39. Issues identified in prior audit
  40. Remediation status (completed/in-progress/pending)
  41. Outstanding items with timeline

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text: Operational DB (PostgreSQL) ├─ incidents table (violations, citations, dismissals) ├─ audit_logs table (all access) ├─ enforcement_requests table (law enforcement queries) ├─ revenue_ledger table (fines, distribution) └─ model_performance table (FPR, retraining events) ↓ Report Generator (Python scheduled job) ↓ Aggregation Engine ├─ Count violations by type ├─ Calculate FPR (dismissed / total) ├─ Sum revenue per bucket ├─ Check audit_logs for enforcement requests └─ Query law_enforcement_requests table ↓ Validation Engine ├─ Ensure all fields populated (no NULLs) ├─ Verify math (percentages sum to 100%) ├─ Spot-check referential integrity └─ Throw error if validation fails ↓ Report Output (JSON) ↓ ┌────────────────┬──────────────┐ ↓ ↓ ↓ Public API PDF Renderer GitHub Commit (civicmesh.app) (annual.pdf) (archival)

docs.title: Report Generator Service
docs.text:
Location: backend/services/report_generator.py

```python class TransparencyReportGenerator:

def generate_monthly_report(period: str) -> dict:
    """Generate monthly summary (3-5 MB JSON, takes ~30s)"""
    # period = "2026-06"

    # Connect to ops DB (read-only)
    db = connect_readonly("operations_db")

    # 1. Network metrics
    active_nodes = db.query("SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT node_id) FROM telemetry WHERE period = ?", period)
    violations = db.query("SELECT violation_type, COUNT(*) FROM incidents WHERE period = ? GROUP BY violation_type", period)

    # 2. Enforcement metrics
    citations_issued = db.query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM incidents WHERE period = ? AND status = 'issued'", period)
    citations_dismissed = db.query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM incidents WHERE period = ? AND status = 'dismissed'", period)
    fpr = citations_dismissed / (citations_issued + citations_dismissed)

    # 3. Revenue (query blockchain ledger, not internal DB)
    ledger = query_blockchain("CivicMeshLedger", "cityCombinedEarnings", period)
    revenue = ledger['gross_revenue']
    distribution = ledger['distribution']

    # 4. Law enforcement requests
    leo_requests = db.query("SELECT * FROM enforcement_requests WHERE period = ?", period)

    # 5. Validate
    validate_report(monthly_report)  # Throws if any field is NULL

    # 6. Return
    return monthly_report

def generate_annual_report(year: int) -> dict:
    """Generate annual comprehensive report (20-30 MB JSON, takes ~2 min)"""
    # Aggregate 12 monthly reports
    # Add year-over-year comparisons
    # Add auditor verification
    # Validate all 8 sections
    return annual_report

```

docs.title: Validation Engine
docs.text:
Location: backend/models/report_validator.py

```python class ReportValidator:

def validate_report(report: dict) -> bool:
    """Ensure all fields populated, no omissions"""

    mandatory_fields = {
        "period": str,
        "generated_at": str,
        "network.active_nodes": int,
        "network.violations": dict,  # must have all violation types
        "enforcement.citations_issued": int,
        "enforcement.citations_dismissed": int,
        "enforcement.false_positive_rate": float,
        "revenue.gross_total": float,
        "revenue.municipal_share": float,
        "revenue.platform_fee": float,
        "revenue.ccsc_patronage": float,
        "revenue.operator_rewards": float,
        "law_enforcement.requests_received": int,
        "law_enforcement.requests_approved": int,
        "law_enforcement.requests_denied": int,
        # ... all 8 sections
    }

    for field, expected_type in mandatory_fields.items():
        value = get_nested(report, field)

        # No NULLs, no missing fields
        if value is None:
            raise ReportValidationError(f"Missing mandatory field: {field}")

        # Correct type
        if not isinstance(value, expected_type):
            raise ReportValidationError(f"Field {field} has wrong type: {type(value)}, expected {expected_type}")

        # If numeric, no negative (except explicitly allowed)
        if isinstance(value, (int, float)) and value < 0:
            raise ReportValidationError(f"Field {field} cannot be negative: {value}")

    # Verify revenue math
    total = report['revenue']['municipal_share'] + \
            report['revenue']['platform_fee'] + \
            report['revenue']['ccsc_patronage'] + \
            report['revenue']['operator_rewards']

    if abs(total - report['revenue']['gross_total']) > 0.01:  # Allow rounding error
        raise ReportValidationError(f"Revenue doesn't sum: parts={total}, total={report['revenue']['gross_total']}")

    # Verify FPR math
    fpr = report['enforcement']['citations_dismissed'] / \
          (report['enforcement']['citations_issued'] + report['enforcement']['citations_dismissed'])

    if abs(fpr - report['enforcement']['false_positive_rate']) > 0.001:
        raise ReportValidationError(f"FPR doesn't match: calculated={fpr}, reported={report['enforcement']['false_positive_rate']}")

    return True

```

```python def test_monthly_report_all_fields_populated(): """Every mandatory field must have a value""" report = generate_monthly_report("2026-06") assert report['period'] == "2026-06" assert report['network']['active_nodes'] > 0 assert len(report['network']['violations']) > 0 assert report['enforcement']['citations_issued'] >= 0 # ... check all fields assert None not in flatten(report) # No NULL values anywhere
