docs.title: 6. Market Opportunity Statement
docs.text: The CivicMesh platform addresses an urgent and documented market gap in Canadian municipal enforcement infrastructure created by Ontario's November 2025 automated speed enforcement camera ban. The City of Ottawa alone lost $27.8M in speed camera revenue and $10.9M in red light camera revenue annually ($38.7M total). The platform's beachhead market — OC Transpo bus lane enforcement — operates under existing HTA authority with no new legislation required, making it deployable within a 12–18 month commercial timeline following platform readiness. The broader market includes: municipal enforcement infrastructure (Flock Safety comparable — $7.5B valuation), community safety technology (Ring comparable — Amazon acquisition at $1.2B), navigation data (Waze comparable — Google acquisition at $1.3B), and insurance telematics (global market $11.4B by 2030). CivicMesh's privacy-preserving, open-source, community-owned architecture addresses the documented political and regulatory vulnerabilities of all major incumbents.

docs.title: 7. Documentation System
docs.text:
The following contemporaneous documentation system is in place from Day 1 to support IRAP milestone reporting and SR&ED claims simultaneously:

System Purpose Setup Status GitHub (D-Central organization) Every commit message describes the technical problem being solved, the approach taken, and any failures encountered. Branch names reference Linear/Jira ticket numbers. Commit history is the primary contemporaneous evidence record. Set up Day 1 — before any R&D begins Linear (project management) Every task tagged with: SR&ED eligible (yes/no), IRAP milestone reference, technical uncertainty area. At year end the SR&ED consultant runs a report and the claim writes itself. Set up Day 1 Technical Decision Log Running document recording: every architectural decision, alternatives considered, why the chosen approach was selected, what was tried and failed, and what uncertainty remains. This is the narrative CRA looks for. Active from Day 1 Time Tracking Every team member logs hours daily by: project, task, SR&ED eligible vs non-eligible activity, IRAP milestone. Non-dilutive funding lives or dies on time tracking discipline. Active from Day 1 — Harvest or Toggl recommended IRAP Milestone Reports Monthly report to ITA covering: work completed, milestones progress, eligible expenditures, hours logged, technical challenges encountered. Submitted within 10 days of month end. Template prepared before project start

docs.title: IRAP-TM-001 NRC IRAP Application TrafficMesh v1
docs.text:
IRAP-TM-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. NRC IRAP Application — TrafficMesh Platform

Document ID IRAP-TM-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — submit before any TrafficMesh R&D spending Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Funding Application Programme NRC Industrial Research Assistance Programme (IRAP) Relationship to IRAP-CM-001 Companion application — CivicMesh platform-level work covered by IRAP-CM-001; TrafficMesh-specific enforcement, fleet, and digital twin R&D covered here Public Safety Alignment Vehicle-mounted enforcement, law enforcement ALPR support, Amber Alert integration, hit-and-run evidence — direct public safety mandate Related Documents IRAP-CM-001

| Executive Summary This application covers TrafficMesh-specific R&D: the hardware node stack, the enforcement AI models, the OBD-II telemetry fusion system, the digital twin engine, and the evidence pipeline optimised for vehicle-mounted enforcement. While IRAP-CM-001 covers platform-level architecture and privacy systems, this application focuses on the technical work performed by TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. as the operating entity building and deploying the enforcement product. | |---|

docs.title: 1. TrafficMesh-Specific Technical Uncertainties
docs.text: Uncertainty Technical Challenge Why Not Routine Tier 2 Seasonal Node Operation Operating edge compute hardware on snowplow and street sweeper fleets at -30°C with salt spray, vibration, and intermittent power during blade operation. Current commercial dashcam systems are not rated for this environment or for the specific power draw profiles of municipal heavy equipment. No off-the-shelf solution exists. Requires novel enclosure design (IP67+), power management firmware, and cold-start protocols for Jetson Orin at extreme temperatures. Digital Twin Continuous Update from Mobile Nodes Fusing georeferenced image frames from 200+ moving vehicles into a continuously updated, queryable spatial model of Ottawa's road infrastructure — sign condition, lane marking visibility, pavement condition, construction zone boundaries — with sub-24-hour update latency for any given road segment. Existing digital twin systems (Nexar CityStream) use civilian dashcams with random coverage. A city-fleet-driven twin with systematic coverage requires novel routing, deduplication, and confidence-scoring algorithms that have not been published or commercialized. Evidence Package Legal Admissibility Optimization Designing the evidence package schema and chain-of-custody protocol to satisfy the specific requirements of the Ontario Highway Traffic Act for automated enforcement evidence — including the technical specification of what constitutes a 'complete' evidence record sufficient to support a provincial offence notice without an in-person officer. The HTA has no existing technical standard for vehicle-mounted camera evidence. The technical design must anticipate the legal requirements through research with legal counsel, creating genuine technical and legal uncertainty. Contractor Node Kit 5-Minute Deploy Designing a self-contained, weatherproof, tamper-evident enforcement node kit that a non-technical city contractor can install in under 5 minutes on any vehicle type — from a dump truck to a line painter to a parks vehicle — without tools and without technical training. The constraint set (universal vehicle compatibility, <5 minute install, tamper-evident, IP67, -40°C, 8-hour battery backup, cellular connectivity, edge compute) has no existing commercial solution. Requires novel modular hardware architecture.

docs.title: 2. TrafficMesh Milestone Plan
docs.text: Phase Milestone Month IRAP Evidence Phase A Officer review portal and ingestion API operational with simulated evidence. Basic ALPR integration documented with accuracy benchmarks on publicly available footage. Month 3 GitHub commit history, Linear tasks, ALPR accuracy report Phase A Digital twin prototype on Ottawa OpenStreetMap data. Simulated node observations populating twin in real time. Month 5 Working demo recording, architecture documentation Phase B First physical node on test vehicle. Evidence packages from real Ottawa routes captured, uploaded, appearing in officer portal. Month 8 Video evidence, GPS tracks, node credential chain, test report Phase B Contractor kit prototype deployed on 2 vehicle types (personal vehicle + borrowed utility vehicle). 5-minute install documented with timing and photo evidence. Month 10 Installation photos, timing records, design iteration log Phase C 3-node fleet operational with 99%+ evidence pipeline uptime over 30-day test period. OC Transpo pilot proposal submitted to OC Transpo board. Month 14 Uptime logs, evidence package samples, OC Transpo submission receipt Phase C Digital twin live on real Ottawa route data from test node fleet. Public Works defect detection demonstrated with 3 real pothole detections validated against 311 records. Month 17 Twin data export, pothole validation report, 311 correlation

docs.title: 3. Public Safety Dual-Use Justification
docs.text: TrafficMesh directly supports the following public safety mandates that align with the January 2026 IRAP dual-use expansion: - Municipal traffic enforcement: bus lane compliance, school zone protection, construction zone worker safety — all directly reducing road fatalities and injuries; - Law enforcement support: ALPR network integration with Ottawa Police ALPR database for real-time stolen vehicle detection across the city fleet node network; - Amber Alert support: when an Amber Alert is active, every node vehicle receives the target plate in real time — the city fleet becomes a 200-vehicle search network; - Hit-and-run investigation support: any node vehicle in the vicinity of a collision provides footage to investigators — a capability Ottawa Police currently cannot access; - Emergency situational awareness: during major events and emergencies, city fleet nodes provide real-time corridor status to the Emergency Operations Centre.

docs.title: 4. SR&ED and IRAP Interaction
docs.text: Key Rule SR&ED eligible expenditures must be reduced by the amount of government assistance (IRAP) received on the same expenditure. Since IRAP covers up to 80% of eligible salaries, the SR&ED claim on those same salaries is calculated on the remaining 20%. Why Both Are Still Optimal IRAP funds 80% of salary now (monthly cash flow). SR&ED refunds a portion of the remaining 20% at year end. Together they cover approximately 88% of eligible R&D salary costs. Using both is always the right strategy — IRAP for cash flow, SR&ED for the year-end top-up. Accounting Requirement The company accountant must track IRAP receipts by project and by expense category and reduce the SR&ED claim accordingly. This is standard procedure for any SR&ED consultant — flag at engagement. IRAP on SR&ED Consultant Fees SR&ED consultant fees are not IRAP-eligible but are themselves SR&ED-eligible expenditures (as contractor costs). Account for this correctly in the SR&ED claim.

docs.title: 2. Phase 1 PoC Scope (for Transport Canada Road Safety Challenge)
docs.text: Challenge Statement (hypothetical) "Transport Canada seeks innovative solutions for mobile, community-based road safety enforcement that complement existing fixed infrastructure while addressing privacy and community acceptance challenges." Proposed Solution CivicMesh TrafficMesh — a privacy-preserving, open-source, community-owned mobile enforcement platform that uses existing city fleet vehicles as enforcement nodes, with human review before every citation and full community revenue sharing. Phase 1 Deliverable Working proof of concept demonstrating: (a) edge AI violation detection on a vehicle-mounted node, (b) evidence package generation with cryptographic chain of custody, (c) officer review portal with human sign-off before citation, (d) privacy-preserving data governance with community audit log, (e) performance benchmarks on Ottawa road footage. Phase 1 Timeline 6 months from award — aligns with IRAP-TM-001 Milestones M1 through M3 Ottawa Advantage Ottawa is the seat of federal government. Transport Canada HQ is in Ottawa. An Ottawa PoC with direct Transport Canada visibility maximizes Phase 2 award probability.

docs.title: 3. Phase 1 Application Narrative Template
docs.text:
The following narrative framework should be customized to the specific challenge language when a matching challenge is posted:

Section Content Framework Problem Statement Ontario's November 2025 automated speed enforcement ban eliminated $38.7M in annual enforcement revenue for Ottawa alone and created a province-wide gap in road safety infrastructure. Existing fixed camera alternatives are: vulnerable to vandalism (known locations), easily avoided (GPS-mapped by civilians), politically vulnerable (perceived as revenue extraction with no community benefit), and unable to cover residential streets, school zones, or construction sites without permanent installation. Proposed Innovation CivicMesh TrafficMesh is a distributed mobile enforcement network that converts existing city fleet vehicles (buses, plows, sweepers, bylaw vehicles) into enforcement nodes. The innovation is in five areas: (1) edge AI violation detection optimized for Canadian road conditions; (2) privacy-preserving evidence architecture with on-device blurring and cryptographic chain of custody; (3) community-owned cooperative model with revenue sharing to participants; (4) W3C DID/VC credentialing for every node, technician, and MSSP operator; (5) federated data governance allowing community-level audit of every law enforcement data access. Technical Approach [Describe the Phase 1 technical work plan — 6-month milestone schedule from IRAP-CM-001 Section 4. Reference the five technical uncertainty areas. Describe the documentation system that will generate contemporaneous evidence of systematic investigation.] Commercial Potential Target market: all 444 municipalities in Ontario for fleet-mounted enforcement ($X estimated annual revenue potential). Secondary: 3,500+ HOAs and condo corporations in Ottawa for CommunityShield deployments. National expansion to all provinces following Ontario pilot. International (US, UK, Australia) in Years 4–6. Team Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste — founder and lead developer. Currently at Algonquin College. Mitacs research partnerships with [Carleton / uOttawa] for [AI / legal] research. [Additional team members at time of application]. Why ISC Phase 1 (not Phase 2) CivicMesh is at TRL 3–4 at time of application — concept validated, prototype in early development. Phase 1 will advance to TRL 6 (technology demonstrated in relevant environment — Ottawa pilot). Phase 2 application will follow with demonstrated OC Transpo pilot results.

docs.title: 4. ISC and IRAP Interaction
docs.text: Can Both Be Used? Yes — ISC Phase 1 and IRAP can be applied to the same project, subject to government assistance accounting rules. ISC grant must be reported as government assistance and reduces SR&ED eligible expenditures. Optimal Stack IRAP covers 80% of eligible salaries monthly. ISC Phase 1 covers project expenses (materials, contractor costs, overhead) as a grant. SR&ED refunds a portion of the remaining eligible costs at year end. All three are complementary. ISC Award Timing ISC Phase 1 awards are typically disbursed in tranches: 50% on contract signing, 50% on PoC delivery and acceptance. Build this into the cash flow model (FIN-CM-001).

LOAN-CM-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. Innovative Solutions Canada (ISC) Phase 2 Application Framework

| Executive Summary ISC Phase 2 is the largest single non-dilutive grant available to D-Central — up to $1M over 2 years. It funds the full platform build following a successful Phase 1 PoC. Phase 2 applications must demonstrate: validated Phase 1 results, a credible commercialization plan, and a clear path to government procurement adoption. This document is a framework to be completed after Phase 1 results are available. The OC Transpo pilot contract, if executed before Phase 2 application, significantly strengthens the commercialization evidence. | |---|

No privacy complaints from pilot period. REG-CM-001 + pilot period data Open source community engagement GitHub repository with 50+ stars, external contributors from Carleton/uOttawa research, published technical documentation GitHub metrics + Mitacs partnership outputs Commercial momentum 2+ additional municipalities or communities in letter-of-intent or active negotiation MKT-TM-GOV-002 + MKT-CM-GOV-001

Technician, MSSP, node, and municipal partner credentials operational. ~15% of budget Nine application suite All nine applications (Member App through Analytics Platform) in production — iOS, Android, and web ~25% of budget MSSP certification programme First 3 external MSSPs certified and operating. CTC with Algonquin accreditation live. ~10% of budget National expansion groundwork Two provincial government engagements (Quebec, BC) initiated. US market entry analysis completed. ~10% of budget

docs.title: 3. Phase 2 Commercialization Plan Summary
docs.text: Government Procurement Path OC Transpo pilot → City of Ottawa fleet → Hamilton/Mississauga/Toronto → provincial-level procurement framework. Each deployment generates revenue and a reference client for the next. MSSP Channel 3 certified MSSP partners nationally by Phase 2 end — each operating in their own territory, paying platform fees, generating data marketplace revenue. Insurance Partnerships At least 1 insurance company (Intact or Desjardins target) with executed Tier 1 data subscription and Tier 2 individual record API agreement. Premium discount programme active for opted-in members. Revenue Forecast at Phase 2 End $[TBD — complete with financial model FIN-TM-001]: combination of MSSP platform fees, citation revenue share, insurance API subscription, training certification revenue, and data marketplace subscriptions.

docs.title: LOAN-CM-003 Mitacs Accelerate University Research v1
docs.text:
LOAN-CM-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. / D-Central Group Inc. Mitacs Accelerate — University Research Partnership Programme

Document ID LOAN-CM-003 Version 1.0 Status Draft — apply after incubator acceptance (Step 3 in DC-CM-GTM-001) Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Funding Application Programme Mitacs Accelerate — mitacs.ca/en/programs/accelerate Funding Structure Business contributes $7,500 per 4–6 month internship unit. Mitacs covers up to $15,000 per unit (75% of total). Net cost to company: $7,500 for $22,500 of research labour. Target Institutions Carleton University Related Documents LOAN-CM-004

| Executive Summary Mitacs Accelerate is the most capital-efficient research programme available to D-Central. For $7,500, you receive 4–6 months of Masters, PhD, or postdoctoral research focused on CivicMesh's hardest technical problems. The research generates publications (credibility), solves genuine technical uncertainties (SR&ED eligible outcomes), and creates relationships with faculty who become academic advocates for the platform. Three simultaneous Mitacs units = $22,500 investment → $67,500 in research value. This is the Mitacs force multiplier described in IRAP-CM-001. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Proposed Research Projects by Institution
docs.text: Institution Faculty Partner Target Research Question CivicMesh Module Publication Potential Carleton University Computer Vision & Pattern Recognition Lab (or Autonomous Vehicles group) Can YOLOv8-based violation detection achieve >85% precision/recall for bus stop obstruction and lane violation detection on Ottawa road footage under variable Canadian weather conditions, and what novel training data augmentation strategies are required? TrafficMesh — Edge AI IEEE CVPR or IEEE T-ITS (Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems) University of Ottawa — Engineering Distributed Systems or Edge Computing group What edge computing architecture minimizes evidence package latency while maintaining cryptographic chain-of-custody integrity under intermittent LTE connectivity in a vehicle-mounted sensor network? CivicMesh Platform IEEE IoT Journal or ACM MobiSys University of Ottawa — Law Technology Law or Privacy Law faculty Are W3C Verifiable Credentials anchored on a public blockchain legally admissible as foundation evidence in Ontario provincial offence proceedings under the Canada Evidence Act, and what credential schema design satisfies the business records exception? CivicMesh — DID/VC / Legal Canadian Bar Review or University of Ottawa Law Journal Algonquin College — Applied Research Computer Engineering Technology program Can a modular vehicle-mounted enforcement node kit achieve IP67 -40°C operation with <5-minute installation time across diverse city vehicle types, and what hardware design constraints are determinative? TrafficMesh — Hardware IEEE Sensors Journal or Applied Sciences Carleton or uOttawa — Traffic Engineering Civil or Systems Engineering What is the deterrence effect of mobile vs fixed enforcement nodes on driver behaviour on Ottawa bus corridors, as measured by speed distributions and violation frequency before and during deployment? TrafficMesh — Policy/Effectiveness Transportation Research Record or Accident Analysis & Prevention

docs.title: 2. What Makes a Faculty Member Say Yes
docs.text:
Understanding the faculty perspective is essential to successful Mitacs partner recruitment. Faculty members need:

Faculty Need How CivicMesh Satisfies It A genuine research question with publication potential Each of the five research questions above is novel, has a clear hypothesis, and targets a top-tier venue. The problems are not solvable with existing published approaches — which is why they are SR&ED eligible. Industry data and access CivicMesh provides: Ottawa road footage datasets, access to the test node platform, real violation detection outputs for evaluation, and co-authorship on the resulting paper. Student training value Students gain experience with: production edge AI systems, embedded hardware constraints, open-source development practices, and a real commercial deployment environment. This is stronger CV material than a purely academic project. No IP conflict CivicMesh is open source. All research outputs are published openly. There is no IP conflict between the academic publication imperative and the commercial interest — open source publications are additive to CivicMesh's credibility, not competitive. Simple relationship Mitacs handles the paperwork, the payment structure, and the administrative interface. The faculty member focuses on research. D-Central focuses on the commercial application of the results.

docs.title: 3. Mitacs Application Process
docs.text:
• Identify faculty partner at target institution — approach professors working on autonomous vehicles, traffic safety, computer vision, privacy law, or distributed systems;
  • Have initial conversation: describe CivicMesh, propose the research question, confirm genuine research interest and publication potential;
  • Identify a suitable graduate student (Masters or PhD candidate) who will conduct the research — the student is the primary Mitacs intern;
  • Identify an approved incubator (Invest Ottawa, Algonquin Innovation Hub, or Carleton — required for business partner eligibility);
  • Complete Mitacs online application: company profile, research project description, faculty supervisor details, student profile, budget;
  • Mitacs reviews and approves (typically 4–6 weeks). On approval, company pays $7,500 and Mitacs releases $15,000 to the university;
  • Research internship begins — 4–6 months of focused research on CivicMesh's technical problem.

docs.title: 4. Incubator Requirement
docs.text: Required Incubator Mitacs requires the business partner to be housed in or affiliated with an approved incubator for Accelerate applications Target — Algonquin College Innovation Hub Easiest first relationship — founder is a current student. Entrepreneurship office has existing relationships with Mitacs. Student founder narrative is strong for both Algonquin and Mitacs. Target — Invest Ottawa Ottawa's primary startup accelerator. Has direct relationships with City of Ottawa, OC Transpo, and federal departments. Being an Invest Ottawa company gives credibility in every government conversation. Target — Carleton University Accelerator Once Carleton research partnership is established, Carleton incubator residency creates dual-relationship leverage (research + incubator) for both Mitacs and university support.

LOAN-CM-004 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur — Founder Stipend Programme

Document ID LOAN-CM-004 Version 1.0 Status Draft — apply once incubator accepted Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Funding Application Programme Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur — mitacs.ca/en/programs/accelerate/entrepreneur Funding Up to $7,500 per internship unit as founder stipend for commercialization work at approved incubator Eligibility Student or postdoctoral entrepreneur at approved incubator — Algonquin College student founder is exactly the target profile Related Documents LOAN-CM-003

| Executive Summary Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur funds student and postdoctoral entrepreneurs to achieve commercialization goals and further develop the research or technology at the core of their startup, through funded internships at approved incubators. As a current Algonquin College student building a technology startup based on genuine R&D (CivicMesh), Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste is the exact target profile for this programme. The stipend of up to $7,500 per unit directly offsets founder living costs during the early development phase, complementing IRAP salary reimbursements. | |---|

docs.text: Eligibility Criterion D-Central Status Evidence Currently enrolled student at a Canadian university or college Yes — enrolled at Algonquin College Student ID / enrollment confirmation OR postdoctoral fellow / recent graduate N/A — student route applies — Startup based on research or technology developed during studies Yes — CivicMesh architecture developed while at Algonquin GitHub commit history, DC-REG-001 master registry, course project documentation Housed in or affiliated with an approved Mitacs incubator Pending — Algonquin Innovation Hub application or Invest Ottawa LaunchPad Apply to incubator before Mitacs submission Commercialization goals that can be achieved through the internship Yes — OC Transpo pilot proposal, CommunityShield HOA deployments, IRAP application, ISC Phase 1 submission Detailed work plan per Section 2 below

docs.text:

Activity Description Commercialization Outcome OC Transpo pilot proposal development Research OC Transpo procurement requirements, develop formal pilot proposal document (MKT-TM-GOV-007), prepare officer review portal demo for OC Transpo presentation Submitted OC Transpo pilot proposal with supporting technical documentation Regulatory compliance research Commission and review HTA compliance legal opinion (REG-TM-001). Identify regulatory pathway for each CivicMesh module in Ontario. Legal opinion confirming HTA compliance — removes primary go-to-market risk First CommunityShield HOA client acquisition Identify, pitch, and sign first 2–3 Ottawa HOA clients using MKT-CS-COM-001 pitch materials Signed HOA participation agreements (LEGAL-CM-012) generating first revenue IRAP and SR&ED documentation system setup Set up GitHub organization, Linear workspace with SR&ED tags, Technical Decision Log, time tracking system per SRED-CM-001 Documentation system operational — enables IRAP claims and SR&ED refunds University research partnership outreach Approach 3 faculty members at Carleton and uOttawa with Mitacs Accelerate proposals (LOAN-CM-003). Achieve at least 1 committed partnership. Mitacs Accelerate research unit approved with first university partner

docs.title: 3. Incubator Application Strategy
docs.text: Incubator Why Apply Timeline Application Requirements Algonquin College Innovation Hub Founder is a current student — strongest possible relationship. Easiest first incubator. Direct path to Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur eligibility. Apply immediately — Month 1 Talk to Algonquin entrepreneurship office. Bring: business concept summary, DC-STRAT-001 executive summary, incorporation certificate (once available) Invest Ottawa LaunchPad Ottawa's primary accelerator. City of Ottawa relationships. Direct OC Transpo and municipal contacts. Adds credibility for government conversations. Apply Month 1–2 — parallel to Algonquin Online application at investottawa.ca. Requires: business plan, team overview, market opportunity statement. Use DC-STRAT-001 and INV-003 as source documents. Carleton University Sprott Once Carleton Mitacs research partnership is established, Carleton incubator provides dual-relationship leverage and access to Carleton alumni network. Apply Month 3–4 — after Carleton research contact made Requires: startup summary, team credentials, research partnership letter from faculty supervisor

docs.title: LOAN-CM-005 FedDev Ontario Application Framework v1
docs.text:
LOAN-CM-005 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. FedDev Ontario — Regional Technology Growth Application Framework

Document ID LOAN-CM-005 Version 1.0 Status Draft — monitor FedDev programme openings Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Funding Application Programme Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario (FedDev Ontario) Funding Variable — project-based. Historically $250,000–$5M for technology growth projects. Programme Focus Business Scale-Up and Productivity Related Documents LOAN-CM-001

| Executive Summary FedDev Ontario supports economic development, business growth, and innovation in Southern Ontario. D-Central Group is an Ottawa-based technology company creating cooperative employment and community economic infrastructure — a strong fit for FedDev's mandate. FedDev programme openings are not constant — they open and close based on federal budget cycles and government priorities. This document is a readiness framework: the narrative, financial data, and supporting evidence needed to respond quickly when a relevant FedDev programme opens. | |---|

docs.title: 1. D-Central FedDev Value Proposition
docs.text: Regional Economic Impact D-Central Group creates cooperative employment in Ottawa — technicians (certified through CivicMesh Training Institute), MSSP operators, security response providers, and NOC/SOC staff. The cooperative model ensures that economic value generated by the platform stays in the Ottawa region rather than flowing to out-of-province shareholders. Technology Leadership CivicMesh is globally differentiated technology — privacy-preserving, open-source, community-owned enforcement infrastructure with no comparable product in Canada or internationally. Ottawa-based development of this platform builds regional technology leadership in an emerging sector. Export Potential Once the Ottawa pilot is proven, CivicMesh is exportable to every Canadian municipality, then to the US and international markets. FedDev investment in the Ottawa development phase leverages significant future export revenue. Community Economic Infrastructure The cooperative structure means FedDev's investment builds community-owned infrastructure, not extractive private capital. This aligns with FedDev's community economic development mandate. Jobs Created Projected employment from CivicMesh Ottawa deployment: [15–25] direct jobs (D-Central staff) + [50–100] indirect jobs (certified technicians, MSSP operators, security response providers, training providers) within 3 years of platform launch.

docs.title: 2. Target FedDev Programmes
docs.text: Programme Description D-Central Fit Timing Business Scale-Up and Productivity (BSP) Repayable contributions for Southern Ontario SMEs to scale operations, improve productivity, and compete globally CivicMesh at scale — post-OC Transpo pilot, scaling to multiple Ontario municipalities and preparing for export Year 2–3 post-pilot Applied Research and Commercialization (ARC) Support for SMEs to commercialize research and bring innovations to market CivicMesh commercialization — Mitacs research outputs being applied to production platform Year 1–2 concurrent with Mitacs Regional Innovation Ecosystems Support for organizations that strengthen innovation ecosystems in Southern Ontario D-Central Training Institute and MSSP cooperative as innovation ecosystem builders in Ottawa Year 2+

docs.title: LOAN-CM-006 Smart Cities Challenge Framework v1
docs.text:
LOAN-CM-006 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. Smart Cities Challenge — Application Framework

Document ID LOAN-CM-006 Version 1.0 Status Draft — monitor next challenge cycle opening Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Funding Application Programme Infrastructure Canada — Smart Cities Challenge Funding Community population-based: Ottawa (pop. 1M+) eligible for up to $50M implementation prize Challenge Focus Connected mobility, community safety, digital equity, environmental sustainability Partnership Required Municipality must be the applicant — D-Central / CivicMesh would be the technology partner Related Documents LOAN-CM-001

| Executive Summary The Smart Cities Challenge funds municipalities to implement connected, smart city infrastructure. D-Central cannot apply directly — the City of Ottawa or another municipality must be the applicant, with CivicMesh as the technology partner. The strategy is: (1) build the OC Transpo pilot into a proven reference deployment, (2) approach the City of Ottawa's Smart City Office with a partnership proposal, (3) jointly develop a Smart Cities Challenge application with CivicMesh as the primary technology component. This is a Year 2–3 opportunity, not a Year 1 priority. It is listed here for completeness and early relationship-building awareness. | |---|

docs.title: 1. CivicMesh as Smart City Infrastructure
docs.text: Connected Mobility TrafficMesh and MeshNav together deliver real-time road intelligence, transit optimization, and enforcement coordination — core connected mobility infrastructure Community Safety The full CivicMesh platform (all five modules) is community-owned, privacy-preserving community safety infrastructure — directly addressing the Smart Cities Challenge community safety theme Digital Equity The cooperative model ensures that smart city benefits (citation revenue, insurance discounts, safety improvements) flow to all community members, not just to a technology vendor. This is the digital equity argument. Environmental Digital twin road condition data enables condition-triggered maintenance, reducing unnecessary road works. MeshEar and TrafficMesh data can be used to optimize signal timing and reduce vehicle idle time at intersections.

docs.title: 2. Partnership Development Strategy
docs.text:
• Establish OC Transpo pilot — proof of concept with real Ottawa data (Month 12–18)
  • Brief City of Ottawa Smart City Office on CivicMesh results — not a pitch, an informational briefing (Month 18)
  • Introduce City CFO to revenue model and cost savings analysis (Month 18–24)
  • Propose joint Smart Cities Challenge application when next challenge cycle opens (Year 2–3)
  • City of Ottawa as applicant, CivicMesh as primary technology partner, Carleton/uOttawa as research partners, Invest Ottawa as ecosystem partner

docs.title: SRED-CM-001 SR&ED Project Ledger CivicMesh v1
docs.text:
SRED-CM-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. / D-Central Group Inc. SR&ED Project Ledger — CivicMesh Platform

Document ID SRED-CM-001 Version 1.0 — Living document, updated daily Status ACTIVE — set up Day 1, non-negotiable Date May 2026 — ongoing Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — CRA Documentation Programme Scientific Research and Experimental Development (SR&ED) Tax Incentive Programme Filing Entity CivicMesh Inc. (or D-Central Group Inc. if not yet incorporated) — CCPC — refundable credits Refund Rate Up to 35% federal refundable investment tax credit on first $3M of qualified SR&ED expenditures for CCPCs. Ontario provincial top-up adds up to 30% = up to 65% combined refund. Related Documents SRED-TM-001

| Executive Summary SR&ED is Canada's largest government R&D programme — $4.5 billion annually in federal budget. For CCPCs, it is refundable, meaning you receive cash back even with no taxable income. The critical requirement is CONTEMPORANEOUS EVIDENCE — documentation created on the day the R&D occurs, not reconstructed months later. This document establishes the documentation system and daily habits that are worth tens of thousands of dollars in refunds. CRA audits SR&ED claims looking specifically for contemporaneous evidence: Git commit logs, code PRs, Jira/Linear tickets, and technical decision log entries created on the actual dates R&D occurred. | |---|

DAY 1 NON-NEGOTIABLE: Before writing a single line of code or running a single model training experiment, set up the GitHub organization, the Linear workspace with SR&ED eligibility tags, and start the Technical Decision Log. Every day you build without this documentation system in place is a day of SR&ED evidence you cannot recover.

docs.title: 1. SR&ED Eligibility Criteria
docs.text:
For work to qualify as SR&ED, it must satisfy all three of the following criteria:

Criterion Definition CivicMesh Application Scientific or Technological Advancement The work must advance knowledge in a field of science or technology — it must attempt to resolve a technological uncertainty Edge AI violation detection in Canadian conditions, privacy-preserving federation protocol, on-device ALPR optimization, DID/VC chain for evidence admissibility, OBD-II telemetry fusion — all advance knowledge in their respective fields Scientific or Technological Uncertainty There must be a technological uncertainty that cannot be resolved through standard practice, routine engineering, or commonly available knowledge See IRAP-CM-001 Section 3 — five documented areas of genuine technical uncertainty where the solution is not known in advance Scientific or Technological Content The work must follow a systematic investigation and experimentation process — hypothesis, experimentation, documentation of results (including failures) GitHub commit history, Linear task log, Technical Decision Log, and model training experiment logs provide the systematic investigation record CRA requires

docs.title: 2. What Qualifies — CivicMesh Specific
docs.text: Activity SR&ED Eligible? Notes Edge AI model development and training for violation detection ✅ YES — Primary Each training run, hyperparameter search, and model evaluation is SR&ED work. Document every experiment. ALPR optimization for Canadian weather and plate conditions ✅ YES — Primary Systematic investigation with documented failed approaches is the core SR&ED narrative. Privacy-preserving federation protocol design and implementation ✅ YES — Primary Novel architecture with no existing solution — strong SR&ED claim. DID/VC credential schema design and blockchain implementation ✅ YES — Strong Novel application of W3C standards to enforcement evidence — documentable uncertainty. Digital twin mapping engine development ✅ YES — Strong Novel continuous-update algorithm from mobile nodes — no existing comparable system. OBD-II telemetry fusion with camera evidence ✅ YES — Strong Speed estimation from moving reference frame — novel sensor fusion problem. On-device face/plate blurring pipeline ✅ YES — Moderate Novel privacy-preserving processing constraint — document the optimization challenge. MeshEar acoustic classification model development ✅ YES — Primary Custom model for Canadian acoustic environment — strong R&D claim. Officer review portal UI development ❌ NO — Routine Standard React/FastAPI development. Not SR&ED. Segregate time carefully. Business development, sales, marketing ❌ NO Not SR&ED eligible under any circumstances. Routine software integration (API calls to third-party services) ❌ NO Not SR&ED eligible. Document separately from eligible work. Legal research on HTA compliance (technical-legal intersection) ⚠️ PARTIAL The technical design work driven by legal uncertainty may qualify. Discuss with SR&ED consultant.

docs.title: 3.1 GitHub Commit Messages — The Primary Evidence Record
docs.text:
Every commit message must describe the technical problem being solved, not just what was changed. CRA looks at commit history as primary evidence of systematic investigation.

Not SR&ED Evidence (DO NOT WRITE) SR&ED Evidence (WRITE THIS) Fix null pointer Resolve null pointer in GPS timestamp sync during LTE dropout — root cause: race condition in buffer flush. Third approach tried — previous two (mutex lock, queue timeout) caused unacceptable latency. Update ALPR model Retrain ALPR model v7 on augmented Ottawa winter dataset — added 2,400 frost-occluded plate images. F1 score 0.71 → 0.78 on held-out test set. Still below 0.85 production threshold. Next: investigate preprocessing defogging filter. Add federation endpoint Implement federation consent gate v3 — previous design (v2) failed audit log atomicity requirement when federation request and community notification fired simultaneously. New approach uses database transaction wrapping both operations. Fix bug in twin update Debug digital twin update latency — 340ms avg vs 100ms target. Profiled: bottleneck is PostGIS spatial index query on high-density node update. Investigating: (1) spatial index rebuild, (2) update batching, (3) Redis spatial cache.

docs.title: 3.2 Linear Task Tags — SR&ED Eligibility Tagging
docs.text: Every task in Linear must be tagged at creation with: - SR&ED Eligible: Yes / No / Partial - SR&ED Project: [CivicMesh-Platform / TrafficMesh / CommunityShield / MeshEar / MeshNav] - Technical Uncertainty Area: [Edge AI / ALPR / Federation / DID-VC / OBD-Fusion / Acoustic / Digital-Twin / Other] - IRAP Milestone: [M1 through M6 — per IRAP-CM-001] At year end, the SR&ED consultant exports the Linear report filtered by SR&ED Eligible: Yes and the claim is built from the tagged task list. This saves 40+ hours of consultant time and significantly reduces audit risk.

docs.title: 3.3 Technical Decision Log — The SR&ED Narrative
docs.text:
A running Google Doc or Notion page maintained daily. For every significant technical decision:

Field What to Record Date The actual date — not a retroactive entry Technical Problem What specific technical problem or uncertainty was being addressed Approaches Considered At least 2–3 approaches considered, with brief rationale for why each was or was not pursued Approach Chosen What was selected and why Result What happened — including failures (failures are SR&ED gold — they prove the uncertainty was genuine) Remaining Uncertainty What is still unknown — what needs to be investigated next Time Spent Hours spent on this investigation (must match time tracking records)

docs.title: 3.4 Time Tracking — The Financial Foundation
docs.text:
Every team member must log hours DAILY using Harvest, Toggl Track, or equivalent. Time entries must include: - Date and duration - Project (CivicMesh Platform / TrafficMesh / etc.) - Task (with Linear ticket reference) - SR&ED eligible: Yes / No - Brief description of work performed

Time tracking is where most SR&ED claims are reduced or denied on audit. A claim for $80,000 in eligible salaries must be supported by time records showing those hours were spent on eligible activities. Retroactive time reconstruction is not accepted by CRA. Log daily — no exceptions.

docs.title: 4. SR&ED Financial Summary Template
docs.text:
Expenditure Category Estimated Annual Amount IRAP Reduction (80% of eligible salary) Net SR&ED Eligible Federal ITC (35% of net) Ontario Top-Up (~30% of net) Total Refund Founder salary (eligible portion) $75,000 $60,000 $15,000 $5,250 $4,500 $9,750 Additional staff salary (eligible portion — 1 FTE) $70,000 $56,000 $14,000 $4,900 $4,200 $9,100 Contractor costs (eligible portion) $20,000 $10,000 (50% IRAP) $10,000 $3,500 $3,000 $6,500 Materials (hardware, cloud compute) $15,000 $0 (not IRAP-eligible) $15,000 $5,250 $4,500 $9,750 TOTAL $180,000 $126,000 $54,000 $18,900 $16,200 $35,100

Note: Actual amounts depend on IRAP approval scope and eligible salary determination. Engage a qualified SR&ED consultant (suggest FSI Digital, Brainhunter, or similar Ottawa-based firm) in Month 1 to review this model and set up the claim structure before year end. Consultant fee is itself SR&ED-eligible as a contractor cost.

docs.title: 5. Annual SR&ED Claim Process
docs.text: Step Action Timing 1 Year-end close: export GitHub commit history, Linear SR&ED-tagged tasks, time tracking records, Technical Decision Log entries December 31 / fiscal year end 2 SR&ED consultant reviews documentation, identifies eligible activities, calculates eligible expenditures January — February 3 IRAP reimbursements received during the year are deducted from eligible expenditure calculations February 4 T2 corporate income tax return prepared with Schedule 31 (Investment Tax Credit) and Form T661 (SR&ED expenditures) March — April 5 CRA processes refund — typically 60–120 days after filing for e-filed returns June — August 6 If CRA selects for audit: provide all contemporaneous documentation. Do not modify any records. As required

docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. docs.title: SRED-TM-001 SR&ED Project Ledger TrafficMesh v1
docs.text:
SRED-TM-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. SR&ED Project Ledger — TrafficMesh

Document ID SRED-TM-001 Version 1.0 — Living document, updated daily Status ACTIVE — Day 1, non-negotiable Date May 2026 — ongoing Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — CRA Documentation Relationship to SRED-CM-001 Companion ledger — platform-level SR&ED documented in SRED-CM-001; TrafficMesh-specific hardware, enforcement AI, and fleet deployment R&D documented here Related Documents SRED-CM-001

| Executive Summary This ledger covers SR&ED activities specific to TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. — the hardware node stack, violation detection AI models, OBD-II telemetry integration, digital twin engine, and the enforcement evidence pipeline. These activities are conducted by TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. as the operating entity, separate from the platform-level architecture R&D conducted by CivicMesh Inc. Both entities file separate SR&ED claims. Both require the same daily documentation discipline. | |---|

docs.title: 1. TrafficMesh SR&ED Activity Register
docs.text: SR&ED Activity Technical Uncertainty Documentation Required Eligible Expenditure Type YOLOv8 violation detection model training and optimization Model performance on Canadian road footage under variable conditions (weather, lighting, motion) — target accuracy not known in advance Training run logs (hyperparameters, dataset composition, evaluation metrics), GitHub commits for each training iteration, failure documentation when model falls below threshold Salaries + contractor costs + cloud compute materials ALPR on-device optimization — cold weather and motion Production-quality plate recognition under -30°C, frost, salt spray, motion blur — no published benchmark for this constraint set Accuracy reports by weather condition, camera settings experiments, preprocessing filter comparison (defogging, deblurring, contrast), failed approach documentation Salaries + hardware materials OBD-II telemetry fusion for speed estimation Mathematical uncertainty: estimating speed of a target vehicle relative to a moving reference frame from OBD-II + GPS + optical flow Sensor comparison logs, mathematical model documentation, accuracy vs reference tests, failed fusion algorithm documentation Salaries + hardware materials + contractor costs Digital twin continuous update algorithm Novel algorithm for fusing 200+ moving node observations into a consistently accurate spatial model with defined update latency guarantees Algorithm design documents, performance benchmarking, accuracy tests against known ground truth, update latency measurements Salaries + cloud compute IP67 -40°C node enclosure design and testing Hardware design uncertainty: achieving IP67 rating at -40°C with 8-hour battery backup and <5-minute install constraint simultaneously Design iteration log, test results (temperature chamber if available; outdoor winter testing otherwise), failed design documentation, STEP file version history Salaries + materials (hardware prototypes) Evidence chain-of-custody cryptographic pipeline Novel: applying ATECC608B secure element key management to per-evidence-package signing in a resource-constrained edge compute environment with LTE dropout tolerance Architecture decision log, implementation iterations, failed key management approaches, performance benchmarking (signing latency vs evidence package generation rate) Salaries + hardware materials Contractor node kit 5-minute universal install design Design uncertainty: universal vehicle compatibility (dump truck to line painter to parks vehicle) with <5-minute install constraint — no existing commercial solution Vehicle compatibility test matrix, install timing records by vehicle type, design iteration log with failed approaches, field test results Salaries + materials (prototype kits)

docs.title: 2. TrafficMesh Commit Message Standards
docs.text:
In addition to the general standards in SRED-CM-001, TrafficMesh-specific commit messages should reference the hardware and model context:

Scenario Example Commit Message Model training run Train TM-DETECT-v12: YOLOv8m fine-tuned on Ottawa-Winter-2026 dataset (4,200 annotated frames). mAP@0.5: 0.68 (target 0.85). Main failure mode: bus stop sign occlusion by snow. Next: augment with synthetic snow occlusion data. Hardware design iteration Enclosure v4 cold seal redesign: v3 IP67 seal failed at -28°C due to silicone Shore hardness change. Switched to -55°C rated silicone (Dowsil 3140). Re-test scheduled. Previous 3 material attempts documented in hardware log. OBD-II integration OBD-II speed fusion experiment 7: Kalman filter approach gives 2.1km/h RMS error vs 3.8km/h for simple averaging. Still above 1.5km/h target for enforcement use. Investigating GPS RTK integration as reference signal. Digital twin pipeline Digital twin update latency fix: spatial query time reduced from 340ms to 87ms by replacing ST_DWithin with ST_Intersects on pre-computed bounding box grid. Meets 100ms SLA target. Approach 3 of 3 — previous two approaches documented in technical decision log.

docs.title: 3. Hardware R&D Materials Documentation
docs.text:
Hardware purchased for SR&ED is eligible as materials expenditure. The following documentation is required for each hardware purchase: - Purchase date and supplier invoice (kept with SR&ED records) - Linear ticket reference showing the R&D activity the hardware supports - Disposition record: whether the hardware was consumed in testing, destroyed in testing, or remains as a prototype asset - If destroyed in testing: describe the test and the outcome in the Technical Decision Log on the date of the test

Hardware Item SR&ED Eligible? Notes NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX development kit Yes — if used directly in R&D prototype Document R&D use in Linear ticket. Keep invoice. IMX477 camera modules — multiple units for testing Yes — as materials consumed in R&D Document each unit's role in the experiment log ATECC608B secure element development boards Yes Document cryptographic pipeline R&D use IP67 enclosure materials — multiple prototype iterations Yes — prototypes consumed in testing Document each design iteration and test result Raspberry Pi / Jetson Nano (early prototypes before Orin) Yes — if used in approved R&D project period Early prototyping hardware — document experiments Production hardware for client deployment No — capital equipment, not R&D materials Depreciate as capital; do not claim as SR&ED materials

docs.title: Node Assembly Guide
docs.text:
See civicmesh-hardware/assembly/ASSEMBLY_GUIDE.md for details.

Steps: 1. Prepare carrier board 2. Install USB hub 3. Connect peripherals 4. Install power management 5. Install in enclosure 6. Run QA tests

docs.title: QA Testing
docs.text:
Power-On: Node powers on, LEDs light, no smoke Connectivity: Camera, OBD-II, GPS, modem all functional Thermal: <40C idle, <60C full load

See civicmesh-hardware/assembly/QA_CHECKLIST.md

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Camera: Check CSI-2, verify dmesg, test with libcamera-hello OBD-II: Check UART0 baud (38400), verify vehicle ON state GPS: Move outdoors, wait 5+ min, check antenna Modem: Check APN, verify SIM, check signal

See civicmesh-hardware/assembly/TROUBLESHOOTING.md

docs.title: Phase 0 Dashcam Specifications & Calibration
docs.text:
Camera selection, lens calibration, and integration guide for bus violation detection

Version: 1.0 Last Updated: 2026-06-10

docs.title: Quick Specs
docs.text: Parameter Spec Notes Resolution 1440p (2560 × 1440) @ 30 fps 4:3 aspect ratio, horizontal dominance Field of View (FoV) 120° horizontal Captures bus lane + 1 adjacent lane Codec H.264 (hardware accelerated) 8–10 Mbps bitrate (LTE friendly) Interface USB 2.0 Plug-and-play, no custom drivers Lens Distortion <10% barrel Corrected via software (Phase 1+) Low-Light Performance f/1.8 aperture Functional at 500+ lux (dawn/dusk) Operating Temp −5°C to +50°C Suitable for Canadian winters + summer

docs.title: Position: Front-Mounted (Bus Windshield)
docs.text:
Placement: - Height: 1.2–1.5m from ground (typical bus windshield height) - Angle: 0° (level with horizon, slight downward 5°−10° for lane markings) - Horizontal Offset: Center of bus front (captures both lanes) - Distance from obstacles: ≥30cm from wipers, ≥10cm from roof overhang

Why front-mounted? - Captures bus lane violations (obstruction, red light running) - Lane detection models trained on forward-facing perspective - Minimal wind noise (mounted behind laminate)

docs.title: Mounting Hardware
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Bus Windshield │ │ (laminated glass) │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Adhesive Mount │ ← 3M VHB 4910 (automotive-grade) │ (camera bracket) │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ USB Dashcam (1440p) │ ← Weatherproof housing │ with antenna opening │ ← Allows cellular antenna view └──────────────────────────┘

Mounting Materials: - Adhesive: 3M VHB 4910 tape (automotive, −40°C to +60°C rated) - Bracket: Aluminum with silicone vibration dampers (reduces road noise) - Cable routing: Through weatherstrip (sealed with silicone)

docs.title: Distortion Correction
docs.text:
Problem: Wide-angle lens (120° FoV) introduces barrel distortion (~8–10% at frame edges), affecting vehicle detection accuracy.

Phase 0 Approach: Pre-calibration profile stored in firmware

Calibration Procedure: 1. Photograph checkerboard pattern (8×6) at 2m distance 2. Use OpenCV calibrateCamera() to compute: - Focal length (fx, fy) - Principal point (cx, cy) - Distortion coefficients (k1, k2, k3)

Sample Calibration Matrix: ``` Camera Matrix (K): ┌ ┐ │ fx 0 cx │ fx ≈ 2800 (pixels) │ 0 fy cy │ fy ≈ 2800 (pixels) │ 0 0 1 │ cx ≈ 1280, cy ≈ 720 └ ┘

Distortion Coefficients: k1 = −0.08, k2 = 0.12, k3 = 0.0 (barrel: negative k1) ```

Storage: Calibration matrix burned into firmware at factory. Applied during frame capture (Phase 1+, optional for Phase 0).

docs.title: Geospatial Calibration (Extrinsics)
docs.text:
Objective: Map pixel coordinates in camera frame to real-world 3D locations.

Camera Height: 1.3m above road surface Camera Angle: −5° pitch (slight downward look) GPS Reference: Measured from bus GPS (u-blox NEO-M9N)

Extrinsic Matrix (simplified): ``` Camera → World Transform: - Translation: (0, 1.3, 0) m relative to bus center - Rotation: Pitch −5°, Yaw 0°, Roll 0°

PerspectiveTransform (for bird's-eye view reconstruction): Map frame corners to road coordinates: (0, 720) → (−3.5, 2.0) m [left edge, near] (2560, 720) → (+3.5, 2.0) m [right edge, near] (0, 0) → (−2.5, 15) m [left edge, far] (2560, 0) → (+2.5, 15) m [right edge, far] ```

Use Case (Phase 1+): Measure vehicle position, lane occupancy, stopping distance

docs.title: On-Device (Raspberry Pi)
docs.text:
Frame Capture (30 fps): python 1. USB camera → raw YUV420 2. Debayer + demosaic (if Bayer pattern) 3. Lens distortion correction (lookup table) 4. Crop to 16:9 (optional, save bandwidth) 5. H.264 encode (hardware-accelerated, 10 Mbps target) 6. Write to circular buffer (5 sec = 60 MB ring)

Inference on Key Frames: - Every 10th frame (3 fps) → run TensorFlow Lite models - Lane detection (semantic segmentation) - Traffic light detection (YOLO object detection) - Vehicle detection (YOLO) - GPU: None (CPU-only on RPi, but models quantized)

docs.title: Backend (Optional Phase 1+)
docs.text: Re-encoding + Format Conversion: - Convert H.264 → MP4 (FFmpeg) - Thumbnail generation (6 frames per minute) - Metadata injection (GPS, speed, timestamp)

docs.title: Dynamic Range
docs.text:
• Minimum: 500 lux (dawn/dusk, overcast)
  • Maximum: 100,000 lux (midday sun, reflected light)
  • Functional Range: 500–50,000 lux
  • Performance: Automatic white balance + gain correction

Note: Far exceeds typical dashcam (480–5,000 lux functional)

docs.title: Frame Rate
docs.text:
• Capture: 30 fps (hardware native)
  • Inference: 3 fps (every 10th frame, CPU budget)
  • Playback: 24 fps (smooth review for officers)

docs.title: Latency
docs.text:
• Capture to buffer: <33 ms (1 frame)
  • Detection to alert: <500 ms (5 frames)
  • Alert to upload trigger: <1 sec (queued for LTE)

docs.title: Operating Conditions
docs.text: Parameter Spec Notes Temperature −5°C to +50°C Canadian winter (−20°C with heated enclosure) Humidity 20–80% RH Silica gel packets in enclosure Vibration MIL-STD-810H (bus suspension) Mounted on dampers Shock 20G peak (pothole bump) Aluminum bracket absorbs Salt Spray ASTM B117 (500 hr) Outdoor exposure in winter salt Dust IP67 rating Sealed enclosure protects sensor

docs.title: Weatherproofing
docs.text:
Lens Housing: - Polycarbonate dome (hydrophobic coating, prevents fogging) - Internal heater element (−10°C, prevents condensation) - Drain holes (weep holes for moisture escape)

Connector Protection: - Grommet-sealed USB port (microfiber gasket) - Conformal coating on PCB (salt spray resistance)

docs.title: Raw Frame Specs
docs.text: Property Value Resolution 2560 × 1440 (4:3) Bit Depth 8-bit per channel (Y'CbCr) Color Space ITU BT.601 (NTSC) Uncompressed Size ~11.2 MB per frame

docs.title: H.264 Encoding
docs.text:
Codec Settings (Hardware, RPi VideoCore IV): - Bitrate: 8–10 Mbps (adaptive, QoS-aware) - GOP (Group of Pictures): 30 frames (1 sec keyframe interval) - Profile: Main (not High, to save CPU) - Level: 4.1 (supports 1440p @ 30 fps)

Compression Ratio: ~1100:1 (from uncompressed 11.2 MB/frame to 0.01 MB)

Example Clips: - 30-second clip: ~3.75 MB (LTE upload: <5 sec @ 10 Mbps) - 2-minute clip: ~150 MB (Phase 2+ evidence storage)

docs.title: Factory Calibration
docs.text:
1. Intrinsic Calibration (Distortion): bash # OpenCV checkerboard capture python calibrate_intrinsics.py --images=checkerboard/*.jpg --output=K.yaml
  2. Produces: Focal length, principal point, distortion coefficients

  3. Stored in: Firmware blob (non-volatile)

  4. Extrinsic Calibration (Position/Angle): bash # Measure: Camera height (1.3m), pitch (−5°), yaw (0°) # Compute: Perspective transform matrix for bird's-eye view # Validation: Test with known lane width (3.0m standard), verify pixel-to-meter conversion

  5. Functional Test:

  6. Capture test footage (lane markings, traffic signals, vehicles)
  7. Run inference (lane detection, traffic light detection)
  8. Compare detected lanes vs. ground truth (manual measurement)
  9. Accept: <5% deviation in lane boundaries

docs.title: Field Verification (Per Bus)
docs.text: OC Transpo Technician Checklist: - [ ] Camera mounted level (use smartphone level app, ±2°) - [ ] No obstructions (wipers, roof lip, antennas) - [ ] USB cable secured (velcro, no strain) - [ ] Lens clean (isopropyl alcohol, microfiber cloth) - [ ] Firmware version matches bundle (QR scan) - [ ] Test clip uploaded (verify H.264 playback in web portal)

docs.title: Issue: Backlighting (Sun in Frame)
docs.text:
Symptom: Lane detection fails when sun is behind vehicle (halo effect)

Mitigation: - Automatic brightness correction (histogram equalization) - Fall back to GPS + OBD speed (no visual verification) - Phase 1: IR filter + multi-spectral sensors

docs.title: Issue: Nighttime Performance
docs.text:
Symptom: Low lux (<500) causes high noise, poor lane visibility

Mitigation: - Infrared LED ring (Phase 1+ option, +$20) - Rely on street lighting (most urban routes suffice) - Disable visual lane detection at night (use GPS trajectory only)

docs.title: Issue: Raindrops on Lens
docs.text:
Symptom: Water spots obscure lane markings

Mitigation: - Hydrophobic lens coating (maintained quarterly) - Wiper-like mechanism (Phase 1+, mechanical or heated) - Accept reduced confidence during heavy rain (human review required)

docs.title: Issue: Lens Distortion at Edges
docs.text:
Symptom: Lane detection model trained on "normal" lenses fails at extreme FoV

Mitigation: - Crop to 90° effective FoV (Phase 1) - Retrain models on wide-angle datasets (currently done) - Apply undistortion pre-processing (CPU budget allows)

docs.title: Lifespan
docs.text:
• Design Life: 3 years (industry standard for automotive dashcams)
  • Common Failures:
  • Sensor: CCD/CMOS degradation (image quality drop)
  • Lens: Fungal growth (humidity), scratches, coatings fade
  • Electronics: Capacitor dry-out (−20°C thermal stress)

docs.title: Replacement Cost
docs.text:
• New Camera Unit: ~$180
  • Labor (install + calibration): ~$30
  • Total: ~$210 per replacement

docs.title: Preventive Maintenance
docs.text:
• Monthly: Wipe lens with microfiber cloth
  • Quarterly: Replace silica gel packets (in enclosure)
  • Yearly: Inspect for condensation, replace if present

docs.title: Pre-Deployment Checklist
docs.text: Test Pass Criteria Method Optical Capture readable lane markings @ 20m Test footage review Encoding H.264 playback without artifacts FFmpeg compliance check Thermal Enclosure <50°C in direct sun (1 hr) IR thermometer Connector USB enumeration on cold-start Plug/unplug 5× Vibration No image blur @ 40 mph over pothole Frame inspection Weatherproofing No internal moisture after rain Humidity sensor

docs.title: Field Validation (Phase 0 Pilot)
docs.text:
• 50 buses × 4 weeks = real-world footage corpus
  • Validate lane detection accuracy (compare to manual review)
  • Collect false negative cases (missed violations)
  • Inform Phase 1 model retraining

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• PHASE_0_MVP_ARCHITECTURE.md § Camera Capture Pipeline
  • OpenCV Camera Calibration
  • USB Video Class (UVC) Specification
  • ITU-R BT.601 Color Space

Version: 1.0 Last Updated: 2026-06-10 Maintainer: CivicMesh Hardware Team

docs.title: Phase 0 Node Hardware Bill of Materials
docs.text:
Per-vehicle node for OC Transpo buses: bus lane obstruction + red light detection

Version: 1.0 Total Cost Range: $485–$600 USD (in-volume pricing) Target Deployment: 50 OC Transpo buses (Phase 0 pilot)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Category Unit Cost Qty Subtotal Notes Compute Raspberry Pi CM4 (2 GB RAM, eMMC) $70 1 $70 Edge inference, event loop Camera USB Dashcam (Front, 1440p, 120°) $180 1 $180 H.264 encoding, 30 fps Vehicle Integration OBD-II Dongle (ELM327 clone) $35 1 $35 Speed, RPM, GPS, diagnostics GPS Module (u-blox NEO-M9N) $28 1 $28 5 mm accuracy, 30 Hz update LTE Modem (Quectel EC25) $50 1 $50 Upload, telemetry, heartbeat Security TPM 2.0 Module (SLB9670) $18 1 $18 Hardware key storage, attestation Power Management OBD-II Power Adapter (12V→5V buck) $25 1 $25 Vehicle power input Battery Backup (5V, 10 Ah, UPS hat) $65 1 $65 2–4 hr uptime during disconnect Power Distribution Board $15 1 $15 Fuse management, voltage regulation Enclosure & Weatherproofing Aluminum Enclosure (150×100×75mm) $28 1 $28 IP67 rated, heat dissipation Silicone Gasket + Stainless Hardware $12 1 $12 Water/dust sealing DIN Rail + Cable Glands $10 1 $10 Internal organization Cabling & Connectors OBD-II Harness (Y-split, fused) $15 1 $15 Power + CAN bus USB Cables (USB-C, micro-USB) $8 2 $16 Camera, modem, RPi CAT6 Ethernet (optional, for bench testing) $5 1 $5 Local network access Terminal Blocks + Connectors $8 1 $8 Power distribution Firmware & Configuration NFC Tag (ISO14443A, 1K) $2 1 $2 Node enrollment, read-only config Micro-SD Card (32 GB, U3, A2) $8 1 $8 OS + application storage Documentation & Labels Tamper-evident Seals $3 5 $15 Chain of custody Labels (model, serial, MAC) $2 1 $2 Asset tracking Assembly & QA Assembly Labor (30 min @ $50/hr) $25 Testing, configuration Shipping & Packaging Custom Packaging (protective foam) $8 1 $8 Safe delivery to bus depot TOTAL (Single Node) $529 Factory cost, ex. taxes TOTAL (50-unit batch) $24,500 $490/unit at scale

docs.title: Compute: Raspberry Pi CM4 (2 GB, eMMC)
docs.text:
Part: Raspberry Pi Compute Module 4 (CM4104032) Cost: $70 USD Specs: - CPU: Broadcom BCM2711 (ARMv8, 4×1.5 GHz) - RAM: 2 GB LPDDR4 - Storage: 4 GB eMMC (OS + models) - GPIO: 28-pin SO-DIMM, I²C, SPI, UART - Power: 5V @ 2.5A typical

Why this model? - Industrial-grade (wide temp range: −10°C to +60°C) - eMMC onboard (no SD card wear) - 2 GB RAM sufficient for TensorFlow Lite models + local buffering - Well-documented for automotive edge inference

Alternative: Raspberry Pi 5 CM module (coming late 2026) for better thermal envelope

docs.title: Camera: USB 1440p Dashcam
docs.text:
Part: Generic USB 1440p dashcam (e.g., Viofo A119 Mini, or OEM equivalent) Cost: $180 USD Specs: - Resolution: 2560 × 1440 @ 30 fps - Codec: H.264 (hardware-accelerated) - FoV: 120° (captures bus lane and adjacent lanes) - Bitrate: 8–10 Mbps (compressed) - Interface: USB 2.0 (480 Mbps, sufficient for single camera) - Lens: f/1.8, wide aperture (good low-light performance) - Storage: Micro-SD up to 256 GB

Why this model? - USB interface (no special drivers for RPi) - H.264 hardware encoding (lower bandwidth) - 120° FoV captures bus lane + one adjacent lane (sufficient for violations) - Vandal-resistant (industrial dashcam body) - Cost-effective in volume

Note: Phase 1 adds interior camera for ride-sharing safety (requires second USB port or Ethernet camera)

docs.title: Vehicle Integration: OBD-II Dongle (ELM327)
docs.text:
Part: ELM327 OBD-II Bluetooth/USB dongle (generic, ISO9141-2 compatible) Cost: $35 USD Specs: - Interface: USB (preferred over Bluetooth for reliability) - Protocols: KWP2000, ISO9141-2, CAN 500K, CAN 250K - Data: Real-time vehicle speed, RPM, fuel consumption, diagnostics - Power: 5V from USB

Why this model? - Standard OBD-II pinout (works on all 1996+ vehicles) - USB preferred over Bluetooth (more reliable in cellular interference) - Provides ground truth for speeding detection (Phase 1) - Well-supported by Linux kernel (vcan, can-utils)

Data Stream (for Phase 0): - Vehicle speed (speed validation for bus lane, red light context) - Engine load (idle detection) - Diagnostic codes (vehicle health)

docs.title: GPS Module: u-blox NEO-M9N
docs.text:
Part: u-blox NEO-M9N (breakout board with antenna) Cost: $28 USD Specs: - Accuracy: 5 mm (typical, good for geofencing) - Update Rate: 30 Hz (responsive to road curves) - Interface: UART (3.3V, 9600 baud) - Sensitivity: −167 dBm - Time to First Fix: 30 sec (cold start) - Antenna: Ceramic patch (integrated, weatherproof)

Why this model? - u-blox is automotive-grade (tested to −40°C) - 5 mm accuracy sufficient for 50m geofence dedup - 30 Hz updates capture lane-change GPS drift - UART interface standard on RPi (GPIO pins)

Data Stream: - Latitude, longitude (incident location) - Altitude (elevation changes on hills) - Dilution of Precision (GPS confidence)

docs.title: LTE Modem: Quectel EC25
docs.text:
Part: Quectel EC25-E (Mini PCIe module, no antenna) Cost: $50 USD (+ $15 for antenna + $5 for USB adapter) Total Modem Cost: $70 Specs: - LTE: Cat-4 (up to 150 Mbps DL, 50 Mbps UL, sufficient for video upload) - 2G/3G fallback (GSM, HSPA+) - Interface: USB via Mini PCIe→USB adapter - Antenna: External RP-SMA (5 dBi, rooftop-mounted) - Power: 3.3V/5V (via USB)

Why this model? - Supports Canada bands (LTE Band 7, 20, 32; 2G band 8, 5) - Low power consumption (Phase 1+ enables 5G for faster uploads) - Well-supported on Linux (usb_modeswitch, ModemManager) - Industrial temperature range

Bandwidth (Phase 0): - Evidence clip: ~100 MB @ 10 Mbps = 80 sec upload - Metadata + heartbeat: <1 KB every 10 sec - Fully async (buffered, non-blocking)

docs.title: Security: TPM 2.0 Module (SLB9670)
docs.text:
Part: Infineon OPTIGA™ Trust M TPM 2.0 (SLB9670, I²C) Cost: $18 USD Specs: - Crypto: RSA-2048, ECDSA P-256, AES, SHA-256 - I²C Interface (3.3V) - Secure key storage (non-extractable) - Hardware RNG - Attestation support

Why this model? - Phase 0 use: Store HMAC-SHA256 node secret (read-only via sealed storage) - Phase 1 use: Hardware attestation (prove node firmware integrity) - Phase 2+ use: Smart contract wallet seeds, DID private keys - Already audited for automotive applications

Note: Phase 0 uses simple shared secrets; TPM is reserved for Phase 1 firmware attestation.

docs.title: Power Management: OBD-II + Battery Backup
docs.text:
OBD-II Power Adapter Part: Generic OBD-II 12V→5V buck converter (10A rated) Cost: $25 USD Specs: - Input: 12V (vehicle battery, 10–16V tolerance) - Output: 5V @ 2.5A regulated - Protection: Fused (15A automotive fuse) - Efficiency: 85% (minimal heat)

Battery Backup (UPS Hat) Part: Generic 5V 10 Ah lithium battery pack (with PCM + boost circuit) Cost: $65 USD Specs: - Capacity: 10 Ah @ 5V - Chemistry: Li-ion 18650 (reliable, widely available) - Runtime: 2–4 hours (RPi + modem only, camera disabled) - Auto-switchover on main power loss - Protection: Over-current, over-temperature

Why this setup? - Continuity: Keeps node alive if vehicle power drops (e.g., ignition off, short disconnect) - Buffering: Evidence clips queued locally during connectivity loss - Resilience: Critical for enforcement (can't lose evidence mid-upload)

docs.title: Enclosure: IP67 Aluminum
docs.text:
Part: Generic aluminum DIN-rail enclosure (150 × 100 × 75 mm) Cost: $28 USD Specs: - IP67 rating (dust + water jet proof) - Material: 1.5 mm aluminum (thermal dissipation for modem/buck converter) - Mounting: DIN rail inside, cable glands on base - Heat dissipation: Passive (no fans, automotive reliability) - Dimensions: Fits under bus seat or in wheel well

Weatherproofing Add-ons: - Silicone gasket + stainless M4 hardware: $12 - Cable glands (M20, M16): $10

Why this config? - Canadian buses exposed to −20°C winters, rain, salt spray (IP67 essential) - Aluminum dissipates modem heat passively (LTE modem ~3W typical) - DIN rail allows easy module replacement in field

docs.title: Cabling & Connectors
docs.text: Item Cost Notes OBD-II Y-harness (power + CAN) $15 Splits 12V (to buck) + CAN (unused Phase 0) USB Type-C (RPi to modem) $5 Shielded, 3m length Micro-USB (dashcam) $3 Redundant (both USB-C + micro available) CAT6 Ethernet (for bench test) $5 Optional, helpful for field diagnostics Terminal blocks + Anderson connectors $8 Reliable power distribution, quick disconnects Subtotal $36

docs.title: NFC Tag (Enrollment)
docs.text:
Part: NTAG216 (ISO14443A, 888 bytes) Cost: $2 USD Data Stored: - Node ID: "OC_TRANSPO_BUS_001" - Firmware version: "0.1.0" - Certificate thumbprint: Blake3 hash - Read-only: Tag locked after factory programming

Why this? - Technician scans tag with smartphone to verify node during installation - Tamper-evident: Locked tag ensures config integrity - Phase 1+ supports W3C Verifiable Credentials (DID authentication)

docs.title: Firmware & OS
docs.text:
Micro-SD Card (32 GB, U3, A2) Cost: $8 USD Contents: - Raspberry Pi OS Lite (−35 MB) - Python 3.11 + dependencies (−200 MB) - TensorFlow Lite models (−80 MB, ALPR + lane detection) - Local incident buffer (remaining space)

Firmware Image: - Pre-burned on 4 GB eMMC at factory - SD card used as read-only backup (emergency boot fallback)

docs.title: Documentation & Chain of Custody
docs.text: Item Cost Notes Tamper-evident seals (5/node) $15 Hologram stickers, unique serial per node QR code labels (model, serial, MAC) $2 Asset tracking, firmware version, MAC address Installation manual (printed) $1 Quick-start guide for OC Transpo technician Calibration certificate $1 GPS accuracy, camera lens distortion profile Subtotal $19

docs.title: Assembly & QA
docs.text:
Assembly Labor: 30 minutes @ $50/hr = $25/unit

QA Checklist: 1. Power delivery test (5V, 12V stable) 2. Camera stream test (USB enumeration, 30 fps capture) 3. GPS cold start (<60 sec lock) 4. OBD-II read (speed validation) 5. Cellular connectivity (LTE registration, data throughput >1 Mbps) 6. TPM secure key load (HMAC verify) 7. Temperature stress (−5°C to +45°C functional) 8. Enclosure seal test (hose spray, no water ingress)

Time: ~20 minutes per node

docs.title: Packaging & Shipping
docs.text:
Per-Unit Packaging: $8 - Custom foam cutout (protective) - Anti-static bag - Thermal padding (winter shipping) - Tracking label

Bulk Shipping (50 units): - Pallet: 50 units @ 2 kg each = 100 kg - Toronto → OC Transpo depot: ~$300 (freight) - Cost per unit: $6

docs.title: Phase 0 Cost Summary
docs.text: Tier Unit Cost 50-Unit Batch Notes COGS (Parts only) $420 $21,000 Hardware components Assembly + QA $25 $1,250 Labor, testing Packaging + Shipping $14 $700 Physical delivery Warranty Reserve (5%) $23 $1,150 Defect replacement Total Cost Per Unit $482 $24,100 Factory cost Markup (30% margin) +$145 — Gross profit Sales Price Per Node $627 — OC Transpo unit cost

docs.text:
• Direct purchase from distributors (RS, Digikey, Mouser)
  • Lead time: 2–3 weeks
  • Cost: $550–650/unit (retail)

docs.title: Phase 1 (50 units, full OC Transpo rollout)
docs.text:
• Contact manufacturers for volume pricing
  • Lead time: 4–6 weeks
  • Target cost: $485–500/unit (factory contract)
  • Suppliers:
  • Compute: Raspberry Pi (direct partnership)
  • Dashcam: Viofo or OEM (bulk rebrand)
  • Modem: Quectel (authorized distributor, CAD inventory)
  • Integration: Contract assembly house in Ontario (30 min labor)

docs.title: Phase 2 (200+ units, city fleet expansion)
docs.text:
• Vertical integration (import components, in-house assembly)
  • Target cost: $420/unit
  • Explore shrink-wrapped kits (modular plug-and-play)

docs.title: Risk & Mitigation
docs.text: Risk Impact Mitigation Component shortage 4–6 week delay Multi-source key components (RPi, modem) Thermal stress Modem shutdown in summer Active heatsink (Phase 1) or vehicle AC OBD-II compatibility Bus model-specific failures Firmware fallback (use GPS + camera only) Battery degradation Data loss during long power-off Replace battery every 2 years ($65) GPS urban canyon Lost signal in downtown tunnels Pre-downloaded map (Phase 1+) Cellular connectivity Slow upload (Toronto coverage gaps) Local buffering (2–4 hr storage)

docs.title: Maintenance & Replacement
docs.text:
Phase 0 (Pilot): OC Transpo responsible for node maintenance (OC Transpo support includes 12-month warranty)

Typical Lifespan: - Compute (RPi CM4): 5+ years - Camera: 2–3 years (lens degradation, sensor burn-in) - Battery backup: 2 years (Li-ion degradation) - Modem: 3–4 years (firmware end-of-life) - Enclosure: 10+ years (aluminum corrosion-resistant)

Replacement Kit Cost: ~$150 (camera + battery)
