docs.title: Phase 0 Power Management Architecture
docs.text:
OBD-II power input, 5V regulation, battery backup, and uptime guarantees for bus nodes

Version: 1.0 Last Updated: 2026-06-10

docs.title: OBD-II Harness Pinout (16-pin)
docs.text:
Pin Signal Voltage Usage Our Use 2 J1962 Power 12V Ignition-switched power ✗ (no ignition dependency) 4 Chassis Ground GND Return path ✓ Ground reference 5 Signal Ground GND CAN/K-line ground ✓ Ground reference 6 CAN High Variable CAN bus (high) ⚠ Phase 1 (speed data) 14 CAN Low Variable CAN bus (low) ⚠ Phase 1 (speed data) 16 Vbat 12V constant Battery power (always-on) ✓ Primary power

Power Extraction (Pins 4 + 16): - Positive (Pin 16): 12V, always-on (vehicle battery) - Negative (Pin 4): Chassis ground - CAN Bus (Pins 6 + 14): Reserved Phase 1 (OBD speed verification)

docs.title: OBD-II Y-Harness Assembly
docs.text:
OBD-II Connector │ ├─────────► CAN High/Low (thin pair, to future CAN hat, Phase 1) │ └─────────► Power Module │ ├─ Fuse holder (15A, ATO automotive) │ └─ Fuse link for safety (hot-swap protection) │ └─ Ring terminal connectors ├─ Positive (red, 10 AWG wire) └─ Negative (black, 10 AWG wire)

Wire Gauge Selection: - OBD-II connector → Buck converter: 10 AWG (5m max run, <3% voltage drop) - Power dissipation at max current: P = I²R = 10² × 0.0009 = 0.09W (negligible)

Fuse Protection: - Fuse rating: 15A automotive (ATO type) - Breaks circuit if current exceeds 15A (protects wiring, buck converter) - Trip time: <1 sec at 300% rated current (safe for electrical fault)

docs.title: Component Spec
docs.text: Part: Generic synchronous buck converter (10A rated) Input: 12V (vehicle battery, 10–16V operating range) Output: 5V ± 0.5V regulated Max Current: 10A (50W output) Efficiency: 85–92% (minimal heat dissipation)

docs.title: Circuit Diagram (Simplified)
docs.text:
12V In ──┬─────────┐ │ │ │ Q1 │ MOSFET (sync buck switch) │ ││ │ │ ││ │ Vin ────┤ ├──┬─ Inductor L ──┬─ Out │ │ │ │ │ Q2 │ Schottky D │ │ ││ │ │ │ ││ └───────────────┼─ 5V (regulated) │ │ │ └─────────┴──────────────┴─ GND

Key Components: - MOSFETs (Q1, Q2): Infineon OptiMOS (low RDSon, <50mΩ) - Inductor (L): 10 µH ferrite core (handles 10A peak current) - Schottky Diode: 10A rated (fast reverse recovery) - Feedback Circuit: TL431 (precision voltage reference)

docs.title: Operating Characteristics
docs.text:
Condition Output Ripple Efficiency Full Load (10A @ 5V) 5.0V 40 mV 92% Half Load (5A @ 5V) 5.0V 20 mV 90% Idle (0A) 5.0V 10 mV 50% (quiescent loss) Cold Start (−5°C) 4.8V 60 mV 85% (thermal lag)

Thermal Performance: - Power dissipation (50W in, 42.5W out): 7.5W lost as heat - Heatsink: Aluminum 50×50mm (θJA ≈ 5°C/W) - Junction temperature rise: 7.5 W × 5°C/W = 37.5°C above ambient - At +50°C ambient: Total ~88°C (safe, within 125°C max)

docs.title: Power Distribution Board (PDN)
docs.text: Function: Aggregate buck converter output, distribute to loads, add protection layers.

docs.title: Schematic (Simplified)
docs.text:
Buck Out (5V) ──┬─────────────────┬────────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌──────┴──────┐ ┌──────┴──────┐ ┌──────┴──────┐ │ Polarity │ │ TVS Diode │ │ Capacitors │ │ Protection │ │ (5.8V) │ │ (100 µF) │ │ (Diode) │ │ (surge) │ │ │ └──────┬──────┘ └──────┬──────┘ └──────┬──────┘ │ │ │ └────────────┬────┴────────────────────┘ │ 5V distribution rail ┌────────┴────────┐ │ │ ┌─────┴──┐ ┌────┴─────┐ │ Fuse 1 │ │ Fuse 2 │ │ 5A │ │ 2A │ └─────┬──┘ └────┬─────┘ │ │ ┌─────▼────────┐ ┌─────▼────────┐ │ RPi CM4 │ │ Modem+Camera │ │ (≤2A) │ │ (≤2A) │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘

Component Details:

Part Rating Purpose Polarity Diode 1N4148 Reverse-polarity protection (prevents magic smoke) TVS Diode SP3012-2BTG (5.8V) Clamp voltage spikes >5.5V (inductive kick from modem) Bulk Capacitor 100 µF × 2 (in parallel) Stabilize voltage sags during 10A current draw Fuse 1 5A (for RPi + modem) Protects compute subsystem Fuse 2 2A (for camera) Protects camera power (USB, isolated)

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Problem: Vehicle ignition off for 10+ minutes → OBD-II power lost → node stops recording → missed violations.

Solution: Battery UPS hat provides 2–4 hours of backup power, maintains local buffering during connectivity loss.

docs.title: Hardware Spec
docs.text:
Part: Generic 5V 10 Ah lithium UPS module (with auto-switchover circuit)

Chemistry: Li-ion 18650 cells (2S5P configuration, 10 cells total) - Series: 2× (3.7V → 7.4V nominal) - Parallel: 5× (1.8 Ah per cell → 9 Ah total) - Nominal: 7.4V / 5A max discharge = 9 Ah effective (accounting for protection circuit)

PCM (Protection Circuit Module): - Overcharge protection: Cutoff at 8.4V (2.1V per cell) - Overdischarge protection: Cutoff at 6.0V (3.0V per cell, max safe discharge) - Over-current protection: Cutoff at 20A (emergency dump, protects cells) - Temperature sensor: Disable charging below 0°C / above 45°C

docs.title: Auto-Switchover Circuit
docs.text:
Topology: Ideal Diode (Schottky + MOSFET gate drive)

12V ────┐ │ ┌───┴────┐ │ Schotty │ (ideal diode) │ D1 │ │ │\__ │ │ │______|─── + 5V to Load │ │ ┌───┴─────┐ │ │ UPS │ │ │ Battery │──┘ └─────────┘

Operation: - Vehicle ON (12V available): Buck converter feeds load (5V), charges battery via 1A charger - Vehicle OFF (12V lost): Ideal diode switches to battery, provides 5V backup (via internal boost circuit, 7.4V → 5V) - Switching latency: <5 ms (imperceptible to RPi, continuous 5V rail)

docs.title: Runtime Calculation
docs.text:
Power Budget (backup mode): - Raspberry Pi CM4: 1.5W (idle, no GPU) - Modem (LTE sleep mode): 0.2W (registered, idle) - Sensors (GPS, OBD, TPM): 0.3W (polling only, no TX) - Total backup draw: ~2.0W @ 5V = 0.4A

Battery capacity: 9 Ah @ 5V (45 Wh)

Runtime: 45 Wh ÷ 2.0W = 22.5 hours continuous backup

Conservative estimate (account for efficiency losses, end-of-discharge curve): - Practical runtime: ~15–18 hours - Phase 0 requirement: 2–4 hours (covers overnight + early morning) - ✓ Exceeds requirement

Evidence Buffering (Phase 0 MVP): - Local buffer: 60 GB on eMMC (RPi CM4 comes with 4 GB, but supplementary storage possible) - At 10 Mbps H.264: 60 GB ÷ (10 Mbps / 8) = 48,000 seconds ≈ 13.3 hours - Battery lasts 15 hr, buffer lasts 13.3 hr → Sufficient (upload resumes when vehicle powers on)

docs.title: RPi CM4 Power Consumption
docs.text:
Mode Voltage Current Power Notes Idle (no load) 5.0V 0.3A 1.5W No GPU, sleep timers active Camera capture 5.0V 0.6A 3.0W USB camera streaming Inference (3 fps) 5.0V 0.9A 4.5W TensorFlow Lite models running Peak (all subsystems) 5.0V 1.2A 6.0W Rare, transient

Average (Phase 0 operation): ~3.5W

docs.title: Modem (Quectel EC25) Power
docs.text:
State Current Power Notes Off <10 mA 0.05W No radio activity Idle (registered) 50 mA 0.25W Maintaining cell connection Transmit (full power) 400 mA 2.0W Upload operation (peak) Receive (DL) 150 mA 0.75W Download/API responses

Average (Phase 0): ~0.3W (idle + periodic heartbeat)

docs.title: Camera + USB Hub
docs.text:
Component Current Power USB Dashcam (1440p capture) 500 mA 2.5W USB hub (buffer, optional) 50 mA 0.25W

Total system average (continuous operation): ~6W

docs.title: When Vehicle Powered On
docs.text:
Charger Input: 12V (OBD-II) Charger Output: 5V @ 1A (dedicated charging circuit, separate from buck converter output)

Charging Profile: - Bulk charge phase: Constant 1A @ <8.4V (until ~85% SOC) - Taper charge phase: Constant voltage 8.4V, decreasing current (until <50 mA) - Float charge: 8.1V trickle (maintains capacity, prevents overcharge)

Charging time (0→100%): ~9 hours (10 Ah ÷ 1A + taper curve)

Scenario: OC Transpo bus operates 16 hr/day, parked 8 hr/night - Daily charge available: 8 hr × 1A = 8 Ah - Daily drain (Phase 0 MVP): ~0.4A × 2 hr (backup usage rare) = 0.8 Ah - Net charge: +7.2 Ah per day → Battery full within 2 days

docs.title: Heat Dissipation Paths
docs.text:
``` Junction (MOSFET) ──┬─ PCB thermal plane (0.05°C/W) │ ├─ Heatsink (5°C/W) │ ├─ Enclosure wall (aluminum) │ └─ Ambient air (15°C/W)

Total θJA ≈ 25°C/W (conservative estimate) ```

Worst-case scenario: Full load (50W input, 42.5W out, 7.5W heat) @ +50°C ambient - ΔT = 7.5W × 25°C/W = 188°C rise (FAILURE) - Mitigation: Reduce load or improve heatsinking (thermal paste, clip-on fin)

Realistic scenario: Average load (6W total system, 1W loss) @ +40°C ambient - ΔT = 1W × 25°C/W = 25°C rise → Junction ~65°C (SAFE)

docs.title: Thermal Management (Phase 0)
docs.text:
Passive cooling sufficient if: 1. Aluminum enclosure mounted with thermal contact to node exterior 2. Cable entry glands allow some convection 3. Node not in direct sunlight (cover with reflective tape if parked in sun)

Phase 1 option: Add small 5V fan (0.3W) for active cooling in high-temp scenarios

docs.title: Scenario 1: OBD-II Connector Loose/Corroded
docs.text:
Symptom: Intermittent 12V power loss, voltages fluctuate 8–15V

Detection: Buck converter voltage monitoring, triggers alert if Vin < 10V for >5 sec

Recovery: - Firmware: Log power disruptions (audit trail) - Failover to battery (if available) - Buffer evidence locally (camera keeps recording) - Upload queued clips when 12V restored

Timeline: - T+0: Connector loosens, Vin drops - T+5 sec: Firmware detects Vin < 10V, switches to battery - T+5 sec to T+24hr: Run on battery, buffer evidence - T+24hr: Vehicle powered on again, 12V restored, uploads resume - ✓ Zero evidence loss

docs.title: Scenario 2: Battery Discharge During Extended Downtime
docs.text:
Symptom: Vehicle parked for 2+ weeks, battery fully discharged, no 5V power

Recovery: - Next vehicle power-up: Buck converter boots RPi from 12V - RPi firmware checks battery SoC (via PCM fuel gauge) - If SoC < 10%: Skip evidence uploads (focus on safety), charge battery for 4 hrs before normal operation - Battery recovers to usable capacity (not 100% after deep discharge, but sufficient for 2–4 hr backup)

Prevention: OC Transpo maintenance schedule includes monthly battery top-off (connect charger during winter)

docs.title: Scenario 3: Modem Power Surge (LTE burst transmission)
docs.text:
Symptom: Modem draws 400 mA peak @ 5V, causes <100 mV voltage sag

Protection: - Bulk capacitors (100 µF) absorb current surge, smooth voltage - TVS diode clamps overvoltage transients - Fuse 2 (2A) breaks if sustained overcurrent (indicates fault)

Impact: RPi unaffected (capacitors maintain 5V±0.5V), modem operates normally

docs.title: Voltage Monitoring (RPi GPIO)
docs.text:
Analog input (via ADC hat, Phase 1): ```python import board import busio import adafruit_ads1x15.analog_in as AnalogIn from adafruit_ads1x15.ads1115 import ADS1115

i2c = busio.I2C(board.SCL, board.SDA) ads = ADS1115(i2c)

docs.title: Monitor 12V rail (via 1:4 voltage divider)
docs.text: channel_12v = AnalogIn(ads, ads.P0) v_12v = channel_12v.voltage * 4 # Scale back to 12V

docs.title: Monitor 5V rail
docs.text: channel_5v = AnalogIn(ads, ads.P1) v_5v = channel_5v.voltage

docs.title: Log periodically
docs.text:
if v_12v < 10.0 or v_5v < 4.8: logger.warning(f"Low voltage: 12V={v_12v}, 5V={v_5v}") ```

Data logged to audit_log: - Timestamp, 12V voltage, 5V voltage, state (vehicle_on/battery/fault) - Helps diagnose power delivery issues post-deployment

docs.title: Battery Health Monitoring (Phase 1+)
docs.text:
Smart BMS (Battery Management System): - Fuel gauge IC (TI bq27441) tracks SoC (State of Charge) - Communicates via I²C to RPi - Estimates time-to-empty, cycle count, temperature

Firmware action: - SoC < 5%: Disable non-critical systems (ALPR, lane detection) - SoC < 2%: Graceful shutdown (avoid data corruption) - Cycle count > 500: Alert maintenance (battery end-of-life)

docs.title: Design Margins & Safety Factors
docs.text: Parameter Target Safety Margin Rationale Max input voltage 16V → 20V TVS clamp Accounts for alternator overshoot Fuse rating 15A 1.5× safety factor Protects wiring before MOSFETs fail Capacitor ripple current 2A RMS → 5A cap rating Electrolytic derating (80% operational) Thermal junction limit 65°C → 125°C absolute max 2× safety margin Battery discharge cutoff 6.0V (3.0V per cell) → 2.8V absolute min Prevents LiPo battery damage Switchover latency <5 ms → <1 ms practical Prevents RPi brownout reboot

docs.title: Bill of Materials (Power Subsystem)
docs.text: Component Qty Cost Notes OBD-II Harness (Y-split) 1 $15 12V + CAN lines Buck Converter (12V→5V, 10A) 1 $25 Synchronous MOSFET type Battery UPS Hat (5V, 10 Ah) 1 $65 Li-ion 2S5P configuration Power Distribution Board 1 $15 Fuses, diodes, caps Heatsink (50×50 mm) 1 $8 Aluminum, self-adhesive Connectors + Cables — $8 Terminal blocks, Anderson connectors Thermal paste + mounting — $3 Transfer pad, hardware Testing/QA labor — $5 Power-on verification Subtotal (Power Subsystem) $144 ~30% of total node BOM

docs.title: Power-On Test
docs.text: bash 1. Connect 12V test supply (OBD-II harness simulation) 2. Measure 5V rail voltage: Expected 5.0V ± 0.5V 3. Measure 12V rail voltage: Expected 12V ± 1V (with some ripple) 4. No fuses blown: Check continuity of fuse holders 5. RPi boots to login: Indicates stable power, no brownouts 6. Battery UPS engaged: Monitor switches from buck to battery when 12V disconnected

docs.title: Battery Endurance Test
docs.text: bash 1. Charge battery to 100% SoC (confirm via fuel gauge, if installed) 2. Connect 5V load (RPi + modem simulation): 0.5A draw 3. Disconnect 12V source, run on battery for 4 hours 4. Measure battery SoC: Expected ≥50% (conservative, accounts for aging) 5. Measure RPi uptime: Expected no reboots, stable 5V ± 0.2V

DC-CM-REG-001 — AMENDMENT 1 D-CENTRAL GROUP | CivicMesh Platform B2B Commercial Vehicle Network Programme Document Registry Addendum — 6 New Documents

Amendment ID DC-CM-REG-001-AMD-001 Amends DC-CM-REG-001 CivicMesh Document Registry v1.0 Also Amends DC-MASTER-DOC-001 Master Documentation Registry v1.0 Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Documents Added 6 new B2B documents — DC-CM-B2B-001 through DC-CM-B2B-006 Registry Section New Section 4G added to Technical Platform Documentation tier Rationale Commercial Vehicle Network Programme identified as missing B2B tier. Rideshare (Uber/Lyft), car rental (Enterprise, Hertz), car share (Communauto, Zipcar), delivery (Amazon, UPS, FedEx, Purolator), and taxi operators form Tier 4 between city fleet (Tier 1–3) and civilian opt-in (Tier 5). docs.text:

Doc ID Title & Description Type Primary Audience Priority Status Notes SECTION 4G — Commercial Vehicle Network Programme (NEW — B2B Tier 4) SECTION 4G — Commercial Vehicle Network Programme (NEW — B2B Tier 4) SECTION 4G — Commercial Vehicle Network Programme (NEW — B2B Tier 4) SECTION 4G — Commercial Vehicle Network Programme (NEW — B2B Tier 4) SECTION 4G — Commercial Vehicle Network Programme (NEW — B2B Tier 4) SECTION 4G — Commercial Vehicle Network Programme (NEW — B2B Tier 4) SECTION 4G — Commercial Vehicle Network Programme (NEW — B2B Tier 4) DC-CM-B2B-001 CivicMesh Commercial Vehicle Network Programme — Master document. B2B Tier 4 sitting between city fleet (Tier 1–3) and civilian (Tier 5). Four agreement models: Fleet Owner (Enterprise, Communauto), Platform Driver (Uber/Lyft), Delivery Network (Amazon, UPS, FedEx), Taxi/Licensed. Revenue model, privacy architecture, cooperative alignment, network effect projection. Strategy All Audiences P2 🆕 New — created this session DC-CM-B2B-002 Fleet Operator Agreement Template — Model A. Enterprise, Hertz, Communauto, Zipcar, Turo. Company-level agreement covering node installation, 25% revenue share, insurance premium reduction facilitation, renter privacy (no renter identity ever collected), no adverse use covenant. Legal Fleet Operators P2 🆕 New DC-CM-B2B-003 Platform Driver Programme Specification — Model B. Uber Canada, Lyft Canada. Two-level structure: Platform API Agreement (trip status, driver DID mapping, revenue routing, insurance data feed) + individual Driver Opt-In Programme (voluntary, benefit-first framing). Anti-surveillance protections, driver rights cannot be waived by platform. Uber pitch strategy. Technical / Legal Uber / Lyft / Drivers P3 🆕 New — Year 2–3 go-to-market DC-CM-B2B-004 Delivery Network Agreement Template — Model C. Amazon Logistics, UPS, FedEx, Purolator. Route-zone data only (not address-level), digital twin premium data tier, last-mile liability documentation tool, higher city share (55%) reflecting digital twin value, no worker surveillance covenant binding on DSPs. Legal Delivery Operators P3 🆕 New DC-CM-B2B-005 Commercial Driver Verifiable Credential Schema — JSON-LD schema for platform-issued driver credentials. Platform DID (Uber Canada) issues to driver personal DID. Fields: platform, driverStatus, vehicleDIDs, revenueShareRecipient, insuranceDataShareConsent, tripContextConsent, citationRevenueOptIn, platformDriverId (hashed). Credential lifecycle: revocation on platform deactivation, multi-platform driver support. Technical Engineering / Platforms P2 🆕 SR&ED: novel VC schema DC-CM-B2B-006 Commercial Platform API Integration Specification — Integration layer architecture. Uber/Lyft: trip webhook receiver, context enrichment service, credential lifecycle manager, revenue attribution engine, scope enforcement gateway. Enterprise/fleet: vehicle roster sync, checkout events. Delivery: route assignment, zone completion events, liability documentation requests. SR&ED: trip context enrichment latency, multi-platform attribution, Polygon revocation latency. Technical Engineering P2 🆕 SR&ED: novel integration

DC-CM-REG-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Platform Document Registry | Version 1.0

Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Document ID DC-CM-REG-001 Version 1.0 — Initial release Date May 2026 Platform CivicMesh (TrafficMesh · CommunityShield · MeshPlate · MeshEar · MeshNav) Total Documents 54 new documents across 8 tiers Status Legend ✅ Complete 🔄 Updated/Needs Revision 📋 Not Yet Produced 🆕 New Relationship All documents below slot into DC-REG-001. Tiers mirror the master registry numbering.

docs.title: Part 1 — CivicMesh Platform Overview
docs.text:
CivicMesh is the federated civic safety operating system built on the TrafficMesh architecture. It generalises the distributed node enforcement model into five coordinated modules, each community-owned, privacy-preserving, open-source, and auditable. Private companies act as operators (MSSP/NOC/SOC), never as data owners. Government earns data access through formal partnership agreements with full chain of custody. Contributors — residents, property owners, fleet operators — are compensated for participation. The five modules and their analogues:

Module Replaces / Improves Core Capability Data Owner Revenue to Community SR&ED TrafficMesh NYC MTA ACE, Rekor, Flock (mobile layer) Mobile node fleet enforcement, digital twin Municipality / community Citation revenue share, insurance API ✅ Primary CommunityShield Ring Neighbors (with compensation) Privacy-preserving doorbell/camera network, NOC dashboard Community (local storage) Stipend, HOA fee credit, insurance discount ✅ Strong MeshPlate Flock Safety / Motorola Vigilant (municipal-owned) Community LPR, hash-before-store, no national private DB Community / municipality Investigation stipend, insurance feed revenue ✅ Novel arch MeshEar ShotSpotter / SoundThinking Community-owned acoustic detection, on-device only, community triage before dispatch Community (no audio retained) Private security cost reduction, dispatch efficiency ✅ AI/audio R&D MeshNav Waze (with superior 24/7 data) Navigation + road intelligence from node fleet, enforcement zone transparency, digital twin layer Community / open Insurance opt-in premium reduction, fleet premium tier ✅ Strong

Defines: platform fee structure, data ownership, SLA obligations, open-source licence compliance, termination and data return provisions. 📋 CivicMesh LEGAL-CM-011 Municipal Data Federation Agreement — Template agreement between CivicMesh-deployed community network and local municipality. Defines: data access scope, investigation-specific federation protocol, chain of custody obligations, audit rights, revenue share from enforcement citations, retention and deletion terms. 📋 CivicMesh / Legal LEGAL-CM-012 HOA / Condo Corporation Participation Agreement — Template for HOA or condo corp joining CivicMesh network. Covers: node installation requirements, resident consent process, data governance, fine revenue distribution, platform fee, exit terms. 📋 CivicMesh LEGAL-CM-013 Resident Node Owner Agreement — Individual resident agreement for connecting personal hardware (doorbell, dashcam, ALPR) to community CivicMesh network. Covers: opt-in consent, data minimisation obligations, compensation terms, liability waiver, data portability and exit rights. 📋 CivicMesh LEGAL-CM-014 Insurance Data Sharing Agreement — Template for insurance company subscriber to CivicMesh data products. Covers: aggregate anonymised data feed, consent-gated individual record API, incident evidence package retrieval, privacy obligations, prohibited uses (no re-identification, no immigration enforcement, no political profiling). 📋 CivicMesh / Finance LEGAL-CM-015 Contractor Node Kit Agreement — Agreement for city contractors receiving modular node kits as condition of city contract. Covers: kit custody, tamper-evident seal obligations, coverage area, data ownership (city, not contractor), kit return and data wipe on contract completion. 📋 TrafficMesh / Legal LEGAL-CM-016 Open Source Licence — CivicMesh Platform (AGPL v3 Community Edition). Dual-licence structure: AGPL community (free, copyleft, any modifications to deployed code must be open-sourced) and Commercial Edition (proprietary MSSP layer, insurance API, data marketplace). docs.title: Legal Defensibility Framework
docs.text: CivicMesh citations must meet Ontario Highway Traffic Act (HTA) evidentiary standards to be defensible in court.

docs.title: Timestamped Evidence
docs.text:
• Every incident must have exact timestamp (server clock, NTP synchronized)
  • Frame-level granularity: Which frame contained the violation?
  • No gaps: Ensure continuous recording when node is active

Implementation: - NTP sync every 12 hours (syncs server clock to national time standard) - Frame timestamps embedded in video metadata - GPS timestamp cross-check

docs.title: Geolocation & Spatial Anchoring
docs.text:
• GPS coordinates accurate to ±5m (sufficient for enforcement)
  • Geofence validation: Is this incident in the jurisdiction being enforced?
  • Road / street name logged (cross-reference with GIS)

Implementation: - GPS module: ±5m accuracy (Ublox Neo-M8N) - Reverse geocoding: Convert lat/lon to street address - Jurisdiction check: City boundary polygon (PostGIS)

docs.title: Encryption & Tamper-Evidence
docs.text:
• Evidence clips encrypted with city's public key (city controls decryption)
  • Hash chain: Evidence hash signed and immutable
  • Audit log: Every access to evidence logged (who viewed, when, why)

Implementation: - Encryption: AES-256-GCM (transport) + AES-256 at-rest - Signature: HMAC-SHA256 on evidence hash - Audit: PostgreSQL audit logs with timestamp + user ID

docs.title: Chain of Custody Log
docs.text: Incident ABC-123: ├─ 14:23:15 — Detected by node "bus_001" at location (45.4215, -75.6972) ├─ 14:23:45 — Uploaded to cloud ├─ 14:23:47 — Deduplication: No match; new incident ├─ 14:23:48 — Routed to review queue ├─ 14:25:12 — Officer "Smith" reviewed (session ID: xyz) ├─ 14:25:35 — Officer approved + digital signature applied ├─ 14:25:37 — NOC generated ├─ 14:25:40 — Sent to MTO └─ (Searchable by: plate, officer, timestamp, location)

docs.title: Human Review Before Citation
docs.text:
Non-negotiable requirement: Every citation must have officer sign-off.

Why this matters: Fixed photo radar (now banned in Ontario) failed partly because it was fully automated. No human discretion. Public perception: automated money grab.

CivicMesh approach: Officer reviews every incident (even fast-track) before citation issuance.

Fast-track process (high confidence): 1. Officer sees summary: "Speeding: 65 km/h in 50 km/h zone" 2. Officer reviews: ~20 seconds 3. Officer approves: Click button, signature added 4. NOC issued

Result: Defensible in court. "I reviewed the evidence and approved the citation."

docs.title: Speeding
docs.text:
• HTA Section: s. 128(1) (exceeding posted limit)
  • Evidence: OBD speed + GPS location + posted speed limit (geofence)
  • Defensibility: Very high (OBD is ground truth; hard to challenge)
  • Fine: Varies by amount over limit (~$110–500 for 20+ km/h over)

docs.title: Stop Sign / Traffic Signal Violation
docs.text:
• HTA Section: s. 136(1) (disobey traffic control signal)
  • Evidence: Signal state (red) + vehicle motion (optical flow)
  • Defensibility: High (signal photo + motion evidence)
  • Fine: ~$200–500

docs.title: Lane Violations
docs.text:
• HTA Section: s. 142(1) (improper lane change)
  • Evidence: Lane segmentation CV + vehicle heading (OBD)
  • Defensibility: Medium (CV can have false positives; human review helps)
  • Fine: ~$110–200

docs.title: Improper Parking (Accessible, Snow Route, Bus Lane)
docs.text:
• HTA Section: s. 217 (regulation parking)
  • Evidence: ALPR + location + MTO/bylaw DB lookup
  • Defensibility: Very high (DB lookup is authoritative)
  • Fine: ~$150–300

docs.title: Mandatory Fields
docs.text:
1. Vehicle identification: Plate + registered owner name/address (from MTO lookup)
  2. Violation description: Type, statute section, posted limit/rule
  3. Date, time, location: Exact (street name + GPS coords)
  4. Evidence photo: Plate crop (optional but helpful)
  5. Officer info: Name, badge number, signature (digital signature + timestamp)
  6. Court/payment info: Where to pay, where to dispute
  7. Right to appeal: Driver has right to trial (standard HTA language)

docs.title: Example NOC
docs.text:
``` NOTICE OF CONTRAVENTION Ticket #: TK-2026-001234 Date Issued: June 1, 2026 at 14:25:37 EST

Registered Vehicle Owner: John Doe 123 Main St, Ottawa, ON K1A 0A1

Vehicle: Plate: ABC1234 Make: Toyota Corolla Year: 2022

Violation: Speeding Highway Traffic Act s. 128(1) Posted Limit: 50 km/h Recorded Speed: 65 km/h Location: King St @ Bay St, Ottawa Date/Time: June 1, 2026 at 14:23:15 EST

Evidence: Electronic enforcement camera Dashcam photo: [embedded] OBD telemetry: [hash reference]

Officer: Officer Smith (Badge #1234) Signature: [digital signature] Timestamp: June 1, 2026 at 14:25:37 EST

Fine: $110

To Pay or Dispute: Online: ontario.ca/pay-ticket By Mail: See reverse side In Court: [courthouse address]

You have the right to dispute this ticket in court. ```

docs.title: Prosecution Strategy
docs.text: When a driver disputes a citation, Crown must prove: 1. Vehicle identification: Plate clearly read (ALPR accuracy) 2. Date/time: Exact (timestamp evidence) 3. Location: Correct jurisdiction (GPS accuracy, geofence) 4. Violation: Evidence supports it (speed, signal state, lane, parking) 5. Officer review: Officer reviewed and approved (digital signature, audit log)

docs.title: Defense Strategy (Driver's Challenges)
docs.text:
• Plate error: "That wasn't my car" (color, model mismatch in evidence photo)
  • Speed error: "My speedometer read 55 km/h, not 65" (rare; OBD is hard to challenge)
  • Signal state: "Light was yellow, not red" (video evidence rebuts this)
  • Location error: "That wasn't King St @ Bay; it was different intersection" (GPS, street sign evidence)

docs.title: CivicMesh Defensibility Strengths
docs.text:
• OBD ground truth: Vehicle speed is direct measurement, not inference
  • GPS anchoring: Location is verified 5+ times per incident
  • Video evidence: Dashcam provides context (lane, signal, surroundings)
  • Officer review: Human sign-off removes "fully automated" criticism
  • Audit trail: Every action logged, timestamped, signed

docs.title: Potential Weaknesses (Mitigation)
docs.text:
• CV model errors: Lane detection can misfire on worn markings
  • Mitigation: High confidence threshold (95%+), officer must watch video, weather-aware thresholding
  • Light vs. shadow: False signal detection in direct sunlight
  • Mitigation: Calibrate detector; require 5+ consecutive frames of same state
  • Geofence misconfiguration: Speed limit zone boundary wrong
  • Mitigation: City GIS is authoritative; audit trail shows which geofence was active

docs.title: City Authority
docs.text:
• Traffic enforcement is delegated to municipalities under HTA
  • City can authorize bylaw officers to issue tickets
  • City can authorize electronic enforcement (now legally proven by photo radar precedent)

docs.title: Provincial Oversight
docs.text:
• MTO maintains vehicle registration database
  • Courts handle disputes
  • Highway Traffic Act defines limits and violations

docs.title: CivicMesh's Role
docs.text:
• Platform is tool; city has authority
  • City owns evidence and retains all data
  • City issues citations (through authorized officers)
  • CivicMesh provides operations support (MSSP), not legal authority

docs.title: Personal Data Minimization
docs.text:
• Collected: Plate (vehicle), timestamp, location, violation type
  • NOT collected: Driver identity (unless incident goes to court), route history, speed history
  • Deleted: Raw video frames deleted after metadata extraction; only metadata persists

docs.title: Consent (Civilian Opt-In)
docs.text:
• Explicit opt-in required for civilian participation
  • Annual consent renewal (or per-incident)
  • Right to withdraw at any time

docs.title: Data Retention
docs.text:
• Incident records: 3 years (statute of limitations)
  • Audit logs: 7 years (regulatory retention)
  • Raw frames: Deleted immediately after metadata extraction (privacy)

docs.title: Third-Party Sharing
docs.text:
• Insurance API: Anonymized aggregate only (no individual driver names)
  • Urban planners: Anonymized pedestrian/cyclist counts (no re-ID)
  • Police: Only for stolen vehicles / expired plates (separate queue)

docs.title: PIPEDA Assessment
docs.text:
• Fair information principles: Collect only what's needed for enforcement ✓
  • Accuracy: Data verified (ALPR confidence, GPS accuracy) ✓
  • Consent: Explicit for civilians, implicit for city vehicles ✓
  • Openness: Policy published (what data collected, how long kept) ✓
  • Individual access: Driver can request their own data ✓
  • Accountability: Audit trail proves no misuse ✓

docs.title: City's Responsibility
docs.text:
• Ensure geofences are accurate (correct speed limit zones)
  • Ensure officers are properly trained
  • Maintain audit logs and respond to FOI requests
  • Defend citations in court

docs.title: CivicMesh's Responsibility
docs.text:
• Ensure evidence is collected per chain-of-custody standards
  • Ensure officer portal enforces human review
  • Provide audit trail and compliance reporting
  • Defend technical integrity in court (if expert witness needed)

docs.title: Annual Compliance Audit
docs.text:
1. Evidence integrity: Sample 100 random incidents; verify chain of custody
  2. False positive rate: Officer rejection rate; investigate systemic issues
  3. False negative rate: Any violations missed? (harder to measure)
  4. Privacy: Verify data deletion; check for unauthorized access
  5. Geofence accuracy: Compare posted limits to city GIS; flag mismatches

docs.title: NYC MTA ACE Program (Favorable)
docs.text:
• Bus-mounted cameras issuing citations for bus lane obstruction
  • Courts upheld as valid enforcement (electronic evidence + officer review)
  • Generated $225M in revenue (2024)

docs.title: Ontario Speed Camera Ban (Unfavorable for Fixed Cameras)
docs.text:
• Ford government banned ASE (automated speed enforcement) due to perception of revenue grab
  • Key difference: Photo radar was fully automated, no human review, perceived as money grab
  • CivicMesh mitigates by: human review, community dividend (civilian revenue share), transparency

docs.title: Red Light Cameras (Precedent)
docs.text:
• Legal in Ontario; courts accept dashcam evidence
  • Operator (city/vendor) must prove:
  • Signal was red
  • Vehicle crossed before signal turned green
  • No mechanical failure in camera
  • CivicMesh exceeds this standard (OBD ground truth for speed; dashcam for context)

docs.title: Recommended Practices
docs.text:
1. Retain raw evidence: Keep dashcam clip + OBD data for 7 years (legal hold)
  2. Publish transparency reports: Monthly enforcement stats (violations by type, locations, revenue)
  3. Publish appeal process: Easy for drivers to dispute (shows fairness)
  4. Officer training: Certification program (officers know HTA + CivicMesh portal)
  5. Technology audit: Annual third-party audit of evidence integrity
  6. Public advisory: Notify residents of enforcement areas before launch

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
CivicMesh is legally defensible if you follow these principles: - Human review before citation (no fully automated enforcement) - Strong chain of custody (timestamps, signatures, encryption) - Transparent process (publish policies, appeal options, revenue splits) - Community benefit (revenue sharing reduces "money grab" perception)

With these safeguards, CivicMesh citations will hold up in court and avoid political backlash that killed photo radar.

docs.title: LEGAL-001 D-Central Group Articles of Incorporation v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. Articles of Incorporation — Federal CCPC | Canada Business Corporations Act

Document ID LEGAL-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — for legal counsel review before filing Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Filing Authority Corporations Canada — canadabusiness.ca (online) Filing Cost $200 CAD (federal online) Related Documents LEGAL-002

| Executive Summary D-Central Group Inc. is the federal holding company for the entire D-Central ecosystem. Incorporated under the Canada Business Corporations Act (CBCA) as a Canadian Controlled Private Corporation (CCPC). CCPC status is a non-negotiable prerequisite for refundable SR&ED tax credits, NRC IRAP grants, Mitacs programmes, and ISC awards. The holding company owns all intellectual property across the ecosystem, executes inter-company licensing to operating subsidiaries, and serves as the contracting entity for investor agreements. It does not conduct direct operations — those are performed by operating subsidiaries. | |---|

docs.title: 1. CCPC Qualification Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Status Consequence of Breach Incorporated in Canada Required — CBCA federal incorporation satisfies Loss of CCPC status — all SR&ED credits become non-refundable Not a public corporation No public share listing at any time Loss of refundable SR&ED credits Not controlled by non-residents 50%+ voting shares held by Canadian residents at all times Loss of CCPC + SR&ED refundability — material financial impact Not controlled by public corporations No public company holds 50%+ of voting shares Loss of CCPC status Action: Before issuing any shares to non-Canadian investors, obtain a legal opinion confirming CCPC status is preserved. Loss of CCPC status converts SR&ED credits from refundable to non-refundable — a direct cash flow impact in the hundreds of thousands.

docs.title: 2. Share Structure
docs.text: Share Class Authorized Voting Dividends Purpose Class A Common Unlimited Yes — 1 vote/share Discretionary Founder shares — Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Class B Common Unlimited Yes — 1 vote/share Discretionary Future employees, advisors, Canadian angels Class C Preferred Unlimited No (except fundamental changes) Cumulative preferred — rate at issuance Institutional investors — anti-dilution + liquidation preference per LEGAL-002 Class D Non-Voting Unlimited No Discretionary Cooperative member economic participation certificates

docs.title: 3. Registered Office and Directors
docs.text: Registered Office Ottawa, Ontario, Canada (specific address confirmed at filing — must be Canadian) Initial Director Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste (sole director at incorporation) Director Residency CBCA requires 25% of directors to be Canadian residents. As sole director this is satisfied. Financial Year End December 31 — aligns with SR&ED claim cycle and government reporting

docs.title: 4. Corporate Objects (Broad Clause)
docs.text:
• To develop, license, and commercialize open-source software, hardware, and technology platforms for community safety, civic infrastructure, distributed connectivity, and cooperative economic systems;
  • To hold, license, and enforce intellectual property including patents, trademarks, copyrights, and trade secrets;
  • To enter into joint ventures, partnerships, and franchise agreements with cooperative entities, municipalities, and private sector partners;
  • To carry on any other lawful business in Canada or elsewhere.

docs.title: 5. Day-1 Filing Checklist
docs.text: Step Action Timing 1 NUANS search — confirm 'D-Central Group Inc.' is available Day 1 2 File Articles of Incorporation at canadabusiness.ca — $200 fee Day 1 3 Receive Certificate of Incorporation (1–5 business days) Day 1–5 4 Hold organizational meeting — appoint officers, issue founder shares, adopt bylaws Day 5–10 5 Open dedicated business bank account (RBC/TD recommended for IRAP direct deposit) Day 5–10 6 Apply for Business Number (BN) and GST/HST through CRA My Business Account Day 10 7 Apply for Federal Employer Account number for payroll (required before first salary draw) Day 10 8 Execute Unanimous Shareholder Agreement (LEGAL-002) and IP Assignment (LEGAL-003) Day 10–15 9 File trademark applications — D-Central, CivicMesh, TrafficMesh, MeshNav, MeshPlate, MeshEar, CommunityShield (LEGAL-004) Week 3 10 Set up dedicated R&D bank account — all SR&ED-eligible expenses flow through this account Day 10 11 Set up GitHub organization, Linear workspace, SR&ED ledger (SRED-CM-001) — Day 1, non-negotiable Day 1

docs.title: 6. Document Control
docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. Distribution requires written authorization from Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste.

docs.title: LEGAL-002 Unanimous Shareholder Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. Unanimous Shareholder Agreement — Term Sheet & Clause Inventory

Document ID LEGAL-002 Version 1.0 — Term Sheet Status Draft — legal counsel to draft full agreement Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Document Type Term sheet only — not a signed legal agreement Parties D-Central Group Inc. and all shareholders (initially: Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste) Related Documents LEGAL-001

| Executive Summary A Unanimous Shareholder Agreement (USA) is a contract among all shareholders that restricts, in whole or in part, the powers of the directors to manage the business and affairs of the corporation (CBCA s.146). For a founder-led startup, the USA is the foundational governance document covering founder vesting, board composition, reserved matters, transfer restrictions, anti-dilution, and mission lock provisions that protect the cooperative and open source commitments from investor pressure. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Founder Share Vesting
docs.text: Founder Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Shares Class A Common — [10,000,000 shares at $0.0001 par value recommended] Vesting Schedule 4-year: 25% cliff vest at Month 12 from incorporation. Remaining 75% monthly over 36 months (1/48th per month). Acceleration Single trigger: 100% on involuntary termination post-change of control. Double trigger: voluntary termination within 12 months post-CoC. Reverse Vesting Company right of repurchase at $0.0001 for unvested shares. Repurchase right lapses as shares vest. IP Covenant Founder executes LEGAL-003 simultaneously. All pre- and post-incorporation IP assigned to D-Central Group Inc.

docs.title: 2. Board Composition
docs.text: Initial Board Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste — sole director On First Institutional Round Expands to 3: 1 founder + 1 investor nominee + 1 independent (mutually agreed) On Series A Expands to 5: 2 founder/management + 2 investor nominees + 1 independent Founder Control Period Founder retains casting vote + board majority until earlier of: (a) Series A close, or (b) founder holds less than 20% of total voting shares Observer Rights Seed investors holding 5%+ receive non-voting board observer rights

docs.title: 3. Shareholder Reserved Matters
docs.text: Reserved Matter Threshold Amendment of articles or USA 75% of voting shares Issuance of new shares (outside approved option pool) Majority of voting shares Sale, merger, or change of control 75% of voting shares Voluntary dissolution 75% of voting shares Incurrence of debt exceeding $500,000 CAD in any 12-month period Majority of voting shares Any related-party transaction exceeding $50,000 CAD Majority — founder recuses Any action that would cause loss of CCPC status 75% — requires prior legal opinion Abandonment of open source licensing model for any D-Central platform 75% of voting shares Any data sharing agreement that contravenes CivicMesh privacy architecture 75% of voting shares

docs.title: 4. Transfer Restrictions
docs.text: Right of First Refusal Selling shareholder must offer shares to existing shareholders pro-rata at proposed price. 30-day exercise window. Board Approval No transfer without board approval. Board withholds approval if CCPC status at risk. Drag-Along 75%+ approving a sale drags remaining shareholders on same terms. Tag-Along If founder sells 10%+ of their shares, minority shareholders may participate pro-rata on same terms. Lock-Up Founder shares subject to 12-month lock-up from any equity financing round.

docs.title: 5. Mission Lock Provisions
docs.text: The following cannot be changed without 75% shareholder approval — these provisions survive any change of control: - Open source licensing of CivicMesh platform core (AGPL v3) cannot be revoked or changed to proprietary — requires 75% approval + 12-month public notice period; - Cooperative structure of CCSC, CTC, MSSP Cooperative, and Guard Cooperative cannot be dissolved or converted; - Privacy architecture principles (no national private database, hash-before-store ALPR, on-device audio processing, community data ownership) cannot be abandoned; - D-Credit cannot be listed on a speculative exchange or structured as a security without 75% approval and securities law compliance; - Any acquirer must agree to be bound by these mission lock provisions as a condition of shareholder approval of any acquisition.

docs.title: 6. Investor Information Rights
docs.text: Right Threshold Timing Monthly management accounts (unaudited) 5%+ of any class Within 30 days of month end Annual audited financial statements All shareholders Within 90 days of fiscal year end Notice of material events (litigation, regulatory action, loss of key contract, government funding change) All shareholders Within 5 business days Access to SR&ED and IRAP records 10%+ Within 10 business days of request

docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. Distribution requires written authorization from Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste.

docs.title: LEGAL-003 IP Assignment Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. Intellectual Property Assignment Agreement

Document ID LEGAL-003 Version 1.0 — Term Sheet Status Draft — execute simultaneously with LEGAL-002 Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Assignor Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste (Founder) Assignee D-Central Group Inc. (upon incorporation) Consideration $1.00 CAD and issuance of Class A Common Shares per LEGAL-001 Related Documents LEGAL-001

| Executive Summary All IP created before incorporation — architecture documents, code, design documents, business plans, training materials, the DC-REG-001 master registry, and all related works — must be formally assigned to the corporation for the corporation to own it. Without this assignment, IP belongs to the founder personally, creating problems for investor due diligence, SR&ED claims (which require corporate ownership of R&D results), and future licensing. This assignment must be executed and dated BEFORE R&D commences. Backdated assignments are not recognized by CRA for SR&ED purposes. | |---|

docs.title: 1. IP Inventory — Pre-Incorporation Works
docs.text: IP Category Description Platform Architecture Documents DC-TM-BIZ-001, DC-CM-BIZ-001, DC-CM-REG-001, DC-MASTER-DOC-001, DC-STRAT-001 through DC-STRAT-005, and all documents listed in DC-MASTER-DOC-001 Software Code All code for CivicMesh platform components: officer review portal (React/FastAPI), ingestion API, evidence pipeline, digital twin prototype, and any other code written before incorporation System Architecture All technical architecture diagrams, API specifications, data schemas, and system design documents Brand and Identity D-Central Group, CivicMesh, TrafficMesh, MeshNav, MeshPlate, MeshEar, CommunityShield names and marks — pending trademark registration LEGAL-004 Business Plans and Financial Models All financial projections, unit economics models, investor materials, and business plans Training Curriculum All CivicMesh Training Institute curriculum outlines, assessment frameworks, and course materials DID/VC Architecture DID method selection analysis, VC schema designs, on-chain anchoring specification, trust registry architecture Hardware Designs Open hardware node reference design specifications, component selection, enclosure design documents Research and Know-How All competitive analysis, regulatory analysis, and technical decision logs created during ecosystem development

docs.title: 2. Future IP Assignment
docs.text: All IP created after incorporation that relates to D-Central Group's business is automatically and immediately assigned to D-Central Group Inc. upon creation. Includes: - All code written for any D-Central platform or product; - All inventions, discoveries, or innovations related to the D-Central business; - All training materials and curriculum created for the Training Institute; - All business development materials, pitch decks, financial models, and strategic documents; - All improvements to any IP listed in Section 1. Carve-out: IP created entirely outside working hours, using no company resources, and wholly unrelated to the D-Central business is not subject to future assignment.

docs.title: 3. Open Source Licence Compatibility
docs.text: AGPL v3 Code Code published under AGPL v3 before incorporation remains under AGPL v3 after assignment. D-Central Group Inc. becomes the AGPL licence holder and may enforce copyleft obligations. CERN-OHL-S v2 Hardware Open hardware designs published under CERN-OHL-S v2 remain under that licence after assignment. D-Central Group Inc. becomes the licence holder. Dual-Licence Right D-Central Group Inc. retains the right to issue commercial licences under the dual-licence structure (Community Edition AGPL + Commercial Edition proprietary) as specified in LEGAL-CM-016.

docs.title: 4. SR&ED Implications
docs.text: SR&ED Filing Entity D-Central Group Inc. or designated operating subsidiary IP Ownership Requirement Corporation must own or have right to exploit SR&ED results at time of claim. This assignment satisfies that requirement. Intercompany Licensing Operating subsidiaries conducting R&D must have a written licence from D-Central Group Inc. to exploit the assigned IP before any SR&ED-eligible R&D commences. CRA Audit Readiness This agreement must be executed and dated before R&D commences. Backdated assignments are not recognized by CRA.

docs.title: LEGAL-004 Brand Trademark Filing Strategy v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-004 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group Inc. Brand & Trademark Filing Strategy

Document ID LEGAL-004 Version 1.0 Status Draft — file within 30 days of incorporation Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Filing Authority Canadian Intellectual Property Office (CIPO) — cipo.ic.gc.ca Cost $458 CAD per mark per class (online) + agent fees Priority Window File Canada first. Paris Convention gives 12 months to claim Canadian priority date in US/international. Related Documents LEGAL-001

| Executive Summary Trademark registration protects the D-Central brand family against third-party use. Filing immediately after incorporation — before public launch — establishes the earliest possible priority date. The Canadian filing starts the 12-month Paris Convention window for US and international applications. A cleared NUANS name search (required for incorporation) does not guarantee trademark clearance — separate trademark searches are required for each mark. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Marks to File — Priority Order
docs.text: Mark Type Nice Classes Priority Rationale D-CENTRAL GROUP Word mark 35, 42, 45 P1 — At incorporation Parent brand — protects group identity across all activities CIVICMESH Word mark 9, 35, 42, 45 P1 — At incorporation Flagship platform — most commercially exposed mark TRAFFICMESH Word mark 9, 35, 42 P1 — At incorporation First product to market — OC Transpo pitch requires clean ownership MESHNAV Word mark 9, 42 P2 — Month 1 Navigation product — file before any public beta MESHPLATE Word mark 9, 42, 45 P2 — Month 1 LPR product mark MESHEAR Word mark 9, 42, 45 P2 — Month 1 Acoustic detection mark COMMUNITYSHIELD Word mark 9, 35, 42, 45 P2 — Month 1 Community camera product — file before HOA deployments D-CREDIT Word mark 35, 36 P3 — Q1 Cooperative currency — file before any public programme

docs.title: 2. Nice Classification Summary
docs.text: Class 9 Computer software, AI software, data processing software, mobile applications, electronic surveillance equipment, edge computing hardware, GPS devices, dashcam systems Class 35 Business services, data analysis, managed services (MSSP), cooperative organization management, traffic and road condition information services Class 36 Financial services, cooperative banking, community currency services (D-Credit), credit union services Class 42 SaaS, PaaS, cybersecurity services, AI research and development, cloud computing, technical security services Class 45 Community safety services, traffic enforcement services, security monitoring, community alert services

docs.title: 3. Domain Name Security — Register Immediately
docs.text: Domain Priority Action dcentralgroup.ca / dcentralgroup.com P1 Register immediately — use .ca as primary, .com as redirect civicmesh.ca / civicmesh.com P1 Register immediately trafficmesh.ca / trafficmesh.com P1 Register immediately meshnav.ca / meshnav.com P2 Register within Month 1 meshplate.ca / meshplate.com P2 Register within Month 1 meshear.ca / meshear.com P2 Register within Month 1 communityshield.ca / communityshield.com P2 Register within Month 1 dcredit.ca / dcredit.com P3 Register before D-Credit programme launch

docs.title: 2. Intercompany Agreements Required
docs.text: Agreement Parties Purpose Priority IP Licence D-Central Group Inc. → CivicMesh Inc. Licence CivicMesh platform IP for exploitation. Royalty rate: TBD with tax counsel — transfer pricing rules apply. P1 — before SR&ED filing IP Sublicence CivicMesh Inc. → TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. Sublicence TrafficMesh-specific IP. Royalty rate: TBD. P1 IP Sublicence CivicMesh Inc. → CommunityShield Inc. Sublicence CommunityShield-specific IP. P2 Management Services Agreement D-Central Group Inc. → all subsidiaries Shared services (finance, legal, HR) at arm's-length fee. Required for inter-company cost allocation. P2 Platform Services Agreement CivicMesh Inc. → D-Central Shield Inc. MSSP platform access and technical ops support. Defines platform fee, SLA, data governance. P2 Cooperative Partnership Agreement D-Central Group Inc. ↔ CCSC Data governance, revenue share distribution, and cooperative governance interface. P2

docs.title: 3. Transfer Pricing Note
docs.text: As the entity structure grows, intercompany transactions (IP licences, management fees, data fees) are subject to Canada's transfer pricing rules (Income Tax Act s.247). All intercompany transactions must be priced at arm's-length rates. Transfer pricing documentation must be prepared annually. Engage tax counsel with transfer pricing expertise before the first institutional investment round creates valuation benchmarks.

docs.title: 4. Key Tax Considerations
docs.text: Small Business Deduction All CCPCs eligible for 9% federal rate on active business income up to $500,000 (vs. 15% general rate). Preserves cash flow in early growth. SR&ED Credits Refundable SR&ED credits accrue to the CCPC that conducts the R&D and owns (or has sublicence rights to exploit) the results. IP licences must be in place before R&D commences in any subsidiary. Cooperative Patronage Patronage payments from cooperatives to members are deductible by the cooperative and income to members. Must be structured as patronage dividends under the Income Tax Act. D-Credit Classification D-Credit must be structured to avoid classification as a security (OSC) or as property income taxable on issuance. Tax and securities counsel required before D-Credit launches.

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-001 CivicMesh Inc Articles v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. Articles of Incorporation — Federal CCPC

Document ID LEGAL-CM-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — file after LEGAL-001 confirmed Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Parent D-Central Group Inc. (100% owner) Purpose Platform IP holding, MSSP franchise management, open source governance, data marketplace, insurance API Related Documents LEGAL-001

| Executive Summary CivicMesh Inc. is the primary operating subsidiary for the CivicMesh platform. It holds CivicMesh-specific IP (licenced from D-Central Group Inc.), issues MSSP franchise agreements, governs the open source dual-licence structure, and operates the data marketplace and insurance API. It is a wholly-owned CCPC subsidiary, ensuring SR&ED eligibility for platform R&D conducted within the entity. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Entity Scope and Revenue Streams
docs.text: Platform IP Held CivicMesh platform architecture, federation protocol, privacy architecture, DID/VC schema registry, NOC dashboard, all five module codebases Revenue Streams MSSP platform fees, data marketplace subscriptions (insurance API, municipal data), open source commercial licences, training cooperative revenue share Does NOT Operate Direct municipal enforcement (TrafficMesh Technologies Inc.), community camera deployments (CommunityShield Inc.), or security response (D-Central Guard Inc.) Governance Wholly owned by D-Central Group Inc. Mission lock provisions mirror LEGAL-002.

docs.title: 2. SR&ED Activities
docs.text:
• Federation protocol design — novel privacy-preserving upward data federation architecture
  • DID/VC credential schema development — W3C standard implementation for multi-entity ecosystem
  • On-chain anchoring — Polygon-based credential registry and revocation system (StatusList2021)
  • Privacy architecture — hash-before-store ALPR, on-device face blurring pipeline, consent management engine
  • Insurance data product development — novel actuarial data pipeline from community safety infrastructure

docs.title: 3. Key Contracts Executed by CivicMesh Inc.
docs.text: Contract Counterparty Document Reference MSSP Partner Agreement Certified MSSP operators LEGAL-CM-010 Municipal Data Federation Agreement City of Ottawa, OC Transpo, other municipalities LEGAL-CM-011 HOA / Condo Participation Agreement HOAs, condo corps, campuses LEGAL-CM-012 Insurance Data Sharing Agreement Intact, Desjardins, Aviva, other insurers LEGAL-CM-014 Open Source Licence (AGPL v3 Community Edition) Open source community — self-executing LEGAL-CM-016 Commercial Licence (CivicMesh Commercial Edition) Paying MSSP operators — included in MSSP Agreement LEGAL-CM-016 Cooperative Partnership Agreement CCSC, CTC, MSSP Cooperative LEGAL-005 IP Sublicence to TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. LEGAL-005 IP Sublicence to CommunityShield Inc. CommunityShield Inc. LEGAL-005 Contractor Node Kit Agreement City contractors receiving modular kits LEGAL-CM-015

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-010 MSSP Partner Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-010 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. MSSP Partner Agreement — Master Franchise Agreement Term Sheet

Document ID LEGAL-CM-010 Version 1.0 — Term Sheet Status Draft — legal counsel to draft full agreement Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Franchisor CivicMesh Inc. Franchisee [Certified MSSP Partner — name at execution] Agreement Type Master franchise — MSSP partner operates CivicMesh for end clients within defined territory Related Documents LEGAL-CM-011

| Executive Summary The MSSP Partner Agreement is the master franchise agreement between CivicMesh Inc. and certified MSSP operators. The MSSP is the client-facing operator for municipalities, HOAs, and campuses. CivicMesh Inc. is the technical infrastructure and IP provider. This agreement defines platform fees, operator obligations, data ownership rules, SLA requirements, open source compliance, and termination provisions. D-Central Shield Inc. holds the first executed agreement as founding MSSP operator. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Grant of Rights
docs.text: Licence Granted Non-exclusive, non-transferable licence to deploy, operate, and market the CivicMesh Commercial Edition within the Authorized Territory, for the purpose of providing managed services to end clients Authorized Territory Specific geographic territory defined at execution. Non-exclusive unless Gold tier certification achieved and territory performance targets met. Authorized Modules Specific CivicMesh modules authorized for this MSSP — listed at execution Sublicence Prohibited MSSP may not sublicence the platform. End client agreements are service agreements, not sublicences. Term Initial 2-year term, renewable annually with 90-day advance notice of non-renewal

docs.title: 2. Fee Structure
docs.text: Fee Type Amount Timing Notes Base Platform Fee $[TBD]/month per MSSP Monthly in advance Covers platform access, NOC tooling, security ops support, model updates Per-Node Fee $[TBD]/active node/month Monthly, prior month count Scales automatically with deployment size Per-Citation Fee $[TBD]/validated citation Monthly, prior month volume Aligns CivicMesh revenue with MSSP enforcement success Data Marketplace Rev Share [X]% of data product revenue from MSSP-managed communities Monthly Insurer API, municipal data subscriptions Certification Fee $[TBD]/staff member — one-time At certification Training Institute Track 2 Annual Audit Fee $[TBD] Annual Covers annual MSSP certification audit

docs.title: 3. MSSP Obligations
docs.text: Category Obligations Certification Maintain CivicMesh MSSP Gold certification. Minimum 2 certified MSSP Operator Track staff per deployment. Annual recertification. Infrastructure SLA 99.9% uptime for evidence pipeline and officer review portal. NOC staffed 24/7. Incident response within 1 hour for P1 events. Security SOC 2 Type II maintained. Cyber liability insurance minimum $5M CAD. E&O insurance minimum $5M CAD. Annual penetration test by approved firm. Data Governance No data sharing beyond what is authorized in Municipal Data Federation Agreement (LEGAL-CM-011). No data broker integration ever. Full audit log maintained. Community transparency reports generated and published annually. Open Source Compliance Any modifications to AGPL v3 Community Edition code must be published under AGPL v3. No proprietary forks. Full compliance with LEGAL-CM-016. Privacy PIPEDA compliance maintained. Privacy Impact Assessment conducted for each new deployment. Breach notification to CivicMesh Inc. within 24 hours of suspected breach. Cooperative Integration Facilitate CCSC member onboarding for civilian opt-in participants. Coordinate patronage distribution reporting. Do not interfere with cooperative governance.

docs.title: 4. Data Ownership
docs.text: Evidence Package Data Owned by the community (municipality or HOA/condo) whose nodes generated it. MSSP has operational access only. Derived Analytics Aggregate, anonymised analytics derived from evidence data are owned by CivicMesh Inc. for the data marketplace. MSSP receives a revenue share. MSSP Business Data Client lists, commercial terms, and MSSP-specific business data are owned by the MSSP. On Termination MSSP must return or destroy all CivicMesh proprietary data within 30 days. Evidence data is transferred to the community owner or CivicMesh Inc. as custodian.

docs.title: 5. Termination
docs.text: By CivicMesh Inc. (with cause — immediate) Material breach of data governance; loss of MSSP certification; insolvency; unauthorized sublicence; breach of open source obligations By CivicMesh Inc. (without cause) 90 days written notice. MSSP receives 30-day wind-down period to transition end clients. By MSSP 90 days written notice. End client transition plan required. Client Continuity Obligation On termination for any reason, MSSP must cooperate in transitioning all end client relationships. No disruption to municipal enforcement operations permitted. Credential Revocation CivicMesh Inc. revokes MSSP Operator Credential from trust registry within 24 hours of effective termination date.
