docs.title: LEGAL-CM-011 Municipal Data Federation Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-011 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. Municipal Data Federation Agreement — Template

Document ID LEGAL-CM-011 Version 1.0 — Template Status Draft — customize per municipality Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Parties CivicMesh Inc. / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. (Platform Operator) and [Municipality Name] (Municipal Partner) Agreement Type Data sharing and federation agreement — not a surveillance contract Related Documents LEGAL-CM-010

| Executive Summary This agreement governs the terms under which a municipality accesses data from CivicMesh-deployed community networks. It is not a procurement contract for surveillance — it is a data federation agreement in which the municipality receives access to community-generated data under defined legal constraints, with full auditability. The municipality's role is as a data recipient and enforcement authority, not as a data owner or platform controller. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Scope of Federation
docs.text: Data Federated Automatically Validated evidence packages (clip + GPS + timestamp + violation type + confidence + node credential chain) for citations within municipal jurisdiction. Aggregate road condition data from digital twin. Infrastructure defect alerts for Public Works. Data NOT Federated (without specific request) Raw continuous video footage. Civilian opt-in node owner identities. Private vehicle movement patterns. Data from communities that have not consented to municipal federation. Acoustic audio clips (MeshEar) — investigation-specific request only. Human Review Mandate No enforcement action on any evidence package without confirmed officer review and DID signature. Platform enforces this technically — citation issuance is blocked without officer signature.

docs.title: 2. Revenue Share Framework
docs.text: Citation Source Municipality Platform Operator Community (CCSC) OC Transpo / City fleet nodes 60–70% 20–25% 10–15% to node owner patronage via CCSC Civilian opt-in nodes 50% 20% 30% — higher share to incentivize civilian participation Contractor node citations 65% 25% 10% to CCSC general fund Digital twin data subscriptions 20% — city data royalty 60% 20% to CCSC data fund

docs.title: 3. Investigation-Specific Federation Protocol
docs.text:
• Requesting officer submits formal request through Officer Portal with: case number, legal authority (warrant or reasonable grounds documentation), data sought, and time window.
  • Platform verifies officer DID credential — confirming authorized jurisdiction and active Municipal Partner status.
  • Request logged to public audit ledger (case number visible; investigation details protected).
  • Legal review flag triggered if request involves: civilian opt-in data, communities that have not consented to municipal federation, or data outside the requesting officer's jurisdiction.
  • Approved request generates data package with full chain of custody documentation.
  • Affected community notified within 24–48 hours of any data access under this protocol.
  • Data automatically purged from municipal access on case closure or retention period expiry (whichever is earlier).

docs.title: 4. Privacy and Compliance Obligations
docs.text: MFIPPA Municipality is the institution under MFIPPA for all data it receives. Municipality handles access requests, reports breaches, and maintains appropriate records. Data Retention Municipality may retain evidence packages for 2 years from citation issuance, or until court proceedings conclude, whichever is later. All other federated data deleted within 90 days unless actively in use. Prohibited Uses Municipality may not: share with federal immigration authorities (CBSA, IRCC) without court order; use for political monitoring; sell or license to any third party; use for any purpose not listed in this agreement. Breach Notification Municipality notifies CivicMesh Inc. within 24 hours of suspected breach. CivicMesh notifies affected communities within 48 hours of confirmed breach. Annual Audit CivicMesh Inc. has right to audit municipal data handling annually with 10-business-day notice.

docs.title: 5. Transparency Obligations
docs.text: Public Audit Log CivicMesh maintains a public log of all federation events: date, data type, requesting department, legal authority type. Published at civicmesh.ca/transparency. Annual Report CivicMesh publishes annual transparency report. Municipality co-publishes a companion report covering its use of federated data. Community Notification Each community whose data was accessed under an investigation-specific request receives notification within 48 hours (may be delayed up to 30 days with judicial authorization for active investigations).

docs.title: 6. Document Control
docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. Distribution requires written authorization from Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste.

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-012 HOA Condo Participation Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-012 D-CENTRAL GROUP CommunityShield Inc. HOA / Condo Corporation Participation Agreement — Template

Document ID LEGAL-CM-012 Version 1.0 — Template Status Draft — customize per community at execution Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Parties CommunityShield Inc. (Platform Operator) and [HOA / Condo Corporation / Property Manager] (Community Partner) Agreement Type Community safety platform participation and revenue sharing agreement Related Documents LEGAL-CM-013

| Executive Summary This is the master agreement between CommunityShield Inc. and a community organization (HOA board, condo corporation, apartment complex landlord, campus security authority, or business park operator) that deploys the CivicMesh community safety platform on their property. It covers node installation requirements, resident consent procedures, data governance, fine revenue distribution, platform fees, insurance programme access, and exit terms. This agreement is the revenue-generating contract for CommunityShield Inc.'s beachhead market. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Deployment Scope
docs.text: Community Type [HOA / Condo Corporation / Apartment Complex / Campus / Business Park / Gated Community / Private Road — select at execution] Property Address [Civic address and legal description of community property] Authorized Modules [Select: CommunityShield camera network / MeshPlate LPR / MeshEar acoustic detection — listed at execution] Node Count [Estimated number of nodes at deployment — firm count confirmed at installation completion] Community Manager [Named individual authorized to access NOC dashboard and manage community incident queue] Term Initial 2-year term, renewable annually with 60-day advance notice of non-renewal

docs.title: 2. Resident Consent Requirements
docs.text: The Community Partner is responsible for obtaining valid consent from all residents affected by node deployment before any node is activated. CommunityShield Inc. will not activate any node until the Community Partner certifies that the following consent steps are complete: - Written notice delivered to all residents describing: the platform being deployed, the types of data collected, how data will be used, who can access it, how to opt in to the revenue share programme, and how to request data deletion; - Notice period of minimum 30 days before node activation (14 days for non-residential properties); - Resident opt-in agreement (LEGAL-CM-013) made available to all residents who wish to connect personal devices to the community network; - Published camera map showing the location of all community nodes — updated within 5 business days of any node addition or removal; - Community Partner certification to CommunityShield Inc. that all consent steps are complete, signed by the authorized Community Manager.

docs.title: 3. Platform Fees
docs.text: Fee Amount Timing Includes Monthly Platform Fee $[TBD] per month per community Monthly in advance NOC dashboard access, node health monitoring, incident queue, transparency reporting, software updates Per-Node Fee $[TBD] per active node per month Monthly, prior month count Node management, firmware updates, remote diagnostics Installation Fee $[TBD] per node — one-time At installation Hardware supply (DCMC kit), certified technician installation, calibration, credential issuance Annual Audit Fee $[TBD] Annual Annual privacy and compliance audit report for community distribution

docs.title: 4. Fine Revenue Distribution
docs.text: Revenue Source Community Partner CCSC Member Nodes CommunityShield Inc. Parking violation fines (private property) 60% — to community operating fund 25% — to node owners whose devices contributed to the evidence 15% — platform processing fee HOA rule violation fines 70% — to HOA operating fund 20% — to contributing node owners 10% — platform processing fee Trespassing / access violation fines 65% — to community 20% — to contributing node owners 15% — platform fee Government enforcement citations (if municipal federation active) Per LEGAL-CM-011 revenue share — flows through to community Per CCSC patronage formula Per MSSP fee structure

docs.title: 5. Insurance Programme
docs.text: Premium Reduction Eligibility Communities with node coverage above the certified density threshold (defined by CivicMesh Insurance Integration Specification DC-CM-INS-001) are eligible for a certified premium reduction from participating insurers. Certification Process CommunityShield Inc. submits annual coverage report to participating insurers. Insurer issues premium reduction certificate to Community Partner. Individual Member Reductions Residents who opt in to the insurance data share programme (through LEGAL-CM-013) receive individual premium reductions directly from their insurer. Community Partner facilitates opt-in but is not party to the individual insurer relationship. No Guarantee CommunityShield Inc. facilitates the insurance programme but does not guarantee any specific premium reduction. Reduction amounts are at the insurer's discretion.

docs.title: 6. Data Governance
docs.text: Data Ownership All evidence packages generated by nodes within the community property are owned by the Community Partner (for community enforcement purposes) and by the individual node owner (for their device's footage). CommunityShield Inc. has operational access only. Law Enforcement Access Community Partner authorizes CommunityShield Inc. to process law enforcement data requests per the federation protocol (DC-CM-FED-001). Community Partner will be notified of every law enforcement access within 24 hours. Transparency Report CommunityShield Inc. generates and publishes an annual transparency report for the community. Community Partner must distribute this report to all residents within 30 days of publication. Retention Evidence packages retained for 90 days unless: actively involved in an enforcement action (retained until action concludes), subject to a law enforcement hold (retained until hold is released), or the Community Partner requests extended retention (maximum 2 years).

docs.title: 7. Exit and Termination
docs.text: By Community Partner (without cause) 60 days written notice. All nodes deactivated and credentials revoked on effective termination date. CommunityShield Inc. removes or deactivates all hardware within 30 days. By CommunityShield Inc. (with cause) Immediately on: material breach of resident consent obligations; use of platform for prohibited purposes; failure to pay platform fees for 60+ days. Data on Exit All community evidence data returned to Community Partner in standard format (CSV + MP4) within 30 days of termination, or deleted per Community Partner instruction. CommunityShield Inc. retains no copies. CCSC Member Continuity Individual CCSC members (residents with personal nodes) retain their cooperative membership and may reconnect their devices to another community network if one is established.

docs.title: 8. Document Control
docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. Distribution requires written authorization from Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste.

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-013 Resident Node Owner Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-013 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. / CommunityShield Inc. Resident Node Owner Agreement — Individual Opt-In

Document ID LEGAL-CM-013 Version 1.0 — Template Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Internal / Presented to members at onboarding Document Type Consumer-facing opt-in agreement — plain language, grade 8 reading level Parties CivicMesh Inc. / CommunityShield Inc. and [Resident — name at execution] Related Documents LEGAL-CM-012

| Executive Summary This is the individual consumer-facing agreement residents sign when connecting a personal device to the CivicMesh network as a node. It must be written in plain language (Ontario plain language standard — grade 8 reading level), clearly explain what data is and is not collected, be fully PIPEDA-compliant, and make the opt-in and opt-out process unambiguous. This document will be scrutinized by privacy advocates and media — it must be impeccable and honest. | |---|

| What you're agreeing to — plain language summary (displayed before full agreement) • YOU GET: A share of enforcement revenue from violations your device helps detect. A cooperative membership in CCSC. An insurance premium reduction if you opt into the driving data share. A vote in how your community's safety network is governed. • WE COLLECT: Short video clips when your device detects a potential violation (not continuous footage). GPS location of your device. Timestamps. License plates in clips (blurred before leaving your device for non-enforcement clips). • WE DO NOT COLLECT: Continuous video footage. Your identity from the footage. Your driving route for advertising. Any data you haven't consented to share. • LAW ENFORCEMENT ACCESS: Only on a formal legal request. Every request is logged and you will be notified within 48 hours of any access to your data. • YOU CAN LEAVE ANYTIME: Disconnect your device, request deletion of all your data, and cancel your membership at any time. No penalty. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Consent and Opt-In Structure
docs.text: Data Category Consent Type Default Changeable? Violation detection clip generation Implied — core service. Cannot opt out and remain a node owner. On No — core function TrafficMesh enforcement contribution (public road violations) Explicit opt-in at onboarding Off Yes — toggle in Member App anytime CommunityShield community camera contribution Explicit opt-in at onboarding Off Yes — toggle in Member App MeshPlate ALPR contribution Explicit opt-in at onboarding Off Yes — toggle in Member App Insurance driving record share (own vehicle only) Explicit opt-in + insurer selection Off Yes — separate opt-in per insurer MeshNav anonymous movement contribution Explicit opt-in at onboarding Off Yes — toggle in Member App Aggregate data for research (anonymised — no PII) Implied with cooperative membership On Yes — can opt out

docs.title: 2. Your Data Rights
docs.text: Access Request a complete copy of all data held about you at any time: Member App → Settings → Privacy → Download My Data. Fulfilled within 30 days. Correction Contact privacy@civicmesh.ca to correct any inaccurate data. Corrections fulfilled within 30 days. Deletion Request deletion of all your data at any time. Deleted within 30 days, except: data in active law enforcement investigations (deleted when investigation closes) and data required by law. Portability Your cooperative membership credential (Verifiable Credential) is held in your own DID wallet — it is portable and does not disappear if you leave the network. Opt-Out Withdraw any consent at any time by toggling the relevant setting in the Member App. Withdrawal does not affect the lawfulness of processing before withdrawal.

docs.title: 3. Compensation Terms
docs.text: Patronage Basis You receive patronage from CCSC based on validated enforcement citations attributed to your node(s). Not guaranteed — depends on your node's coverage area and violation volume. Payment Methods CAD direct deposit, D-Credit, platform fee credit, or transit credit (where available). Selected in Member App. Tax Obligations Patronage payments are income. CCSC will issue a T4A slip for annual patronage exceeding $500 CAD. You are responsible for reporting this income on your tax return. Government Stipends If your municipality operates a node owner incentive programme, your stipend is paid by the municipality through CCSC as administrator. Insurance Rebates Insurance premium reductions are between you and your insurer. CivicMesh facilitates the data sharing but does not guarantee any specific reduction amount.

docs.title: 4. Document Control
docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. Distribution requires written authorization from Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste.

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-014 Insurance Data Sharing Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-014 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. Insurance Data Sharing Agreement — Template

Document ID LEGAL-CM-014 Version 1.0 — Template Status Draft — customize per insurer at execution Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Parties CivicMesh Inc. (Data Provider) and [Insurance Company Name] (Data Subscriber) Agreement Type B2B data subscription agreement — not a surveillance or enforcement agreement Related Documents LEGAL-CM-013

| Executive Summary This agreement governs the terms under which an insurance company subscribes to CivicMesh data products for actuarial, underwriting, claims, and fraud investigation purposes. Three data product tiers are available: aggregate anonymised corridor and community data (no PII — actuarial teams), consent-gated individual driving record API (opt-in policyholders only — underwriting), and incident evidence package retrieval (specific claims — claims and subrogation teams). All data sharing requires consent at every level, is prohibited from being used for re-identification or adverse purposes, and is auditable on request. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Data Product Tiers
docs.text: Product Tier Description Data Included PII? Pricing Model Tier 1 — Aggregate Feed Anonymised aggregate data for actuarial modelling and risk assessment Corridor risk scores, violation density heatmaps by type/time/season, road condition index by segment, infrastructure asset condition, collision hotspot analysis No PII — all data aggregated and anonymised to census-tract level minimum Annual subscription — per city/region Tier 2 — Individual Record API Consent-gated verified driving record for opted-in policyholders at renewal 12-month driving behaviour summary: lane discipline, following distance, time-of-day profile, validated incident history (consented policyholders only) Yes — PII for consented individuals only. No consent = no access. Per-query fee at underwriting renewal Tier 3 — Incident Evidence Package Specific incident footage and metadata for claims investigation and subrogation Video clip, GPS coordinates, timestamp, violation classification, confidence score, full node credential chain (chain-of-custody proof) Minimum necessary PII for identified claim only Per-package flat fee

docs.title: 2. Consent Requirements by Tier
docs.text: Tier Consent Required Consent Mechanism Consent Verification Tier 1 — Aggregate Community-level consent only (municipal federation agreement or HOA participation agreement) Already obtained via LEGAL-CM-011 or LEGAL-CM-012 CivicMesh Inc. confirms community consent before any Tier 1 data is included in the feed Tier 2 — Individual Explicit individual policyholder opt-in — separate consent per insurer Policyholder opts in through CivicMesh Member App (DC-CM-APP-002) — selects specific insurer(s) to share record with CivicMesh Inc. verifies active consent credential in member DID wallet before processing any Tier 2 query. Query rejected if consent is absent, expired, or revoked. Tier 3 — Incident Individual claimant consent OR valid legal authority (court order, subpoena) Consent obtained through claims process or legal authority presented to CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Inc. logs every Tier 3 retrieval in public audit trail (case reference only)

docs.title: 3. Prohibited Uses
docs.text:
The Data Subscriber expressly agrees that CivicMesh data products will not be used for: - Re-identification of any anonymised individual from Tier 1 aggregate data; - Denial of insurance coverage based solely on residence in a geographic area covered by CivicMesh nodes (proxy discrimination); - Sharing with or selling to any third party, including reinsurers, without CivicMesh Inc.'s prior written consent; - Any immigration enforcement, law enforcement, or government surveillance purpose (insurance data products are strictly for insurance underwriting, claims, and fraud purposes); - Political monitoring or profiling of any individual or group; - Any purpose not listed in Schedule A of this agreement (purposes listed at execution).

Prohibited use clause is a material term. Breach of any prohibited use provision entitles CivicMesh Inc. to terminate this agreement immediately and requires the Data Subscriber to destroy all CivicMesh data in its possession within 24 hours.

docs.title: 4. Pricing Summary
docs.text: Product Pricing Model Estimated Range Renewal Tier 1 — Aggregate Feed Annual subscription per geographic region $[TBD] per city/region per year — scales with data coverage area Annual renewal with 60-day notice Tier 2 — Individual Record Query Per-query fee at policyholder renewal $[TBD] per query — payable on query submission regardless of result No subscription — pay-per-use Tier 3 — Incident Evidence Package Per-package flat fee $[TBD] per package retrieved No subscription — pay-per-use Premium Reduction Certification Annual certification fee for communities $[TBD] per community per year — insurer pays to certify coverage density Annual

docs.title: 5. Audit and Compliance
docs.text: CivicMesh Audit Right CivicMesh Inc. has the right to audit Data Subscriber's use of CivicMesh data products annually with 15-business-day notice. Subscriber must provide access to relevant data use records. Consent Audit For Tier 2 queries, CivicMesh Inc. maintains a complete log of every consent verification. Subscriber may request a consent audit report for any query within 12 months of the query date. Data Breach Data Subscriber must notify CivicMesh Inc. within 24 hours of any suspected breach involving CivicMesh data. Subscriber is liable for any breach resulting from inadequate security controls on its side. Regulatory Changes If PIPEDA or any provincial privacy law changes in a way that affects the lawfulness of any data product, CivicMesh Inc. may suspend that product immediately. Subscriber receives pro-rata refund of any prepaid subscription fees for the suspended period.

docs.title: 6. Incentive Programme Integration
docs.text: Premium Discount Commitments Insurer agrees to offer the following minimum premium discounts to opted-in CivicMesh members: [Tier table to be negotiated per insurer — reference MKT-INS-001 discount table]. These discounts are the primary civilian opt-in incentive and are material to the CivicMesh community value proposition. Fast-Track Claims Insurer agrees to offer an expedited claims process for opted-in policyholders involved in incidents where CivicMesh footage is available. Target: claims resolution within [X] business days where footage is determinative. Marketing Collaboration Insurer may market the premium discount programme to its customers using CivicMesh-approved marketing materials. Co-branded materials require CivicMesh Inc. approval before distribution.

docs.title: 7. Document Control
docs.text: Ver Date Author Description 1.0 May 2026 Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Initial release Classification Notice — This document is CONFIDENTIAL and intended for internal use, legal counsel, and authorized personnel only. Distribution requires written authorization from Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste.

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-015 Contractor Node Kit Agreement v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-015 D-CENTRAL GROUP TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. Contractor Node Kit Agreement — Template

Document ID LEGAL-CM-015 Version 1.0 — Template Status Draft — incorporated into city contractor procurement Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Parties TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. (Kit Provider) and [Contractor Name] (Contractor) Agreement Type Equipment custody and data governance agreement — incorporated by reference into city contractor agreement Related Documents LEGAL-CM-011

| Executive Summary The Contractor Node Kit Agreement governs city contractors who receive modular CivicMesh node kits as a condition of their city contract. The kit is checked out from the city like safety equipment — it activates when plugged in, auto-enrolls the contractor's vehicle, runs passively while the contractor works, and de-enrolls when the kit is returned. The contractor's vehicle becomes a temporary network node while on city contract. This agreement covers custody, tamper obligations, data ownership (city and community, not contractor), and kit return procedures. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Kit Description and Issue
docs.text: Kit Contents CivicMesh Contractor Node Kit CM-TM-TIER3-001: weatherproof edge compute module, forward-facing camera (1080p minimum), GPS module, OBD-II or 12V adapter, cellular SIM (city-provisioned), magnetic/suction mount hardware, tamper-evident seal, quick-start card Kit Issue Kit issued by [City Department — Public Works / Fleet Management] at contract commencement. Contractor signs this agreement as a condition of kit receipt. Kit Identification Each kit has a unique serial number and an active Node DID registered on the CivicMesh blockchain. Kit serial number is linked to contractor's company registration and contract number in the city's procurement system. No Purchase Contractor does not purchase the kit. It is city property in the custody of the contractor for the duration of the city contract.

docs.title: 2. Contractor Obligations
docs.text: Obligation Details Consequence of Breach Installation Install kit on the primary vehicle used for city contract work within 2 business days of kit receipt. Follow installation guide exactly. Do not modify the installation. Kit deactivated. Contractor notified. City notified if kit not installed within 5 business days. Tamper Prohibition Do not open the kit housing, cover the camera, obstruct the GPS antenna, disconnect the cellular module, or interfere with the kit's operation in any way. Immediate tamper event logged to blockchain. Kit DID suspended. City notified immediately. Potential contract breach. Connectivity Ensure kit remains powered and connected during all city contract work. Do not disable the kit's cellular connection. Kit health alert sent to City NOC. Contractor contacted for explanation. Kit Security Store the kit securely when not in use. Do not loan the kit to any other person or use it on any vehicle other than the registered vehicle without prior written approval. Unauthorized use logged. City notified. Contractor liable for any unauthorized data collected. Kit Return Return the kit to [City Department] within 2 business days of contract completion or termination. Kit must be in the same condition as issued (normal wear and tear excepted). Late return: city may deduct replacement cost from contractor holdback. Damaged/missing kit: contractor liable for replacement cost at $[TBD]. Cooperation with Inspection Allow TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. or city representatives to inspect the kit installation on 24-hour notice. Failure to cooperate is a contract breach reportable to the city.

docs.title: 3. Data Ownership and Contractor Privacy
docs.text: Data Ownership All data collected by the kit while on city contract — evidence packages, GPS tracks, video clips, telemetry — is owned by the City of [Municipality] and subject to the Municipal Data Federation Agreement (LEGAL-CM-011). Contractor has no ownership rights in any data collected by the kit. Contractor Vehicle Data OBD-II data (vehicle speed, position, heading) is collected for the purpose of anchoring evidence packages geospatially. This data is used solely for enforcement evidence purposes and is not used to monitor contractor work performance, driving behaviour, or route compliance (beyond what is necessary for evidence package generation). Contractor Identity Protection Contractor identity (company name, driver identity) is not embedded in evidence packages. Packages identify the node ID and contract number. Driver identity is not disclosed in enforcement actions. No Personal Monitoring The kit is not a contractor monitoring device. It is a road safety enforcement device. CivicMesh Inc. and the city agree not to use kit data to monitor contractor compliance with work schedules, route requirements, or employment obligations. Privacy of Third Parties Faces and personal vehicle plates of non-violating vehicles are blurred by the kit before any data is transmitted. The kit does not collect personal information beyond what is necessary for violation detection.

docs.title: 4. Contractor Benefits
docs.text: Construction Zone Protection When a contractor's kit is active, the CivicMesh platform automatically activates a construction zone enforcement envelope around the contractor's vehicle location. Speed violations within this envelope are flagged and enforced. This protects the contractor's workers from speeding vehicles at no additional cost to the contractor. Safety Record Participating contractors receive an annual CivicMesh Contractor Safety Participation certificate — a documented record that their operations contributed to road safety enforcement. This can be used in future contract bids as a safety credential. Optional Civilian Extension Contractors whose employees wish to connect personal dashcams to the civilian opt-in programme (and earn patronage revenue) are free to do so through the standard CCSC membership process. This is entirely voluntary and separate from this agreement.

docs.title: 5. Kit Return and Data Wipe
docs.text:
• Contractor returns kit to [City Department] within 2 business days of contract completion or termination.
  • City department confirms kit physical condition and serial number.
  • TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. deactivates the kit's Node DID on the blockchain within 24 hours of return confirmation.
  • Kit's local storage (any evidence packages buffered locally and not yet uploaded) is wiped within 24 hours of deactivation.
  • Kit is inspected, recertified if required, and prepared for reissue to next contractor.
  • Contractor receives a Data Clearance Certificate confirming all kit data has been wiped and the Node DID is deactivated.

docs.title: 6. City Integration
docs.text:
This agreement is intended to be incorporated by reference into the city's standard contractor agreement. The following clause should be added to all city contracts where node kit deployment applies:

| Recommended Contract Clause for City Procurement • "As a condition of this contract, the Contractor agrees to receive, install, operate, and return a CivicMesh Contractor Node Kit in accordance with the CivicMesh Contractor Node Kit Agreement (Document LEGAL-CM-015), which is incorporated by reference into this contract. The Contractor acknowledges that the kit is city property, that all data collected by the kit is city property, and that any tampering with or unauthorized use of the kit constitutes a material breach of this contract." | |---|

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-016 Open Source Licence Strategy v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-016 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. Open Source Licence Strategy — AGPL v3 Dual-Licence Architecture

Document ID LEGAL-CM-016 Version 1.0 Status Draft — for legal counsel review Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Internal — portions to be published Licence Type Dual-licence: AGPL v3 Community Edition + Proprietary Commercial Edition Hardware Licence CERN Open Hardware Licence Strong Reciprocal (CERN-OHL-S v2) Related Documents LEGAL-CM-001

| Executive Summary CivicMesh uses a dual-licence model. The Community Edition is published under GNU AGPL v3 — the copyleft provision means any organization that deploys the platform as a service must open source their modifications. The Commercial Edition adds proprietary layers (MSSP orchestration, insurance API, data marketplace) available only to commercial licence holders. This model: drives adoption (anyone can evaluate and deploy Community Edition free), generates revenue (production enforcement deployments require Commercial Edition), protects against proprietary forks (AGPL prevents competitors from taking the code without contributing back), and enables academic partnerships (universities use Community Edition under AGPL). | |---|

docs.title: 1. Dual-Licence Component Map
docs.text: Component Community Edition (AGPL v3) Commercial Edition Node firmware and edge AI models ✅ Open source — GitHub ✅ Same codebase — commercial licence required for enforcement use Ingestion API and evidence pipeline ✅ Open source ✅ Included Officer review portal (React + FastAPI) ✅ Open source ✅ Included Digital twin mapping engine ✅ Open source ✅ Included Federation protocol implementation ✅ Open source ✅ Included DID/VC credential schemas ✅ Open source (W3C standard) ✅ Included MSSP orchestration layer ❌ Not in Community Edition ✅ Commercial only — multi-tenant management, partner billing, SLA tooling Insurance API integration ❌ Not in Community Edition ✅ Commercial only — consent-gated individual records, aggregate feed Data marketplace API ❌ Not in Community Edition ✅ Commercial only — insurer and municipal subscriber management

docs.title: 2. AGPL v3 Key Obligations for Deployers
docs.text: Network Use Provision (the AGPL difference) Unlike GPL, AGPL requires that any organization that deploys the software over a network (SaaS or API) must provide source code to users. This is the anti-competitive fork protection — a competitor who builds a commercial enforcement platform on CivicMesh code must open source all their modifications. Attribution Requirement All deployments must retain copyright notices and attribution to CivicMesh Inc. in source code and user-facing documentation. Share-Alike Any derivative work distributed or deployed over a network must be licenced under AGPL v3. Cannot be relicenced to a proprietary licence. Patent Grant Contributors grant a patent licence for patents necessarily infringed by their contributions — protects community from patent aggression.

docs.title: 3. Commercial Licence Summary
docs.text: Who Needs One Any MSSP deploying CivicMesh for municipal enforcement, HOA fine enforcement, or any revenue-generating deployment. Academic/research use under AGPL — no commercial licence required. What It Grants Rights to: deploy Commercial Edition features; keep modifications proprietary (no AGPL share-alike on Commercial Edition additions); use CivicMesh marks in partner marketing per brand guidelines What It Does Not Grant Cannot: sublicence to third parties; deploy outside Authorized Territory; use for prohibited purposes (immigration enforcement, political monitoring); remove attribution from Community Edition components Licence Fee Incorporated into MSSP Partner Agreement (LEGAL-CM-010) — no separate payment. Commercial licence is granted as part of MSSP certification.

docs.title: 4. CERN-OHL-S v2 — Open Hardware Licence
docs.text: Applies To All CivicMesh node hardware designs: PCB schematics, enclosure STEP files, component BOM, hardware-specific firmware Key Obligation Any modified version of the hardware design that is distributed or manufactured at scale must be published under CERN-OHL-S v2. Prevents a large manufacturer from forking the design with proprietary improvements. Commercial Manufacture Permitted under CERN-OHL-S v2 — the licence does not restrict commercial use, only the obligation to share modifications. DCMC Position DCMC manufactures the reference design. Other certified manufacturers may produce compatible nodes using the published design.

docs.title: 5. Contributor Licence Agreement (CLA)
docs.text: Purpose Any external contributor must sign a CLA: (a) confirms right to contribute the code; (b) grants CivicMesh Inc. right to include contribution in both Community and Commercial editions; (c) contributor retains copyright. Dual-Licence CLA Contributors agree their contributions may be included in both AGPL v3 Community and Commercial editions. Without this, contributions could not be included in the Commercial Edition. CLA Platform CLA Assistant or equivalent deployed on GitHub. Automated — contributors sign electronically when submitting their first pull request.

docs.title: LEGAL-CM-017 MSSP Franchise Disclosure Document v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-CM-017 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. MSSP Franchise Disclosure Document — Arthur Wishart Act Compliance

Document ID LEGAL-CM-017 Version 1.0 Status Draft — legal counsel required for full FDD Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Governing Legislation Arthur Wishart Act (Franchise Disclosure), 2000, S.O. 2000, c. 3 Required Delivery Minimum 14 days before franchisee signs any agreement or pays any money Related Documents LEGAL-CM-010

| Executive Summary Ontario's Arthur Wishart Act requires franchisors to provide a disclosure document to prospective franchisees at least 14 days before signing any franchise agreement or receiving any payment. The CivicMesh MSSP Partner Agreement (LEGAL-CM-010) may constitute a franchise agreement under the Act — this depends on whether the definition of 'franchise' (s.1) applies to the MSSP model. Legal counsel must confirm applicability before the first external MSSP Partner Agreement is executed. This document specifies the content required if the Act applies. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Arthur Wishart Act Applicability Analysis
docs.text: Element Definition CivicMesh MSSP — Does It Apply? Grant of right to engage in business of offering/selling goods or services MSSP deploys CivicMesh platform as a service to municipalities and communities Likely yes — MSSP offers enforcement platform services Significant degree of control over franchisee's business CivicMesh Inc. controls: platform standards, SLA requirements, certification requirements, data governance rules, branding requirements Likely yes — significant operational control Franchise fee or other payment MSSP pays platform fees, per-node fees, per-citation fees to CivicMesh Inc. Likely yes — ongoing payment for franchise rights Use of franchisor's trademark MSSP uses CivicMesh marks in client marketing per brand guidelines Yes Action: Engage Ontario franchise law counsel to determine whether the Arthur Wishart Act applies to the CivicMesh MSSP model before executing any external MSSP Partner Agreement. If it applies, a full FDD must be prepared and delivered 14 days before execution. The content requirements are specified in Section 2 below.

docs.title: 2. FDD Content Requirements (if Act applies)
docs.text: Required Item Content Summary Franchisor's business background D-Central Group Inc. and CivicMesh Inc. incorporation, principal business, officers and directors, prior franchise experience Business experience of directors and officers Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste — education, prior business experience, relevant technical background Litigation history Any civil or criminal proceedings involving the franchisor or its principals — disclose all Franchise system description CivicMesh platform, the five modules, MSSP role, end client types, territory definition Fees payable All fees in LEGAL-CM-010 — base, per-node, per-citation, certification, audit — in full Financial projections (if provided) If CivicMesh Inc. provides any revenue or earnings projections to prospective MSSPs, these must be included with their basis disclosed Existing and former franchisee list List of all current MSSP partners and any former partners with contact information (for prospective MSSP to contact independently) Financial statements CivicMesh Inc. audited (or reviewed) financial statements for the most recent fiscal year Material contracts All agreements the MSSP will be required to sign — LEGAL-CM-010, LEGAL-CM-016, any ancillary agreements

docs.text:
LEGAL-CS-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CommunityShield Inc. Articles of Incorporation — Federal CCPC

Document ID LEGAL-CS-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal Parent D-Central Group Inc. (100% owner) Purpose CommunityShield, MeshPlate, and MeshEar operations for HOA, condo, campus, and private property Related Documents LEGAL-CM-001

| Executive Summary CommunityShield Inc. is the operating entity for the three community-facing CivicMesh modules: CommunityShield (doorbell/camera network), MeshPlate (community LPR), and MeshEar (acoustic detection). It contracts with HOAs, condo corporations, apartment landlords, university campuses, and business parks. It manages CCSC member node owner relationships and processes community fine revenue for distribution. This is the entity through which community revenue share flows to CCSC members — it is the beachhead revenue product while TrafficMesh is in regulatory approval. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Modules Operated
docs.text: Module Description Regulatory Basis Key Document CommunityShield Privacy-preserving doorbell and outdoor camera network for HOAs, condos, apartments, campuses. On-device face/plate blurring. Local NAS/private cloud storage. PIPEDA + Ontario RTA for residential. No municipal approval required for private property deployments. REG-CS-001 MeshPlate Community-owned LPR with hash-before-store privacy architecture. No national private database. Community and municipal data only. Ontario — legislative gap for ALPR (no specific statute). Charter s.8 analysis required. REG-MP-001 MeshEar Event-triggered acoustic detection. On-device processing only — no raw audio uploaded. 10-second max clip on event detection. Criminal Code s.184 wiretapping analysis — event-triggered design is the key legal distinction from continuous monitoring. REG-ME-001

docs.title: 2. Community Revenue Flow
docs.text: CommunityShield Inc. processes community fine revenue for HOA and condo deployments: - Community violation confirmed by HOA/property manager (equivalent to officer review for private property enforcement); - Fine collected by CommunityShield Inc. from violating party; - Platform processing fee deducted; - Remainder distributed per community revenue share agreement: HOA operating fund + CCSC member node owner patronage + CivicMesh Inc. data product allocation; - All distributions logged in NOC dashboard and annual transparency report.

docs.title: 3. Why This Is the Beachhead Revenue Product
docs.text: No regulatory dependency HOA and condo deployments are on private property under civil authority — no municipal approval, no HTA analysis required. Can deploy from Month 3. Immediate revenue NOC platform fees from the first HOA client provide cash flow while TrafficMesh regulatory approval is in progress. Network building Every CommunityShield deployment adds nodes to the CivicMesh network. When the civilian opt-in programme launches, these nodes are already enrolled and operational. Case study generation 5 Ottawa HOA deployments give you real revenue data, real safety outcome data, and real privacy compliance track record to show OC Transpo and City of Ottawa.

LEGAL-CTC-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative (CTC) Articles & Bylaws Specification — Ontario Cooperative Corporations Act

Document ID LEGAL-CTC-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — requires cooperative law counsel Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Internal / Public on establishment Governing Legislation Ontario Cooperative Corporations Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.35 Cooperative Type Multi-stakeholder — training providers as producer members Related Documents LEGAL-CCSC-001

| Executive Summary CTC is the cooperative that governs the CivicMesh Training Institute. D-Central Group Inc. is the founding member and curriculum standards anchor. Accredited training providers (Algonquin College, Carleton, private trainers) join CTC as member providers. Training revenue is distributed back to member providers proportionally. Curriculum standards are co-developed by members. This cooperative structure ensures that the certification programme is governed by practitioners — not unilaterally controlled by D-Central — which gives it credibility and adoption among professional training organizations. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Membership
docs.text: Founding Member D-Central Group Inc. (curriculum standards anchor — permanent seat on standards committee) Provider Members Accredited training organizations: Algonquin College (target first accredited provider), Carleton University, uOttawa, private security training companies Accreditation Requirements Provider must: (a) deliver curriculum that meets CTC competency standards; (b) have qualified instructors (minimum L3 certified for technical tracks); (c) meet assessment quality standards; (d) submit to annual CTC audit Membership Fee $2,000 CAD per year per provider organization

docs.title: 2. Revenue Distribution
docs.text: Revenue Source Delivering Provider CTC Common Fund Curriculum Development Track 1 — Node Technician ($299–$799) 60% 30% 10% Track 2 — MSSP Operator ($1,200) 60% 30% 10% Track 3 — Community Manager ($399) 60% 30% 10% Track 4 — Security Response ($399) 60% 30% 10% Track 5 — Municipal Partner ($599) 60% 30% 10%

docs.title: 3. Curriculum Governance
docs.text: Standards Committee D-Central permanent seat + elected provider representatives (1 per 5 members, minimum 2). Reviews and approves curriculum changes. RFC Process Any member may propose curriculum changes via a formal RFC. Standards Committee reviews within 30 days. Approved changes take effect on next certification cycle. Version Control All curriculum documents version-controlled in CTC shared repository. Each certification cohort trained on the current version at time of enrollment. Rapid Update Protocol Platform updates that affect certification content (new module release, regulatory change) trigger an expedited 2-week review and mandatory provider notification.

docs.title: 4. Algonquin College Partnership — First Target
docs.text: Relationship Algonquin is the founder's institution. The innovation hub relationship provides the incubator connection required for Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur. Programme Integration CivicMesh Node Technician as a continuing education certificate. CivicMesh Training as an applied research collaboration under Algonquin's industry partnership programme. Revenue Impact If Algonquin delivers 50 L1 certifications in Year 1 at $299 each ($14,950 revenue), Algonquin receives 60% ($8,970) and CTC receives 40% ($5,980). This is real revenue before a single city contract is signed. Mitacs Lever Algonquin as accredited CTC provider also strengthens the Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur application — two Algonquin relationships (incubator + CTC provider) make the application compelling.

docs.title: LEGAL-GUARD-COOP-001 CivicMesh Guard Cooperative v1
docs.text:
LEGAL-GUARD-COOP-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Security Response Cooperative Articles & Bylaws Specification — Ontario Cooperative Corporations Act

Document ID LEGAL-GUARD-COOP-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Internal Cooperative Type Producer cooperative — licensed security response providers as members Related Documents LEGAL-CCSC-001

| Executive Summary The CivicMesh Security Response Cooperative governs the certified security response providers who receive dispatch through the CivicMesh platform. D-Central Guard Inc. is the founding member and first certified responder. As other licensed security companies become certified CivicMesh responders, they join the cooperative. Members receive patronage from dispatch revenue, set response standards collectively, and participate in the continuous improvement of the dispatch protocol. This cooperative structure converts competing security companies into network participants with shared economic interests in the platform's growth. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Membership and Dispatch Revenue Share
docs.text: Membership Eligibility Licensed security provider in Ontario (Private Security and Investigative Services Act), CivicMesh Response Track 4 certified, valid E&O and liability insurance, active dispatch integration Membership Fee $2,500 CAD per year Dispatch Revenue Model Per-dispatch fee paid by community (HOA, campus, municipality) to CivicMesh platform. Platform retains [X]%, dispatches [Y]% to responding provider. Cooperative patronage returns [Z]% of platform's dispatch revenue share to all members pro-rata by dispatch volume. Response SLA Standard: dispatch acceptance within 5 minutes; on-scene within 30 minutes (urban Ottawa). Gold: 3 minutes / 20 minutes. SLA tier determines dispatch queue priority.

docs.title: 2. D-Central Guard — First Responder
docs.text: Role D-Central Guard Inc. is the first certified response provider and serves as the operational proof of concept for the dispatch model before other providers are certified. Founding Member Holds one permanent cooperative founding member vote. Sets initial response standards that become the cooperative standard. Transition Plan As cooperative membership grows, D-Central Guard transitions from default responder to one competitive provider among many — competing on response time and quality, not on exclusivity. Geographic Priority D-Central Guard focuses on Ottawa central while certifying partner providers for suburbs, other Ontario cities, and eventually other provinces.

docs.title: 3. Integration with Private Investigation
docs.text: PI Services Cooperative members who hold private investigation licences (in addition to security licences) may receive referrals for evidence package follow-up investigations from D-Central Forensics. Cost Reduction Argument Municipal client pitch: CivicMesh + cooperative response dispatch reduces sworn officer dispatch costs by routing sub-criminal incidents to licensed security response at [30–50]% lower cost per incident. Police called only when cooperative responder confirms escalation need. Chain of Custody All cooperative responder actions are logged with responder DID signature, timestamped, and attached to the originating evidence package chain of custody.

LEGAL-MSSP-COOP-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh MSSP Cooperative Articles & Bylaws Specification — Ontario Cooperative Corporations Act

Document ID LEGAL-MSSP-COOP-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Internal Cooperative Type Producer cooperative — MSSP operators as members Related Documents LEGAL-CM-010

| Executive Summary The CivicMesh MSSP Cooperative gives certified MSSP operators a formal cooperative stake in the platform standards and governance. Members participate in setting platform SLAs, certification requirements, and service catalog standards. Patronage returns flow from platform fee revenue to members proportional to their nodes under management. This converts channel partners from commercial licensees into cooperative stakeholders — with aligned incentives and governance rights that increase switching costs significantly. | |---|

docs.title: 1. Membership and Governance
docs.text: Membership Eligibility Any CivicMesh-certified MSSP Gold operator in good standing (current certification, SLA compliance, no outstanding audit findings) Membership Fee $5,000 CAD per year Voting One member one vote. D-Central Group Inc. holds one founding member vote (permanent, non-dilutive). Platform Standards Committee Elected from membership. Reviews and approves SLA changes, service catalog additions, certification requirement changes. D-Central retains veto on security and privacy standards changes. Patronage Return [X]% of net platform fee revenue (after D-Central operating costs) distributed to members pro-rata by nodes under management in the prior year.

docs.title: 2. What Members Gain Beyond Commercial Access
docs.text: Benefit Description Patronage returns Pro-rata share of platform fee revenue — MSSP operators earn from the platform's total success, not just their own clients Governance rights Vote on platform standards, SLA minimums, certification requirements — the standards you must meet are standards you help set Early feature access MSSP Cooperative members get early access to new platform features for client pilot testing Collective marketing MSSP Cooperative brand badge for use in municipal procurement bids — signals D-Central ecosystem alignment and cooperative values Peer network Annual MSSP Cooperative conference, shared playbooks, shared sales tools — competing MSSPs on geography, cooperating on platform improvement

docs.title: 3. D-Central's Role as Founding Member
docs.text: Founding Member Status D-Central Group Inc. holds one permanent founding member vote. This prevents hostile takeover of platform standards by a single large MSSP operator. Technical Authority D-Central retains unilateral authority over: security architecture changes, privacy architecture changes, open source licence terms, DID/VC schema updates. All other platform standards subject to cooperative governance. Platform Operator D-Central Shield Inc. (D-Central's own MSSP) is also a Cooperative member, competing with other MSSPs on equal governance terms.

docs.title: REG-CM-001 CivicMesh PIPEDA Compliance Framework v1
docs.text:
REG-CM-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh PIPEDA Compliance Framework

Document ID REG-CM-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — for legal counsel review Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal / Regulatory Purpose Privacy impact assessment template, consent management, retention/deletion, breach notification, individual access rights Applies To All CivicMesh modules and all community deployments — mandatory Related Documents DC-CM-PRV-001

docs.title: 1. PIPEDA Applicability
docs.text: Does PIPEDA apply? Yes. CivicMesh collects personal information (video footage containing identifiable faces, vehicle plates linked to identifiable owners, location data linked to device owners) in the course of commercial activity. PIPEDA applies to all commercial organizations in Canada collecting, using, or disclosing personal information. Provincial laws that supplement PIPEDA Quebec (Law 25 — stronger consent and transparency requirements), British Columbia (PIPA), Alberta (PIPA). When deploying in these provinces, the provincial law requirements take precedence where stricter than PIPEDA. Exemption analysis Purely personal use exemption (PIPEDA s.4(2)(b)) does not apply — CivicMesh is a commercial platform. Law enforcement exemption does not apply to CivicMesh as operator — only to law enforcement agencies requesting data through the federation protocol.

docs.title: 2. Privacy Impact Assessment Template
docs.text: PIA Element Required Content Responsible Party Data inventory Complete list of personal information collected by module, with legal basis for collection and stated purpose CivicMesh Privacy Officer + MSSP operator Data flow diagram Visual map of data from collection to storage to use to disclosure to deletion — for each module Engineering team — reviewed by Privacy Officer Risk assessment Identification of privacy risks, likelihood, and severity. Mitigation measures for each identified risk. Privacy Officer with MSSP input Consent mechanism Description of how consent is obtained, recorded, and can be withdrawn for each data type Engineering + Legal Third-party disclosures Complete list of third parties who receive personal information, legal basis, contractual protections Legal — reviewed by Privacy Officer Retention and deletion Retention period for each data type, deletion mechanism, verification that deletion occurs as scheduled Engineering + NOC Breach response plan Step-by-step response to a privacy breach — containment, assessment, notification, remediation Privacy Officer + MSSP NOC

docs.title: 3. Ten PIPEDA Principles — CivicMesh Technical Controls
docs.text: Principle CivicMesh Technical Control 1. Accountability Privacy Officer designated per entity. Automated compliance monitoring in SOC Console. Annual privacy audit. 2. Identifying Purposes Purpose tags on all data at collection. Evidence packages carry violation_type and legal_authority fields. No repurposing without new consent. 3. Consent Technical consent enforcement at GraphQL API layer — unconsented data access is technically blocked, not just prohibited. Opt-in default for all sensitive categories. 4. Limiting Collection On-device data minimization — non-violation frames discarded on-device. No continuous recording. Hash-before-store for ALPR. 5. Limiting Use, Disclosure, Retention Purpose-tagged data cannot be used for other purposes. TTL-enforced deletion. Prohibited use clause in all commercial agreements. 6. Accuracy Evidence package immutability — signed packages cannot be modified post-capture. Any modification invalidates the signature. 7. Safeguards TLS 1.3 in transit. AES-256 at rest. ATECC608B hardware key storage on nodes. SOC 2 Type II for MSSP operators. Annual penetration testing. 8. Openness Public privacy architecture documentation. Annual transparency report. civicmesh.ca/privacy — plain language privacy notice. 9. Individual Access Self-service data export in Member App within 30 days. Data correction within 30 days. Deletion within 30 days. 10. Challenging Compliance Privacy complaint process in Manager Dashboard and Member App. Privacy Officer responds within 30 days. OPC complaint pathway documented in all privacy notices.

docs.title: 4. Breach Notification Procedure
docs.text:
• Breach detected (by SOC Console, NOC operator, or external report).
  • Privacy Officer notified immediately. Breach contained — isolate affected systems if necessary.
  • Breach assessed within 24 hours: What data was accessed? How many individuals affected? What is the real risk of significant harm?
  • If real risk of significant harm: notify OPC within 72 hours of determination. Prepare breach report (OPC form).
  • Notify affected individuals as soon as reasonably possible with: description of breach, information at risk, steps taken to reduce harm, steps individual can take, contact information for questions.
  • Notify affected communities via NOC Console emergency alert within 48 hours.
  • Maintain breach record for minimum 2 years — even if no notification was required.
  • Post-breach review within 30 days: root cause analysis, technical remediation, policy updates, annual report entry.

docs.title: REG-CM-002 CivicMesh Ontario Privacy Act Analysis v1
docs.text:
REG-CM-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Ontario Privacy Act Analysis

Document ID REG-CM-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft — for legal counsel review Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal / Regulatory Scope MFIPPA obligations for city-federated data, HOA/condo data governance, biometric data prohibition Related Documents REG-CM-001

docs.title: 1. MFIPPA — Municipal Institutions
docs.text: What is MFIPPA The Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA) governs the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information by Ontario municipal institutions (cities, transit authorities, school boards, etc.). When it applies to CivicMesh When City of Ottawa, OC Transpo, or other Ontario municipal institutions receive TrafficMesh evidence data under a Municipal Data Federation Agreement (LEGAL-CM-011), they become subject to MFIPPA for that data. CivicMesh Inc. is not directly subject to MFIPPA — the municipality is. Key municipal obligations (1) Privacy Impact Assessment required before implementing a new information system (IPC/Ontario guidance); (2) Personal information collected only for lawful authority; (3) Individual access rights to their own information; (4) Retention and disposal schedules; (5) Annual reporting to council on surveillance systems. IPC/Ontario guidance on surveillance The Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario has published guidance on municipal surveillance systems. TrafficMesh should be reviewed against this guidance by the municipal partner's privacy team before deployment. D-Central provides a MFIPPA-ready data governance package as part of the municipal deployment.

docs.title: 2. Biometric Data — Critical Prohibition
docs.text: Definition Biometric data in Ontario includes: facial recognition outputs, fingerprints, iris patterns, and any unique physical characteristic used to identify a person. Face blurring outputs are NOT biometric data — they are processed video where biometric data has been removed. CivicMesh position CivicMesh performs face detection only for the purpose of blurring. No facial recognition is performed. No biometric data is generated, stored, or transmitted. The platform is explicitly designed to destroy, not create, biometric data. What is prohibited Running facial recognition to identify individuals from footage. Storing facial feature vectors or encodings. Matching faces across cameras. None of these occur in CivicMesh. Contractual prohibition The MSSP Partner Agreement (LEGAL-CM-010), HOA Participation Agreement (LEGAL-CM-012), and Municipal Data Federation Agreement (LEGAL-CM-011) all explicitly prohibit adding facial recognition capabilities to the platform. This prohibition survives agreement termination. Why this matters Flock Safety and Amazon Ring have faced regulatory scrutiny and civil litigation over facial recognition capabilities (even if disabled by default). CivicMesh's architectural design makes facial recognition technically impossible — not just prohibited.

docs.title: 3. Condo Corporation Data Governance
docs.text: Legal status Condominium corporations in Ontario are governed by the Condominium Act, 1998. They are not municipal institutions — MFIPPA does not apply. PIPEDA applies as a commercial activity. Authority to deploy cameras Condo corporations have authority to install security cameras in common areas under the Condominium Act. The declaration and rules may specify procedures for security decisions — community manager must confirm board authority before installation. Common element surveillance Cameras in common elements (lobby, parking, amenities) are clearly within condo authority. Cameras that could observe individual unit entrances or private outdoor spaces require more careful positioning and privacy analysis. Tenant/owner notification The Condominium Act requires notice to owners and tenants of significant changes to common elements. Camera installation is typically a significant change — notice and (depending on declaration) owner vote may be required.

docs.title: REG-CM-003 CivicMesh Open Source Licence Compliance v1
docs.text:
REG-CM-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. CivicMesh Open Source Licence Compliance

Document ID REG-CM-003 Version 1.0 Status Draft — for legal counsel review Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal / Regulatory Scope AGPL v3 obligations for deployers, dual-licence commercial exceptions, CLA requirements, attribution Related Documents LEGAL-CM-016

docs.title: 1. AGPL v3 Compliance for CivicMesh Deployers
docs.text: Obligation What It Means for CivicMesh Deployments How CivicMesh Enforces Network use provision Any organization that deploys CivicMesh as a service (SaaS or API) must make the source code — including any modifications — available to users. This is the core anti-competitive fork protection. MSSP Partner Agreement requires AGPL compliance. Annual MSSP audit includes open source compliance review. Any unreported modification is a material breach. Attribution retention All deployments must retain copyright notices and attribution to CivicMesh Inc. in source code and documentation. Automated attribution check in firmware build system. Documentation templates include mandatory attribution. Share-alike Modifications distributed or deployed over a network must be licensed under AGPL v3. Cannot be relicensed to a proprietary licence. Verified at MSSP annual audit. CLA for external contributors grants D-Central rights to include contributions in Commercial Edition. CLA requirement External contributors must sign a CLA before their code is merged. CLA grants CivicMesh Inc. rights to include contribution in both Community and Commercial editions. CLA Assistant enforced on GitHub — contribution blocked without signed CLA.

docs.title: 2. Commercial Edition Boundary
docs.text: What Commercial Edition adds MSSP orchestration layer, insurance API integration, data marketplace API, NOC dashboard premium features. These are proprietary additions to the AGPL core. Commercial licence grant MSSP operators receive a Commercial Edition licence as part of the MSSP Partner Agreement. No separate commercial licence fee — included in MSSP fee structure. What Commercial Edition does not allow Re-closing the AGPL components. Removing attribution from Community Edition code. Sublicensing to any third party. Deploying outside Authorized Territory. The dual-licence paradox An MSSP could theoretically self-host the AGPL Community Edition without a Commercial licence — they just wouldn't have the MSSP orchestration layer, insurance API, or data marketplace features. This is acceptable — the Community Edition is genuinely open.

docs.title: 3. CERN-OHL-S v2 Hardware Compliance
docs.text: Obligation Application Consequence of Non-Compliance Share-alike for hardware modifications Any manufacturer that modifies the open hardware design must publish modifications under CERN-OHL-S v2 Modified hardware loses CivicMesh certification — Node Provenance Credential will not be issued. Cannot be deployed on the network. Attribution on manufactured hardware CivicMesh design attribution must appear on PCB silkscreen and in product documentation Certification requirement — verified at manufacturing audit Source file availability All design source files (KiCad, STEP, BOM) must be provided to customers on request Certification requirement

docs.title: REG-COOP-001 Cooperative Regulatory Compliance Matrix v1
docs.text:
REG-COOP-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. Cooperative Regulatory Compliance Matrix

Document ID REG-COOP-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — for legal counsel review Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal / Regulatory Scope Ontario Cooperative Corporations Act requirements, CRA cooperative tax treatment, member equity rules, FSRA path for DECU Related Documents LEGAL-CCSC-001

docs.title: 1. Ontario Cooperative Corporations Act — Key Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Ontario CCA Section D-Central Cooperative Obligations Compliance Approach Annual General Meeting s.78 — within 6 months of fiscal year end All D-Central cooperatives (CCSC, CTC, MSSP Coop, Guard Coop) must hold AGM within 6 months of December 31 fiscal year end — by June 30 annually. AGM calendar set in cooperative bylaws. Automated reminder 90 days before deadline in cooperative governance module. Financial statements s.79 — presented at AGM Audited or reviewed financial statements presented to members at AGM. Annual financial statements prepared by accountant. Audit required for cooperatives with revenue above threshold. Member register s.44 — maintain register of members Each cooperative maintains a current member register with name, address (for legal purposes — CivicMesh uses DID as identifier), date of membership. CCSC membership managed via CivicMesh Member App — DID as primary identifier, legal name collected for T4A purposes only. Director qualifications s.67 — Canadian resident majority Majority of directors must be Canadian residents. All current D-Central cooperative directors are Canadian residents. Board composition reviewed at each election. Dissolution restrictions s.120 — surplus to members On dissolution, surplus distributed to members or donated to charitable purpose — not retained by corporate entity. Cooperative bylaws specify dissolution procedure consistent with s.120.

docs.title: 2. CRA Cooperative Tax Treatment
docs.text: Tax Consideration CRA Position D-Central Application Patronage dividends Deductible by the cooperative when paid to members. Income to members in the year received. CCSC patronage distributions are deductible by CCSC and income to members. T4A issued for patronage >$500 CAD. T4A reporting Cooperatives must issue T4A slips to members receiving patronage dividends > $500 in a calendar year. Member App tracks cumulative patronage. T4A generated and distributed by January 31 for prior year. D-Credit classification CRA has not issued specific guidance on cooperative digital currencies. D-Credit should be structured to be received as D-Credit and not taxable until converted to CAD. Legal opinion on D-Credit tax treatment required before public launch. Structured as a cooperative patronage instrument denominated in cooperative currency units. Non-share capital cooperative CCSC and most D-Central cooperatives are non-share capital (no shares — member equity through contributions and patronage). Different from business cooperatives with share capital. Non-share capital structure confirmed in each cooperative's articles. No share issuance possible.

docs.title: 3. DECU — Credit Union Regulatory Path
docs.text: Governing legislation Ontario Credit Unions and Caisses Populaires Act, 2020 (CUCPA 2020). Regulated by Financial Services Regulatory Authority of Ontario (FSRA). Minimum requirements to incorporate (1) Minimum 150 founding members in Ontario; (2) Minimum $250,000 in member shares; (3) Business plan approved by FSRA; (4) Board of directors meeting FSRA fitness requirements; (5) Insurance through Deposit Insurance Corporation of Ontario (DICO). Realistic timeline 12–24 months from initial application to FSRA approval and operating licence. This is a Year 3–5 initiative. D-Credit integration path D-Credit is designed for integration with DECU once DECU is operational. D-Credit balances held in DECU accounts — providing deposit insurance protection and CRA clarity on the tax treatment of D-Credit as a cooperative account balance rather than a digital currency. Interim structure While DECU is in the regulatory path, D-Credit is managed by CivicMesh Inc. as a cooperative accounting instrument. Patronage is converted to D-Credit at par with CAD. D-Credit can be redeemed for CAD through the platform. This structure avoids securities classification.

docs.title: REG-CS-001 CommunityShield Surveillance Regulatory Analysis v1
docs.text:
REG-CS-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP D-Central Group / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. / CivicMesh Inc. CommunityShield Surveillance Regulatory Analysis

Document ID REG-CS-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft — for legal counsel review Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Legal / Regulatory Scope Municipal camera bylaws, condo corporation authority, landlord rights, Residential Tenancies Act, PIPEDA application to residential networks Related Documents DC-CS-ARCH-001

docs.title: 1. Municipal Surveillance Camera Bylaws
docs.text: Municipality Relevant Bylaw / Policy Applicable to CommunityShield? Key Requirement City of Ottawa No Ottawa-specific camera registration bylaw as of 2026. IPC/Ontario guidance applies. No registration required for private property cameras. Cameras must comply with PIPEDA. Signage recommended ('Premises under video surveillance'). City of Toronto Toronto has adopted a surveillance technology policy for city-owned cameras. Private property cameras governed by PIPEDA. Toronto policy applies to city cameras — not to private HOA deployments. Private HOA cameras in Toronto: PIPEDA + Toronto Building Code for installation. Vancouver No camera registration bylaw. BC PIPA applies for commercial entities. BC PIPA requirements apply to condo corporations and property managers. Privacy notice required. BC PIPA complaints handled by OIPC BC.

docs.title: 2. Condo Corporation Camera Authority
docs.text: Legal authority Under the Ontario Condominium Act, 1998, the board of directors has authority to manage common elements, which includes reasonable security measures. Camera installation in common areas is within board authority. Declaration and rules check The condominium declaration may specify procedures for security decisions. Some declarations require owner vote for significant changes. The community manager must review the declaration before installation. Owner notification Ontario Condominium Act s.97 requires notice to owners before making an addition, alteration, or improvement to the common elements that is not a repair or maintenance. Camera installation typically requires s.97 notice. Template notice provided in CommunityShield HOA deployment package. Hallway cameras — specific guidance Cameras in building hallways should be positioned to monitor common area activity, not to enable ongoing monitoring of which tenants are entering/leaving their units. Cameras positioned directly opposite unit entrances with no other coverage purpose are more legally exposed. Angle cameras to capture common area while minimizing coverage of specific unit entrances.

docs.title: 3. Residential Tenancies Act — Landlord Limits
docs.text: Scenario Permitted? Legal Basis CommunityShield Design Response Exterior building cameras (entrances, parking) Yes Landlord-owned property. Standard security camera authority. Fully supported. Lobby and common area cameras inside building Yes, with notice Landlord property. RTA s.22 (quiet enjoyment) requires notice. Supported. Tenant notification package provided. Camera in building hallway Yes, with restrictions Hallways are common areas. Camera must not unreasonably intrude on tenants' reasonable expectation of privacy in accessing their unit. Camera placement guidance in deployment package: angle away from direct unit entrance monitoring. Camera inside tenant unit No Tenant has exclusive possession under RTA s.2 and quiet enjoyment rights under s.22. Architecturally impossible — CommunityShield only provisions exterior and common area cameras. Requiring tenant to install camera as lease condition No This would be an unlawful condition in a tenancy agreement under RTA s.4. Tenant opt-in programme is voluntary and independent of lease terms.
