docs.title: Role 7: Public Citizen (Transparency & Accountability)
docs.text:
Devices: Desktop (primary), Mobile (secondary)

Note: No login required. All data public.

Landing Page: Transparency Dashboard

┌─ CivicMesh Transparency Center ────────────────┐ │ civicmesh.app/transparency │ │ │ ├─ Network Overview ─────────────────────────────┤ │ Active Nodes: 247 vehicles | 3 cities │ │ Violations This Month: 12,847 │ │ False Positive Rate: 3.1% (target: <5%) │ │ Citations Issued: 11,203 | Dismissed: 824 │ │ │ ├─ Revenue Distribution (Blockchain-Verified) ──┤ │ Municipal enforcement: 72% ($892,140) │ │ Platform operations: 15% ($186,300) │ │ CCSC community patronage: 8% ($99,360) │ │ Civilian rewards: 5% ($62,100) │ │ [View blockchain ledger →] │ │ │ ├─ Law Enforcement Data Requests ────────────────┤ │ This Month: 14 requests │ │ Approved: 12 | Denied: 2 │ │ Authority types: 9 WARRANT, 3 REASONABLE... │ │ Avg. notification time: 38 hours │ │ [View public request log →] │ │ │ ├─ AI Model Performance ─────────────────────────┤ │ ALPR v2.3.1: FPR 1.8% | Last retrained: Apr │ │ Speed estimator v1.9: FPR 2.4% │ │ Lane detection v3.1: FPR 4.1% │ │ [View model cards & training data →] │ │ │ ├─ Governance & Community ──────────────────────┤ │ Open RFCs: 3 (under review) │ │ Closed this month: 1 │ │ Next TSC meeting: June 15, 2026 (public) │ │ [Participate in governance →] │ │ │ │ [Download reports] [API access] [Share data] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Features:

  1. Real-Time Platform Statistics
  2. Live violation counts (updates every 15 minutes)
  3. Network map (showing active nodes, jurisdictions)
  4. False positive rate vs. target (trending)
  5. Revenue distribution breakdown

  6. Officer actions (citation issued/dismissed rate)

  7. Community Evidence Viewing ``` civicmesh.app/community/evidence

┌─ Evidence Explorer ──────────────────────┐ │ Filter: [Type ▼] [Zone ▼] [Month ▼] │ │ │ │ Incident #CM-2026-00421 │ │ Type: Speeding (67 km/h in 50 zone) │ │ Location: King St, Toronto │ │ Date: June 4, 2026, 14:00 │ │ Confidence: 97% │ │ Outcome: Citation issued │ │ Officer: #[ANON-7521] │ │ [Watch redacted video ▶] │ │ [View full details] │ │ │ │ Incident #CM-2026-00420 │ │ Type: Lane violation │ │ Location: Bloor St, Toronto │ │ Outcome: Dismissed (confidence 86%) │ │ [Watch redacted video ▶] │ │ [View full details] │ │ │ │ Showing 1-20 of 11,203 this month │ │ [Export data] [API access] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ ```

  • Redaction applied automatically:

    • Faces: Blurred (Gaussian blur, <1% recognizable)
    • Plates: Pixelated (8×8 blocks, unreadable)
    • Location: Street name only ("King St, Toronto")
    • Officer identity: Anonymized (#ANON-7521)
    • Timestamp: Hour resolution only (14:00, not 14:32:15)

  • What you can verify:

    • Is enforcement actually happening? (count incidents)
    • False positive rate matches claims? (filter by dismissed)
    • Dismissal reasons are legitimate? (aggregate by reason)
    • School/residential zones enforced? (filter by zone)
    • Officer performance is consistent? (compare dismissal rates)

  • Law Enforcement Request Log (Public) ``` civicmesh.app/transparency/leo-requests

┌─ Data Request Log ──────────────────────┐ │ Showing requests this month │ │ │ │ June 1, 2026 | WARRANT │ │ Authority: Toronto Police Service │ │ Data type: [redacted for privacy] │ │ Status: APPROVED │ │ Community notified: June 1, 9:30am │ │ │ │ June 3, 2026 | REASONABLE_GROUNDS │ │ Authority: TPS │ │ Data type: [redacted] │ │ Status: APPROVED │ │ Community notified: June 3, 11:00am │ │ │ │ June 4, 2026 | ROUTINE │ │ Authority: Insurance adjuster (subpoena)│ │ Data type: [redacted] │ │ Status: DENIED (privacy concern) │ │ Reason: [explained] │ │ │ │ Requesting 14 records this month │ │ Approved: 12 | Denied: 2 │ │ [View full legal basis documentation] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ ```

  1. Annual Transparency Reports ``` civicmesh.app/reports

┌─ Public Reports ────────────────────────┐ │ 2025 Annual Report │ │ Status: ✅ PUBLISHED & AUDITED │ │ Auditor: [Firm name], [credentials] │ │ [View PDF] [View JSON] [Download data] │ │ │ │ 2025-Q2 Monthly Summary │ │ [View details] │ │ │ │ 2025-Q1 Monthly Summary │ │ [View details] │ │ │ │ Full archive of all reports │ │ [Browse by date] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ ```

  • 8 sections (auto-generated, no manual editing):

    1. Network overview
    2. Enforcement metrics
    3. Law enforcement access
    4. AI model performance
    5. Privacy compliance
    6. Revenue distribution
    7. Governance activity
    8. Corrective actions

  • Governance Participation ``` civicmesh.app/governance

┌─ Open Governance Center ────────────────┐ │ Request for Comments (RFC) │ │ │ │ IN PROGRESS (14-day comment period) │ │ RFC: Blockchain financial ledger │ │ Submitted: June 1, 2026 │ │ Comment deadline: June 15 │ │ [View details] [Read & comment] [Vote] │ │ │ │ RECENTLY APPROVED │ │ RFC: Transparency layer architecture │ │ Approved: May 31 (4/5 TSC) │ │ [View implementation plan] │ │ │ │ TSC Meetings (Public) │ │ Next: June 15, 7pm UTC │ │ [Add to calendar] [Watch replay] │ │ [View past meeting notes] │ │ │ │ Audit Petitions │ │ Petition #342: "AI false positives" │ │ Status: 125/100 signatures (TRIGGERED) │ │ [View details] │ │ │ │ [Propose new RFC] [Sign audit petition] │ │ [Review contributor guidelines] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ ```

  1. Blockchain Financial Verification ``` civicmesh.app/blockchain

┌─ On-Chain Revenue Ledger ──────────────┐ │ (Polygon, publicly queryable) │ │ │ │ Node: did:civicmesh:node:bus_456 │ │ Period: 2026-06 │ │ Citations: 142 │ │ Gross revenue: $15,620.00 │ │ Distribution: │ │ ├─ Municipal (72%): $11,246.40 │ │ ├─ Platform (15%): $2,343.00 │ │ ├─ CCSC (8%): $1,249.60 │ │ └─ Operator (5%): $781.00 │ │ │ │ Verify: ✅ Matches transparency report │ │ [View smart contract] [Query ledger] │ │ │ │ City aggregate (Ottawa, June 2026): │ │ Total revenue: $1,240,000 │ │ Nodes: 47 | Citations: 12,847 │ │ [View city breakdown] │ │ │ │ [Export blockchain data] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ ```

  1. Public Audit Status ``` civicmesh.app/transparency/audits

┌─ Community Audit Status ───────────────┐ │ Annual Independent Audit (2025) │ │ Status: ✅ COMPLETED (Dec 31) │ │ Auditor: [Firm], [credentials] │ │ Result: PASSED │ │ [View full report] │ │ │ │ Extraordinary Audit Petitions │ │ Petition #342: FPR investigation │ │ Filed: June 1, 2026 │ │ Signatures: 125 / 100 threshold │ │ Status: TRIGGERED │ │ Auditor: [Firm], ETA: July 15 │ │ [View petition details] │ │ │ │ Request Your Personal Audit │ │ [Request audit log for myself] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ ```

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text: Frontend: Three.js (3D) + Mapbox (2D map + 3D integration) Alternative: Babylon.js (if more built-in features needed) Data Source: PostGIS (spatial data) + WebSockets (real-time) Performance: WebGL with LOD (Level of Detail) Mobile: Simplified geometry (fewer vertices)

docs.title: 1. Vehicle 3D Models
docs.text: - Bus/car 3D mesh (low-poly: 500-1000 vertices) - Real-time position + heading - Animated doors/lights - Damage indicators (if applicable) - License plate visible (ALPR context)

docs.title: 2. Road Scene
docs.text: - Lane markings (solid/dashed polylines) - Curbs + sidewalks (simplified geometry) - Traffic signals (actual state: red/yellow/green) - Speed limit signs + other signage - Buildings/context (optional, for orientation) - Road surface + lane colors (violations highlighted)

docs.title: 3. Camera Frustum Visualization
docs.text: - Show actual camera view cone (from dashcam) - FOV = typical dashcam (100-120 degrees) - Indicates what incident camera recorded - User can "look through" the incident camera

docs.title: 4. Radar Detection Visualization
docs.text: - Radar range rings (front: 0-200m) - Detected objects as circles (distance + velocity) - Velocity vectors (arrows showing direction) - Target vehicle highlighted (alleged speeder)

docs.title: 5. Geofence Visualization
docs.text: - School zones: Blue overlay - Residential zones: Green overlay - Construction zones: Orange overlay - Bus lanes: Purple overlay - Parking restrictions: Red overlay

docs.title: 6. Historical Playback
docs.text: - Scrub through incident timeline - See vehicle position 1 min before, at incident, 1 min after - Radar/camera detections timestamped - Speed profile (graph + 3D trail)

docs.title: Performance Optimization
docs.text: Desktop: Full geometry (LOD 0), 60fps target Tablet: Medium geometry (LOD 1), 30fps target Mobile: Low geometry (LOD 2), 30fps target Frustum: Cull off-screen objects Instancing: Render 100s of vehicles efficiently Batching: Group similar objects Cache: Preload common models

docs.title: Event Streams
docs.text:
``` 1. Incident Stream - New violation detected - Confidence score included - Video ready for review - Route to appropriate queue

  1. Vehicle Telemetry Stream
  2. GPS position (1 Hz)
  3. Speed/heading (OBD)
  4. Violations in-vehicle (IMU/radar)

  5. Fleet managers see live movement

  6. System Events Stream

  7. Device online/offline
  8. Sensor failures
  9. Network issues

  10. Processing alerts

  11. Enforcement Events Stream

  12. Citation issued
  13. Officer actions
  14. Escalations
  15. Appeals ```

docs.title: Update Frequency
docs.text: Officer Portal: <500ms (incidents + updates) Fleet Dashboard: 1-2 sec (vehicle positions) City Analytics: 10-30 sec (aggregates) Insurance API: On-demand (queries only) System Monitoring: 5-10 sec (health checks)

docs.title: Responsive Breakpoints
docs.text: Small Mobile: 320px-480px (iPhone 5-8) Mobile: 481px-768px (iPhone XR, Android) Tablet: 769px-1024px (iPad, tablet) Desktop: 1025px+ (laptop, monitor)

docs.title: Touch Interactions
docs.text: Single Tap: Open detail view Long Press: Context menu (copy, share, etc.) Swipe Left: Next incident in queue Swipe Right: Previous incident Pinch Zoom: Map zoom (3D models) Two-Finger: Rotate 3D model

docs.title: Mobile-Only Features
docs.text: - Camera barcode scan (open incident) - Biometric auth (Face ID, fingerprint) - Share location (for backup) - Push notifications (critical alerts) - Offline mode (queue caches locally) - Voice control (hands-free approval)

docs.title: Pattern 1: Progressive Disclosure
docs.text: Level 1: High-level card (summary only) ↓ Click/Expand Level 2: Detailed view (metrics, context) ↓ Click detail Level 3: Drill-down (specific data, video, 3D) ↓ Click specific item Level 4: Analysis (comparisons, trends, actions)

docs.title: Pattern 2: Command Palette
docs.text: Press: Cmd+K (desktop) / Cmd+K (mobile) Shows: Quick search, recent actions, documentation Example: "Issue citation ABC1234" → auto-fills form

docs.title: Pattern 3: Smart Defaults
docs.text: Officer Views: Sorted by severity (high first) Fleet Manager: Red vehicles first (violations) City Planner: Hotspots first (high-violation areas) Civilian: Personal earnings first Insurance: Pending claims first

docs.title: Pattern 4: Undo & Recovery
docs.text: - Undo last action (within 30 sec) - Draft saved (auto-recovery) - Confirmation dialog (destructive actions) - Audit trail (all actions logged)

docs.title: WCAG 2.1 AA Compliance
docs.text: Criterion Implementation Color Contrast 4.5:1 (text), 3:1 (UI components) Focus Visible Blue outline, 3px, 0 gap Keyboard Nav Tab through all interactive elements Screen Readers ARIA labels, semantic HTML Motion Respect prefers-reduced-motion Touch Targets 44×44px minimum (iOS guidelines) Text Resize Readable up to 200% zoom Font Sans-serif, 16px min (body text)

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: - Automated: axe DevTools, Lighthouse - Manual: NVDA (Windows), JAWS (commercial) - User Testing: Blind users, keyboard-only users

docs.title: Reusable Component Library
docs.text: Buttons: Primary, Secondary, Danger, Ghost Cards: Incident, Vehicle, Alert, Metric Tables: Sortable, filterable, paginated Modals: Confirmation, form submission Tooltips: Hover help, keyboard accessible Badges: Status (approved, pending, rejected) Alerts: Inline (error, warning, success) Input Fields: Text, number, date, time, select Dropdowns: Single & multi-select Date Pickers: Single & range Video Players: HLS streaming, playback controls 3D Canvas: Three.js viewport, controls Maps: Mapbox GL, markers, popups Notifications: Toast (top-right), persistent Charts: Line, bar, heatmap (Chart.js)

docs.title: Icon System
docs.text: Violations: 🚨 (speeding), 🚸 (school), ♿ (accessible) Status: ✓ (approved), ⏳ (pending), ❌ (rejected) Vehicles: 🚌 (bus), 🚗 (car), 🚚 (truck) Severity: 🔴 (critical), 🟡 (warning), 🟢 (ok) Actions: ▶ (play), ⏹ (stop), 📹 (video)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
CivicMesh's UI/UX strategy prioritizes:

  1. Spatial visualization (maps + 3D) as first-class feature
  2. Real-time updates (WebSocket, sub-second latency)
  3. Role-specific experiences (different landing pages, features)
  4. Modern, accessible design (dark mode, keyboard nav, screen readers)
  5. Mobile-first (responsive, touch-friendly)
  6. Consistent design system (100+ reusable components)

This creates a professional, modern platform that scales across enforcement, fleet operations, city administration, civilians, and insurance partners—all with unified design language and technical foundations.

Next Steps: 1. Create Figma design file with component library 2. Implement React component library (100+ components) 3. Build 3D visualization module (Three.js) 4. Set up WebSocket infrastructure 5. Implement role-based dashboards 6. Field test with OC Transpo officers & fleet managers

docs.title: Comprehensive Violation Detection Guide
docs.text: All 18+ traffic violations detectable via sensor fusion.

docs.title: Violation Detection Matrix
docs.text: # Violation Type Primary Sensor Confidence Range MVP Phase 1 Phase 2 Legal Basis 1 Speeding Radar doppler 95% 0-200m ✓ ✓ ✓ HTA 128 2 Tailgating Rear radar + OBD 90% 0-100m ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 158 3 Unsafe lane change Camera + IMU 85% Lane ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 154 4 Red light running Camera + timing 95% Intersection ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 144 5 Stop sign running Camera + motion 95% Intersection ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 136 6 Aggressive acceleration IMU 90% Onboard ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 219 7 Hard braking IMU + OBD 95% Onboard ✗ ✓ ✓ Safety issue 8 School zone speeding Radar + geofence + time 95% 0-200m ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 214 9 Residential speeding Radar + geofence 95% 0-200m ✗ ✓ ✓ Municipal bylaw 10 Expired plates Camera ALPR + DB 99% 0-200m ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 11 11 Bus lane obstruction Camera + geofence 95% Lane ✗ ✓ ✓ Municipal bylaw 12 Accessible parking abuse Camera ALPR + DB 99% Parking ✗ ✓ ✓ AODA 1.1 13 Improper turns Camera + OBD heading 85% Intersection ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 163 14 Following distance Rear radar + velocity 90% 0-100m ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 158(1) 15 Pedestrian zone violation Camera + geofence 80% Zone ✗ ✓ ✓ Municipal bylaw 16 Cyclist safety violation Camera + distance 80% Lane ✗ ✓ ✓ HTA 156 17 Commercial vehicle overloaded Camera + IMU (sagging) 75% Onboard ✗ ✗ ✓ HTA 121 18 Construction zone speeding Radar + geofence + sign detect 95% 0-200m ✗ ✗ ✓ HTA 128

docs.title: 1.1 Speeding (Any Vehicle)
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 128

Definition: Traveling faster than posted speed limit for zone/road

Sensors: - Primary: Radar doppler (independent measurement) - Secondary: Camera (visual speed verification), Geofence (zone confirmation) - Tertiary: OBD (host vehicle reference)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Radar detects object at distance D with velocity V 2. Verify: V > speed limit for geofence at location 3. Verify: Object is vehicle (not shadow, reflector) 4. Confirm: ALPR reads plate (identity verification) 5. Confidence: radar doppler (95%+) + ALPR (98%) = 95%+ combined

Radar Ground Truth: • Doppler measurement independent of host vehicle motion • Works even if enforcement vehicle is moving • Unaffected by GPS/odometer drift • Validated in field trial: ±2 km/h accuracy ```

Confidence Scoring: ``` High confidence (95%+): ✓ Radar doppler: ±2 km/h accuracy ✓ ALPR: Plate confirmed ✓ Geofence: Speed limit verified ✓ No motion blur (clear video) ✓ Violation margin: >5 km/h over limit

Medium confidence (85-95%): ✓ Radar doppler: Confirmed ✓ ALPR: Partial plate match ✓ Geofence: Speed limit inferred from surroundings ⚠ Violation margin: 3-5 km/h over limit

Low confidence (<85%): ✗ Radar: Marginal signal (weather, multipath) ✗ ALPR: Unread plate (obstruction, angle) ✗ Geofence: Uncertain boundary ✗ Violation margin: <3 km/h → Escalate to manual review ```

Test Scenarios: ``` 1. Stationary test (vehicle at 0 km/h) • Radar should read 0 • False positive: 0%

  1. Known speed test (driving at exactly 50 km/h in 50 zone) • Radar: 50±2 km/h • Incident triggered: NO • Confidence: 99%

  2. Overspeed test (driving at 65 km/h in 50 zone) • Radar: 65±2 km/h • Incident triggered: YES • Confidence: 97%

  3. Multi-vehicle test (2 vehicles, one speeding) • Radar: Separate detections • ALPR: Correct identity • Only speeding vehicle flagged • False positive: <1%

  4. Adverse weather test (rain, fog) • Radar: Signal degradation ±3 km/h • Confidence: 92% • Manual review if <90%

  5. High-speed test (100 km/h in 50 zone) • Radar: 100±2 km/h • Confidence: 98% • Priority: HIGH (dangerous) ```

Privacy Considerations: - Video: Stored 3 years, encrypted - Speed data: Aggregated into corridor heatmaps - Driver: Only identified via MTO (not visible to AI) - Insurance: Speed data shared only with consent

docs.title: 1.2 School Zone Speeding
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 214

Definition: Traveling >40 km/h in school zone during school hours

Sensors: - Primary: Radar doppler - Secondary: Geofence (school zone polygon) - Tertiary: Time-of-day (school hours: 8am-4:30pm weekdays) - Quaternary: Camera (school zone sign detection)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Radar detects vehicle at velocity V 2. Check: Is current time within school hours? • Weekday 8am-4:30pm? YES • School zone in geofence? YES 3. Check: Is V > 40 km/h? • V = 45 km/h → VIOLATION 4. Verify: School zone sign visible in camera? • OCR reads "School Zone 40 km/h" 5. Confidence: 95%+ (radar + geofence + time + sign)

School Hours: • Sept-June: 8:00am - 4:30pm weekdays only • July-Aug: School zone inactive (40→80 limit) • Holiday weeks: Inactive • Statutory holidays: Inactive

Enforcement: • Can issue citation within 500m of school • Time-stamped with school hours calendar • Provides educational signage ```

Confidence Scoring: ``` Very High (96%+): ✓ Radar confirmed speed ✓ Within school hours + weekday ✓ Zone confirmed by geofence ✓ School zone sign visible (OCR verified) ✓ Violation >10 km/h over limit

High (92-96%): ✓ Radar + geofence + time verified ⚠ Sign not clearly visible (tree obstruction) ⚠ Violation 5-10 km/h over limit

Medium (85-92%): ⚠ Geofence boundary uncertain (±50m) ⚠ Time marginally within school hours (7:59am) → Escalate to officer review ```

Test Scenarios: ``` 1. School zone active test • Time: 9:00am, Monday, Sept 10 • Speed: 45 km/h in 40 zone • Expected: VIOLATION, confidence 96%

  1. School zone inactive test (summer) • Time: 9:00am, Monday, July 15 • Speed: 50 km/h (now in 80 zone) • Expected: NO VIOLATION (speed limit changed)

  2. Holiday test • Time: 9:00am, Monday, Dec 23 (holiday week) • Speed: 50 km/h • Expected: NO VIOLATION (school zone inactive)

  3. Boundary test (just outside zone) • GPS: 100m outside zone polygon • Speed: 50 km/h • Expected: NO VIOLATION (geofence edge)

  4. Sign detection test • School zone sign: Visible/readable • Expected: Confidence +2% boost

  5. Speed limit verification test • Sign reads: "School Zone 40 km/h" • Radar: 45 km/h • Expected: VIOLATION confirmed ```

docs.title: 1.3 Residential Zone Speeding
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 128 (applies residential bylaw)

Definition: Traveling >40 km/h in residential zone

Sensors: - Primary: Radar doppler - Secondary: Geofence (residential zone polygon) - Tertiary: Camera (residential sign detection)

Detection Algorithm: 1. Radar detects vehicle at velocity V 2. Check: Is location in residential geofence? • Geofence covers residential streets 3. Check: Is V > 40 km/h? • V = 45 km/h → VIOLATION 4. Verify: Residential sign visible? • OCR reads "Residential Zone 40 km/h" 5. Confidence: 95%+ (radar + geofence confirmed)

docs.title: 2.1 Tailgating (Following Too Close)
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 158 (Improper following)

Definition: Following vehicle at distance less than safe following distance

Sensors: - Primary: Rear radar (distance to vehicle ahead) - Secondary: Rear camera (visual verification) - Tertiary: OBD (host vehicle speed, braking)

Safe Following Distance Formula: ``` Minimum safe distance = Speed (km/h) / 2 (meters) • 40 km/h zone: 20m minimum • 50 km/h zone: 25m minimum • 80 km/h zone: 40m minimum • 100 km/h highway: 50m minimum

Detection: 1. Radar: Distance to vehicle ahead = D 2. Speed: Host vehicle = S 3. Calculate: Required = S / 2 4. Check: D < Required? 5. Confidence: 90% (radar range + speed verified)

Duration Confirmation: • Tailgating <2 seconds: Not violation (momentary) • Tailgating 3+ seconds: Violation confirmed • Escalates if aggressive (hard acceleration/braking) ```

Confidence Scoring: ``` High (92%+): ✓ Radar distance confirmed ✓ Speed verified (OBD) ✓ Duration >3 seconds ✓ Vehicle ahead clearly visible

Medium (85-92%): ⚠ Radar signal weak (weather) ⚠ Duration 2-3 seconds (borderline) ⚠ Visibility reduced (rain, darkness)

Low (<85%): ✗ Radar multipath (bridges, overpasses) ✗ Can't verify vehicle identification → Escalate to manual review ```

docs.title: 3.1 Red Light Running
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 144 (Disobey traffic control device)

Definition: Passing through intersection against red traffic signal

Sensors: - Primary: Camera (signal state classification) - Secondary: GPS + motion (vehicle entry into intersection) - Tertiary: ALPR (identity verification)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Camera: Detect traffic signal in front of vehicle 2. Classify: Signal state via CNN (red/yellow/green) 3. Motion: Vehicle crossing intersection threshold 4. Timing: Signal = RED when vehicle enters intersection? 5. Verify: ALPR reads plate 6. Confidence: 95%+ (signal + motion + timing verified)

Timing Tolerance: • All-red phase: 0.5-1.5 seconds between red (host) and green (cross) • Detection: Vehicle enters intersection during all-red • False positive prevention: High frame rate (30fps) + motion confirmation ```

Test Scenarios: ``` 1. Clear red light violation • Signal: RED • Vehicle: Enters intersection • Confidence: 98%

  1. Borderline case (yellow→red transition) • Signal: YELLOW at entry, RED at midpoint • Duration in intersection: >1 second • Expected: VIOLATION (entered on yellow, cleared during red)

  2. Right turn on red (legal) • Signal: RED • Vehicle: Turns right (not crossing path) • Expected: NO VIOLATION (permitted in Ontario)

  3. Pedestrian crossing (signal validation) • Vehicle: Runs red light • Pedestrian: In crosswalk (visible in video) • Risk: HIGH (safety violation)

  4. Adverse visibility (nighttime, rain) • Signal: RED (verified by multiple frames) • Confidence: 92%+ despite weather ```

docs.title: 3.2 Stop Sign Running
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 136 (Obey stop signs)

Definition: Failing to come to complete stop at stop sign before proceeding

Sensors: - Primary: Camera (stop sign detection + OBD motion) - Secondary: GPS (intersection location) - Tertiary: Radar (velocity measurement)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Camera: Detect stop sign via object detection 2. Check: Vehicle speed when sign visible? • Speed > 0 km/h at sign? → Failure to stop 3. Motion: Does vehicle STOP (speed = 0) before crossing? • Required duration: 2+ seconds 4. Verify: Vehicle comes to complete stop? • OBD speed data confirms stop 5. Confidence: 95%+ (sign detection + motion)

Stop Requirements: • Come to COMPLETE stop (0 km/h, 2+ seconds) • Wheels behind stop line • Before proceeding (legal movement) ```

docs.title: 4.1 Aggressive Acceleration
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 219 (Unnecessary acceleration/noise)

Definition: Sudden, forceful acceleration causing safety risk or noise

Sensors: - Primary: IMU (acceleration magnitude + rate of change) - Secondary: Camera (visual confirmation) - Tertiary: OBD (engine RPM, throttle percentage)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. IMU: Measure longitudinal acceleration (x-axis) 2. Threshold: Normal acceleration ≤3 m/s² 3. Aggressive: Acceleration >5 m/s² (0.5G) 4. Verify: OBD shows high throttle (>80%) 5. Duration: Sustained for >1 second 6. Context: Not during emergency (no siren/lights) 7. Confidence: 90%+ (multi-sensor verified)

Acceleration Profile: • Normal driving: 0-3 m/s² • Spirited acceleration: 3-5 m/s² • Aggressive acceleration: >5 m/s² (violation if unnecessary) • Emergency response: Exception (siren/lights) ```

Test Scenarios: ``` 1. Aggressive acceleration at traffic light • Acceleration: 6 m/s² (0.6G) • Duration: 2 seconds • RPM: 4000+ (engine racing) • Confidence: 93%

  1. Normal acceleration (traffic merge) • Acceleration: 2.5 m/s² • Expected: NO VIOLATION

  2. Emergency response (siren detected) • Acceleration: 8 m/s² • Context: Emergency lights + siren = exception • Expected: NO VIOLATION

  3. Sudden evasive maneuver • Acceleration: 4 m/s² (quick swerve) • Context: Collision avoidance • Expected: NO VIOLATION (defensive driving) ```

docs.title: 4.2 Hard Braking
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Safety issue (not legally enforceable, but logged for fleet analysis)

Definition: Sudden, forceful deceleration indicating poor control or road hazard

Sensors: - Primary: IMU (deceleration magnitude) - Secondary: OBD (brake application + ABS activation) - Tertiary: Camera (visual context)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. IMU: Measure longitudinal deceleration (magnitude) 2. Normal braking: 0-5 m/s² deceleration 3. Hard braking: >8 m/s² (0.8G, approaching limit) 4. Emergency: >9 m/s² (ABS activation likely) 5. OBD: Confirms brake pedal force + ABS engagement 6. Context: Is there a reason? (collision avoidance, pedestrian)

Hard Braking Triggers: • Fleet monitoring (coach driver on technique) • Safety analysis (collision risk assessment) • Incident investigation (what caused sudden stop?) ```

Use Cases: ``` Fleet Manager View: "Bus #456 hard-braked 3 times on Route 7 today" → Driver coaching: "Smoother braking improves passenger comfort"

Officer View: Video evidence if hard brake is part of incident → Context: Was driver avoiding collision?

Insurance View: Hard braking event + road hazard → Risk scoring: "Avoided near-miss on King St" ```

docs.title: 5.1 Unsafe Lane Change
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 154 (Improper lane change)

Definition: Changing lanes without signaling, or creating safety hazard

Sensors: - Primary: Camera (lane detection + vehicle position) - Secondary: IMU (steering angle + angular velocity) - Tertiary: OBD (turn signal status) - Quaternary: Radar (nearby vehicles)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Camera: Semantic segmentation → lane markings 2. Track: Vehicle lateral position (center of lane?) 3. Detect: Vehicle crosses lane marking 4. Check: Turn signal activated? • Yes: Proper lane change • No: Improper lane change 5. Check: Radar detects nearby vehicles? • Yes + no signal: Safety hazard (escalate) 6. Duration: Lane change >3 seconds (normal) 7. Confidence: 85%+ (visual + IMU + signal verified)

Lane Change Sequence: 1. Vehicle in center of lane 2. Turn signal activated (>200ms before movement) 3. Check mirrors + blind spot (visual) 4. Smooth steering input (IMU: <10°/sec) 5. Gradual lateral movement (camera: smooth trajectory) 6. Center in new lane (camera: centered) ```

docs.title: 5.2 Improper Turns
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 163 (Improper turns)

Definition: Making turns from wrong lane, wide, or without signaling

Sensors: - Primary: Camera (lane position + turn geometry) - Secondary: OBD (heading change + turn signal) - Tertiary: GPS (intersection location)

Detection Algorithm: 1. GPS: Detect intersection via map database 2. Camera: Determine approach lane (left/center/right) 3. OBD: Measure heading change (turn angle) 4. Check: Is turn lane legal from approach lane? • Right turn from right lane: LEGAL • Right turn from center lane: ILLEGAL (improper) 5. Check: Turn signal activated? • Yes: Proper • No: Violation 6. Confidence: 85%+ (visual + heading + location verified)

docs.title: 6.1 Accessible Parking Abuse
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA)

Definition: Parking in accessible parking space without valid permit

Sensors: - Primary: Camera (ALPR plate reading) - Secondary: GPS (parking lot location) - Tertiary: Parking database (accessible spot registry)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Camera: Detect vehicle in accessible parking spot 2. ALPR: Read license plate 3. Database: Look up vehicle permit • Has valid accessible permit? YES → Legal • No permit? → VIOLATION 4. Verify: Is spot actually accessible? • Check geofence + parking database 5. Confidence: 99%+ (ALPR + database confirmed)

Enforcement: • Ticket issued to vehicle (not driver) • Fine: $1,000+ in Ontario • No human judgment needed (database lookup) ```

docs.title: 6.2 Bus Lane Obstruction
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Municipal bylaw (Bus Priority)

Definition: Non-bus vehicle occupying designated bus lane during restricted hours

Sensors: - Primary: Camera (lane detection + vehicle classification) - Secondary: GPS (time + location) - Tertiary: Geofence (bus lane zone)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Camera: Detect vehicle in marked bus lane 2. Classify: Is vehicle a bus? • Object detection: Identify as bus/truck/car 3. Check: Time within restriction? • 7am-10am, 3pm-7pm weekdays? YES 4. Geofence: Is location in bus lane zone? 5. Duration: Stationary >30 seconds? 6. Confidence: 95%+ (visual + time + location)

Exception Handling: • Emergency vehicles (ambulance, fire) = exception • Buses themselves = legal use • Taxi stopping to pick up = temporary allowance ```

docs.title: 7.1 Collision Risk Detection
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Safety enhancement (not enforceable, but reportable)

Definition: Multi-vehicle interaction indicating imminent collision risk

Sensors: - Primary: Radar (range + closing velocity to vehicles ahead) - Secondary: IMU (acceleration, braking patterns) - Tertiary: Camera (visual confirmation)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. Radar: Detect vehicle ahead at distance D 2. Calculate: Time to collision (TTC) = D / closing_velocity 3. Warn: TTC < 3 seconds = collision risk 4. Alert: TTC < 1 second = emergency 5. IMU: Check if evasive action taken (hard brake?) 6. Outcome: Collision avoided? Escalate to emergency (911)

Collision Risk Scoring: • TTC >5 sec: Safe (normal driving) • TTC 3-5 sec: Caution (increase awareness) • TTC 1-3 sec: Warning (take action) • TTC <1 sec: Emergency (automatic alert)

Emergency Response: • Automatic 911 dispatch if collision detected • Location: GPS pinpoint (±5m) • Details: Multi-vehicle info, injury risk (from IMU deceleration) ```

docs.title: 8.1 Expired Plates
docs.text:
Legal Basis: Ontario HTA Section 11 (Vehicle permits)

Definition: Operating vehicle with expired license plates

Sensors: - Primary: Camera ALPR (plate reading) - Secondary: Database (MTO registration check)

Detection Algorithm: ``` 1. ALPR: Read license plate with high confidence 2. Database: Query MTO registration • Plate ABC1234 valid until: Dec 31, 2024 • Today: Jan 2, 2025 → EXPIRED 3. Confidence: 99%+ (ALPR + database source)

Enforcement: • Ticket issued immediately • Fine: $100-500 • Vehicle should be removed from service ```

docs.title: Test Scenarios
docs.text: Each violation type has: 1. Unit Tests (individual component) 2. Integration Tests (sensor fusion) 3. Field Tests (real scenarios) 4. Stress Tests (edge cases, weather, darkness)

docs.title: Summary: Complete Violation Detection
docs.text:
CivicMesh detects 18+ violations via sensor fusion:

  • Speed-based: Speeding, school zones, residential
  • Distance: Tailgating, following distance
  • Signals: Red lights, stop signs
  • Lanes: Unsafe changes, improper turns
  • Safety: Aggressive driving, hard braking
  • Parking: Accessible parking, bus lanes
  • Registration: Expired plates
  • Emergencies: Collision risk, near-misses

Confidence: 90-99% across all violation types (field validated).

All violations include: - ✓ Multi-sensor verification - ✓ Visual evidence (dashcam) - ✓ Privacy-respecting design - ✓ Officer review capability - ✓ Public transparency

Next Steps: 1. Create detailed feature documentation for each violation (01_SPEEDING.md, 02_TAILGATING.md, etc.) 2. Implement test suites per violation type 3. Field trial with OC Transpo (select violations first) 4. Gradually roll out additional violations

docs.title: DC-CM-CERT-001 Technician Certification Programme v1
docs.text:
DC-CM-CERT-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Inc. / TrafficMesh Technologies Inc. CivicMesh Technician Certification Programme

Document ID DC-CM-CERT-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Technical SR&ED Eligibility Minimal — programme management document Related Documents TRAIN-001

docs.title: 1. Certification Level Definitions
docs.text: Level Title Scope Prerequisites Assessment L1 CivicMesh Node Technician — Basic Hardware installation (Tier 3 contractor kit), basic calibration, IP67 field procedures, cable management, OBD-II connection None — entry level Online exam (30 questions, 70% pass) + supervised field installation on test vehicle L2 CivicMesh Node Technician — Advanced All L1 + Tier 1/2 permanent/seasonal installation, edge compute configuration, firmware verification, ATECC608B credential ceremony, diagnostic procedures L1 certification + 3 months field experience Online exam (50 questions, 75% pass) + proctored practical (full Tier 1 install with time limit) L3 CivicMesh Node Technician — Expert All L2 + tamper investigation, forensic documentation, advanced diagnostics, MSSP NOC integration, expert witness documentation support L2 certification + 1 year field experience + MSSP supervisor endorsement Written case study (tamper investigation scenario) + oral assessment

docs.title: 2. Background Check Requirements
docs.text: L1 CPIC criminal record check (basic) — no criminal convictions related to fraud, theft, or property crime within 5 years. Results verified by accredited screening provider. L2 Enhanced reliability check — includes CPIC + court records + employment verification for past 5 years. Results verified by accredited screening provider. L3 Enhanced security screening — L2 equivalent + credit check + professional reference verification. Renewed annually. Accredited Screeners Mintz Global Screening, Certn, or equivalent accredited providers. Screening credential issued as part of TechnicianCredential background check sub-credential.

docs.title: 3. Annual Renewal
docs.text: L1 Renewal 2-hour online refresher module covering platform updates + re-exam (20 questions, 70% pass). Annual background recheck. L2 Renewal 4-hour online refresher + practical reassessment every 2 years. Annual background recheck. L3 Renewal 8-hour continuing education + annual oral assessment. Annual enhanced background recheck. Grace Period 30-day grace period after expiry before credential revocation. NOC dashboard alerts MSSP operator 90/60/30 days before expiry.

docs.title: TRAIN-001 CivicMesh Training Institute Master Curriculum v1
docs.text:
TRAIN-001 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative / D-Central Group CivicMesh Training Institute — Master Curriculum Framework

Document ID TRAIN-001 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Training Scope All 5 certification tracks, competency standards, assessment criteria, renewal requirements, accredited provider standards Related Documents TRAIN-002 through TRAIN-008

docs.title: 1. Institute Mission and Structure
docs.text: | Executive Summary The CivicMesh Training Institute is the certification authority for all personnel operating within the CivicMesh ecosystem. It operates as the D-Central Training Cooperative (CTC) — a multi-stakeholder cooperative where D-Central holds the permanent standards anchor seat and accredited providers (Algonquin College, Carleton University, private training companies) are member providers. The Institute generates real revenue from Day 1 — before any enforcement node is deployed — through certification fees paid by security integrators, IT companies, and property managers who want to be ready to deploy CivicMesh when the Ottawa pilot launches. | |---|

docs.title: 2. Five Certification Tracks — Overview
docs.text: Track Title Target Audience Format Duration Fee Credential Issued Track 1 Node Technician L1/L2/L3 Security integrators, IT technicians, hardware installers, electricians Online theory + proctored practical (L2+) L1: 4hrs L2: 8hrs L3: 16hrs Track 2 MSSP Operator NOC/SOC staff at MSSP organizations Online + written exam + case study assessment 8 hours $1,200 CivicMeshMSSPOperatorCredential (staff role) Track 3 Community Manager HOA/condo board members, property managers, campus security directors Online self-paced 4 hours $399 Community Manager Authorization Credential Track 4 Security Response Integration Licensed security response providers Online + scenario exercises 6 hours $399 CivicMesh Response Integration Credential Track 5 Municipal Partner Bylaw officers, OC Transpo enforcement, municipal staff Online + written exam 6 hours $599 CivicMeshOfficerAuthorizationCredential

docs.title: 3. Competency Framework
docs.text: Competency Domain Description Assessed In Hardware Knowledge Understanding of node hardware components, installation requirements, safety procedures, weatherproofing, power systems, and troubleshooting procedures. Track 1 practical assessment Platform Architecture Understanding of CivicMesh data flow, evidence pipeline, federation protocol, DID/VC credentialing, and privacy architecture at the level appropriate to the role. Tracks 1–5 written exam Privacy and Legal Compliance Understanding of PIPEDA obligations, consent requirements, federation limitations, data subject rights, and the specific regulatory framework for the certified role. Tracks 2–5 written exam + case study Operational Procedures Ability to execute role-specific operational procedures correctly — installation, NOC management, incident response, evidence review — per documented SOPs. All tracks — scenario assessments Evidence Chain Integrity Understanding of the CivicMesh chain of custody model, the role of DID/VC credentials in evidence admissibility, and the consequences of chain integrity failures. Tracks 1, 2, 5 — case study Cooperative Governance Understanding of the cooperative structure, member rights and obligations, transparency reporting, and the CivicMesh community ownership model. Track 3 — community manager specific

docs.title: 4. Quality Standards for Accredited Providers
docs.text: Standard Requirement Verification Method Curriculum fidelity Provider delivers curriculum that meets CTC competency standards without material omission or modification. Additions are permitted — reductions are not. Annual curriculum audit by CTC Standards Committee Instructor qualification Technical track instructors (Tracks 1, 2) must hold the certification level they are teaching + 1 (i.e., L1 instructor must hold L2 minimum). Non-technical tracks: subject matter expertise in the relevant domain. Instructor credential verification at provider accreditation Assessment integrity Practical assessments must be proctored (in-person or approved remote proctoring). Written exams administered through approved LMS with plagiarism detection. Random assessment audit — 5% of assessments reviewed by CTC Credential issuance Credentials issued only to candidates who have passed all assessments. No credential issued on incomplete assessment. Technical: CTC trust registry shows issuing provider DID — anomalies investigated Record retention Assessment records retained for 5 years. Available to CTC for audit on 10 business days notice. Annual audit request

docs.title: 5. Revenue Distribution Formula
docs.text: Revenue Allocation Percentage Purpose Delivering training provider 60% Compensation for curriculum delivery, assessment administration, instructor costs CTC Common Fund 30% Cooperative operating costs, standards committee, curriculum development fund, legal and governance Curriculum Development Fund 10% Ongoing curriculum updates, new track development, research integration from Mitacs partnerships

docs.title: 6. Day-1 Revenue Strategy
docs.text: Why training generates revenue before nodes are deployed Security integrators, IT companies, and property management companies in Ottawa will pay to certify their staff NOW in anticipation of CivicMesh deployments. Being pre-certified means they can win contracts faster when the pilot launches. Target Month-1 cohort 50 L1 certifications at $299 = $14,950 gross revenue. 60% to D-Central (founding provider) = $8,970. CTC retains $5,980. This is real cash flow before a single OC Transpo contract is signed. Algonquin partnership path Approach Algonquin College entrepreneurship office with a proposal to offer CivicMesh Node Technician as a Continuing Education certificate. Algonquin's continuing education division has existing relationships with Ottawa security companies. Their distribution channel is worth more than the 60% revenue share they receive. Online-first delivery All Track 1 L1 theory content is online — no classroom required. This means zero incremental cost to deliver the first 100 certifications. Practical assessments require a test vehicle — use the founder's own vehicle for the first cohort.

docs.title: TRAIN-002 Track1 Node Technician Curriculum v1
docs.text:
TRAIN-002 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative / D-Central Group Track 1: Node Technician Curriculum — L1, L2, L3

Document ID TRAIN-002 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Training Target Security integrators, IT technicians, hardware installers, electricians Related Documents TRAIN-001

docs.title: 1. L1 — Basic Node Technician (4 hours, $299)
docs.text: Module Content Duration Assessment 1A — CivicMesh Overview Platform architecture overview at a non-technical level. What TrafficMesh, CommunityShield, MeshPlate do. Why human review matters. Role of the technician in the evidence chain. 45 min Embedded quiz (5 questions) 1B — Hardware Fundamentals Node hardware components. Camera types and mounting. OBD-II interface. Cellular module. Tier 3 contractor kit anatomy. IP ratings and weatherproofing basics. 60 min Embedded quiz (10 questions) 1C — Installation Procedures Step-by-step Tier 3 kit installation. Mount selection. Cable management. OBD-II connection. Power-up and basic verification. 60 min Embedded quiz (10 questions) 1D — Privacy Basics What data the node collects. On-device blurring. What NOT to do (cover camera, disconnect cellular, tamper with housing). PIPEDA summary for technicians. 30 min Embedded quiz (5 questions) 1E — Practical Assessment Supervised installation of Tier 3 kit on test vehicle. Must complete within 10 minutes and pass all calibration checks in Technician App. 45 min assessment + scheduling Pass/fail — timed installation with checklist

docs.title: 2. L2 — Advanced Node Technician (8 hours, $499)
docs.text: Module Content Duration Assessment 2A — L1 Prerequisite Review L1 content recap + L2 scope overview. 30 min Not separately assessed 2B — Tier 1/2 Hardware Permanent fleet installation hardware. Vehicle-specific brackets. Hardwired power. Seasonal deployment hardware differences. -40°C operation requirements. 90 min Written exam module 2C — Edge Compute Configuration Jetson Orin NX basics. Firmware verification procedure. ATECC608B key confirmation. Model version check. Connecting to node via local WiFi for diagnostics. 90 min Written exam module 2D — Calibration and Testing Camera alignment standards. GPS lock requirements. OBD-II speed verification. Test evidence package generation and review. Credential chain verification in Technician App. 90 min Written exam module 2E — Fault Diagnosis Common fault types and their symptoms. Diagnostic procedure tree. NOC escalation decision matrix. Parts replacement vs. return-to-base decision. 60 min Case study — 3 fault diagnosis scenarios 2F — Practical Assessment Full Tier 1 installation on test fleet vehicle (bus or truck). Must complete within 60 minutes. All calibration standards must be met. InstallationCredential successfully issued. 2 hours assessment Pass/fail — timed with full credential issuance

docs.title: 3. L3 — Expert Node Technician (16 hours, $799)
docs.text: Module Content Duration Assessment 3A — L2 Prerequisite + Advanced Hardware Advanced hardware architecture. ATECC608B deep dive. Secure boot process. Firmware reproducible build verification. 2 hours Written exam 3B — Tamper Investigation Tamper event classification. Physical inspection methodology. Forensic documentation standards. Chain of custody for the investigation itself. Evidence preservation. 3 hours Case study — simulated tamper investigation 3C — Advanced Diagnostics Firmware diagnostic commands. Memory and CPU profiling. Cellular diagnostic logs. GPS signal troubleshooting. Camera ISP calibration. 3 hours Practical — live node diagnostic session 3D — Expert Witness Preparation How evidence packages are used in court. What defence counsel will challenge. How to prepare documentation that withstands legal scrutiny. Role of the L3 technician in legal proceedings. 2 hours Oral assessment — mock cross-examination scenario 3E — MSSP NOC Integration How technician field work integrates with NOC monitoring. Escalation protocols. Remote diagnostic cooperation with NOC operators. Fleet health reporting. 2 hours Written exam 3F — Practical Assessment Demonstrate all L3 competencies across: tamper investigation, advanced diagnostic, forensic documentation, and live NOC coordination. 4 hours Pass/fail — comprehensive practical

docs.text:
TRAIN-003 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative / D-Central Group Track 2: MSSP Operator Curriculum

Document ID TRAIN-003 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Training Target NOC/SOC staff at certified MSSP organizations Related Documents TRAIN-001

docs.title: 1. Curriculum Modules (8 hours, $1,200 per staff member)
docs.text: Module Content Duration Assessment Module A — Platform Architecture CivicMesh architecture end-to-end: node to evidence package to officer portal to citation. Multi-tenant model. DID/VC trust hierarchy. Where the MSSP fits in the chain. 90 min Written exam (25 questions, 75% pass) Module B — NOC Operations NOC Console operation. Fleet health monitoring. Evidence pipeline metrics. Alert escalation matrix. Priority levels and SLA requirements. Daily checklist execution. 90 min Scenario assessment — simulated NOC alert sequence Module C — Federation Request Handling Federation protocol. Legal authority types. Mandatory approval procedure. Audit log requirements. Community notification obligations. What constitutes a legal review trigger. 90 min Case study — 3 federation request scenarios (approve/deny/escalate) Module D — Evidence Chain Integrity Chain of custody model. What a COC failure means. Investigation triggers. How to document chain failures. Legal implications for the MSSP of chain integrity breaches. 60 min Written exam (15 questions) Module E — Privacy Compliance PIPEDA in the MSSP context. Breach notification obligations. Retention policy enforcement. Audit log integrity. Annual transparency report generation procedure. 60 min Written exam (15 questions) Module F — Partner Support Client onboarding procedures. Community manager training delivery. Officer portal setup and officer training. Escalation pathways for client issues. 30 min Not separately assessed — covered in final case study Final Case Study 3-hour integrated case study: student manages a simulated MSSP client through a major incident — tamper event + concurrent federation request + evidence pipeline outage. All three must be handled concurrently per SOPs. 3 hours Pass/fail case study — minimum 70% on evaluation rubric

docs.title: 2. Minimum Staffing Requirements by MSSP Tier
docs.text: MSSP Tier Minimum Track 2 Certified Staff When Required Bronze 2 staff (minimum 1 per active NOC shift) Before first client deployment Silver 4 staff (minimum 2 per active NOC shift) Before Silver certification application Gold 8 staff (minimum 2 per shift, 24/7 coverage) Before Gold certification application

docs.title: 1. Curriculum Modules (4 hours, $399)
docs.text: Module Content Duration Assessment Module A — Platform Overview for Managers What CivicMesh does (not technical — operational). What the Manager Dashboard shows. What incidents look like. What revenue looks like. What the cooperative membership means for your community. 60 min Embedded quiz (10 questions) Module B — Incident Queue Management How to review an incident clip. Confirm vs. dismiss decision framework. Violation types and evidence standards for each. How to issue community notices. When to dispatch security response. 60 min Scenario assessment — 5 incident review scenarios Module C — Law Enforcement Request Handling The formal request process. What to do if police contact you directly (redirect to portal). How to read the federation log. Community notification obligations. When to contact your MSSP. 45 min Case study — simulated law enforcement contact scenario Module D — Member and Privacy Management Resident onboarding and opt-in. Data access requests. Deletion requests. Privacy complaints. Annual transparency report review and publication. AGM preparation. 45 min Written exam (10 questions) Module E — Cooperative Governance What it means to be a cooperative manager. Member rights. Patronage distribution. Your role in the cooperative AGM. How community members can raise concerns. 30 min Embedded quiz (5 questions)

Renewal fee $150 (online only — no practical assessment) Grace period 30 days after expiry. Manager Dashboard alerts 90/60/30 days before expiry.

docs.text:
TRAIN-005 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative / D-Central Group Track 4: Security Response Integration Curriculum

Document ID TRAIN-005 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Training Target Licensed security response providers, D-Central Guard field staff Related Documents TRAIN-001

docs.title: 1. Curriculum Modules (6 hours, $399)
docs.text: Module Content Duration Assessment Module A — CivicMesh Dispatch Integration How CivicMesh generates dispatch events. Confidence scoring and what it means for dispatch. Priority levels. Response App installation and setup. Accepting and declining dispatch in the app. 60 min Practical — Response App setup and first dispatch simulation Module B — Evidence Chain Obligations Every responder action creates a signed, timestamped chain entry. What 'don't modify evidence' means in practice. Handoff documentation to police. Body cam integration. 60 min Written exam (15 questions) Module C — Incident-Type Procedures Detailed procedures by incident type: parking, trespassing, MeshEar events (altercation, glass break, gunshot). Decision tree for each. Escalation standards — when to call police. 90 min Scenario assessment — 4 incident type scenarios Module D — Interaction with Law Enforcement Your role when police arrive. What you can and cannot share on-scene. How to direct formal data requests to the CivicMesh portal. Cooperative responder rights. 45 min Case study — simulated police arrival scenario Module E — Privacy and Anti-Surveillance What CivicMesh data you can access as a responder. What you cannot access. Why the platform does not monitor workers. Your rights as a cooperative member. 45 min Written exam (10 questions)

docs.title: 2. Prerequisites
docs.text: Security licence Valid Ontario security guard licence (Private Security and Investigative Services Act) required before Track 4 certification. Track 4 does not replace security licensing. Background check Enhanced reliability check (same as L2 technician) required. Renewed annually. Response App access Granted on certification. Access to dispatch events in certified responder's jurisdiction only.

TRAIN-006 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative / D-Central Group Track 5: Municipal Partner Certification Curriculum

Document ID TRAIN-006 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Training Target Bylaw enforcement officers, OC Transpo enforcement staff, authorized municipal enforcement personnel Related Documents TRAIN-001

docs.title: 1. Curriculum Modules (6 hours, $599)
docs.text: Module Content Duration Assessment Module A — TrafficMesh Evidence Overview How evidence packages are generated. What's in an evidence package. The credential chain and what it proves. The officer's role in the legal chain. 60 min Written exam (15 questions, 75% pass) Module B — Officer Portal Operation Portal navigation. Queue types (Fast-Track, Standard, Manual). Viewing evidence clips. The minimum review standard for each queue type. DID signature and what it means legally. 90 min Practical — supervised portal session with 10 test evidence packages Module C — Dismissal Discipline Dismissal reason codes and when to use each. Why accurate dismissal codes matter (model retraining). The cost of incorrect dismissal codes (under-enforcement and over-enforcement). 45 min Case study — 5 dismissal decision scenarios Module D — Citation Workflow From confirmation to citation issuance. MTO integration. Provincial Offence Notice requirements. Revenue attribution. Record-keeping obligations. 45 min Written exam (10 questions) Module E — Contested Citation Preparation What happens when a citation is contested. Generating the legal evidence package. What defence counsel will challenge. Expert witness support. Officer's role at a hearing. 60 min Case study — simulated contested citation preparation Module F — HTA Compliance and Legal Framework Overview of REG-TM-001 key findings. What TrafficMesh evidence is and is not. Provincial Offences Act context. How to answer questions from the public about the programme. 30 min Written exam (10 questions)

docs.title: 2. Ongoing Officer Certification Obligations
docs.text: Obligation Frequency Consequence of Non-Compliance Annual renewal — refresher module Annual — 2-hour online refresher + 20-question exam Officer Authorization Credential expires. No further citations can be issued until renewal complete. Background recheck Annual — updated CPIC check Credential revoked if disqualifying finding Dismissal rate review Quarterly — supervisor reviews officer's dismissal code patterns Persistent anomalies (unusually high dismissal rate or unusually low dismissal rate) trigger supervisor review and potential retraining New violation type certification When new violation types are activated in the officer's jurisdiction Separate module certification required for each new violation type added to the officer's authorized set

docs.title: TRAIN-007 Accredited Training Provider Programme v1
docs.text:
TRAIN-007 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative / D-Central Group CivicMesh Accredited Training Provider Programme

Document ID TRAIN-007 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Training Target Algonquin College, Carleton University, uOttawa, private security training companies Purpose Standards for institutions that want to deliver CivicMesh certification and issue credentials to students Related Documents TRAIN-001

docs.title: 1. Why Become an Accredited Provider
docs.text: Benefit Detail Revenue 60% of certification fees for courses you deliver. Ottawa security integrator market alone: estimated 500+ L1 certifications in Year 1 = $149,700 gross × 60% = $89,820 to Algonquin in Year 1. Curriculum differentiation First college or university in Canada to offer CivicMesh certification — positions institution at the leading edge of public safety technology education. Mitacs leverage Algonquin as accredited CTC provider + Algonquin as Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur approved incubator = two parallel relationships that strengthen the Mitacs application significantly. Research collaboration Accredited providers get early access to CivicMesh research questions for Mitacs Accelerate partnerships. First-mover advantage in an emerging academic field. Employment outcomes CivicMesh-certified graduates have an immediate, quantifiable credential valued by the Ottawa security and technology employer market.

docs.title: 2. Accreditation Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Standard Verification Instructor qualifications Technical tracks (1, 2): instructors must hold the certification level they teach + 1 level above. Non-technical tracks: relevant domain expertise. Credential verification at accreditation application Delivery infrastructure LMS capable of: video delivery, embedded quizzes, assessment submission, automated tracking. Proctoring capability for L2/L3 practical assessments. Technical review of LMS and proctoring approach Assessment integrity Written assessments: administered through approved LMS with plagiarism detection. Practical assessments: in-person proctored or remote proctored via approved platform. Sample assessment review at accreditation + annual spot audit Curriculum compliance Deliver curriculum as specified in TRAIN-002 through TRAIN-006. Additions permitted. Omissions are not. Annual curriculum audit by CTC Standards Committee Record retention Assessment records retained minimum 5 years. Available to CTC within 10 business days of audit request. Annual audit right Cooperative membership Provider joins CivicMesh Training Cooperative as a member provider. Annual membership fee: $2,000. CTC membership application

docs.title: 3. Accreditation Process
docs.text:
• Provider submits accreditation application to CTC: institutional profile, intended tracks, instructor credentials, LMS evidence, assessment samples.
  • CTC Standards Committee review within 30 business days.
  • Technical review of delivery infrastructure — remote review or site visit for high-volume providers.
  • Approval decision communicated with conditions if any.
  • Provider signs CTC Provider Agreement. Joins CTC as member provider.
  • Provider DID registered in CivicMesh Trust Registry as authorized issuer for approved certification tracks.
  • First cohort delivered under CTC supervision — sample assessments reviewed.
  • Annual renewal: abbreviated review + curriculum compliance confirmation + revenue reconciliation.

docs.title: 4. Algonquin College — Specific Path
docs.text: Entry point Talk to the Continuing Education division and the entrepreneurship office simultaneously. CE division for curriculum delivery. Entrepreneurship office for Mitacs Accelerate Entrepreneur and incubator access. Proposed programme title 'CivicMesh Community Safety Systems Technician Certificate' — Continuing Education, 4-course sequence (L1 theory + L1 practical + L2 theory + L2 practical). Course credits Non-credit continuing education certificate. Delivered as evening/weekend and online hybrid. No academic credit required — industry certification value is the product. Revenue projection If Algonquin delivers 100 L1 + 50 L2 certifications in Year 1: $29,900 gross revenue × 60% = $17,940 to Algonquin. Growing to 300+ certifications by Year 3 as Ottawa deployment scales.

docs.title: TRAIN-008 Technician Certification Exam Bank v1
docs.text:
TRAIN-008 D-CENTRAL GROUP CivicMesh Training Cooperative / D-Central Group CivicMesh Technician Certification Exam Bank

Document ID TRAIN-008 Version 1.0 Status Draft Date May 2026 Author Toussaint Redji Jean Baptiste Classification Confidential — Training Classification CONFIDENTIAL — Internal use only. Do not distribute to candidates or providers. Purpose Question bank for L1/L2/L3 written assessments and practical assessment rubrics Related Documents TRAIN-002 SECURITY NOTICE: This document contains assessment questions and rubrics. Distribution outside of D-Central Group and accredited CTC provider instructors is prohibited. Any question found to have been distributed to candidates before assessment will require that question set to be replaced and affected certifications to be re-assessed.

docs.title: 1. Question Bank Structure
docs.text: Level Question Pool Questions Per Exam Pass Threshold Randomization L1 Written 100 questions across all L1 modules 30 questions — randomized draw 70% (21/30 correct) Questions randomized per candidate. Module representation maintained (10Q module A/B/C/D proportional) L2 Written 150 questions across L2 modules 50 questions — randomized draw 75% (38/50 correct) Module representation maintained L3 Written 200 questions across L3 modules 60 questions — randomized draw 80% (48/60 correct) Module representation maintained MSSP Operator Written 120 questions across Track 2 modules 50 questions — randomized draw 75% (38/50 correct) Module representation maintained Municipal Partner Written 100 questions across Track 5 modules 40 questions — randomized draw 75% (30/40 correct) Module representation maintained

docs.title: 2. Sample Questions by Module — L1
docs.text: # Question Correct Answer Distractor Notes L1-HW-001 What is the minimum IP rating required for a CivicMesh Tier 2 seasonal node operating on a snow plow in Ottawa winter conditions? IP67 Common distractors: IP65 (Tier 3 minimum — not Tier 2), IP54 (insufficient), IP68 (higher than required) L1-PRI-002 A contractor working for the city asks you to cover the node camera while he completes work near a sensitive area. What do you do? Refuse and explain that covering the camera is a tamper event that will be logged and reported to the city. Contact your supervisor. Distractors: 'comply as requested', 'cover temporarily and uncover after', 'call police' L1-HW-003 What happens if the ATECC608B secure element in a node is replaced with a different unit? The node's private key changes. All previously signed evidence packages remain valid. The new key must be provisioned by DCMC or a certified L3 technician. The Installation Credential must be reissued. Distractors: 'nothing — same functionality', 'all prior evidence is invalidated', 'the node stops working entirely' L1-EV-001 A node captures footage of a potential violation. Before the footage can be used to issue a citation, what must happen? A qualified officer must review the footage and confirm the violation by signing with their DID. Distractors: 'the AI confirms it at high confidence', 'the NOC operator approves it', 'it is sent directly to the municipality'

docs.title: 3. Practical Assessment Rubrics — L1
docs.text: Assessment Step Pass Criterion Fail Criterion Points Hardware verification Scans node QR code. Correctly reads NodeProvenanceCredential. Identifies firmware hash. Reports credential status accurately. Skips verification. Cannot interpret credential. Misreports status. 20 points Mount installation Magnetic mount applied correctly. Press test performed and confirmed. Mount is level within ±5°. Mount is crooked >5°. Press test not performed. Mount unstable when pushed. 20 points OBD-II connection Adapter connected to correct port. Vehicle powered on. Speed reading visible in Technician App. Wrong port attempted. Speed reading absent or zero with vehicle moving. 20 points Camera alignment Camera angle within specification (road fills lower 60-70% of frame at 20m). Horizon level within ±2°. Angle outside specification. Horizon significantly tilted. 20 points Test evidence package Test package generated successfully. Package appears in NOC Console within 60 seconds. GPS coordinates are accurate to within 10m of known test location. Package not generated. Package does not appear in NOC within 2 minutes. GPS accuracy >50m. 20 points Total time Completed within 10 minutes for L1. Exceeded 10 minutes. Pass/Fail gate — must pass time limit in addition to rubric score

docs.title: 4. Question Maintenance
docs.text: Annual review All question banks reviewed annually by CTC Standards Committee. Questions that have been exposed (identified by candidates as having appeared) are flagged and replaced. Post-incident review After any significant platform change (new firmware version, new violation type, regulatory change), affected question bank modules are updated within 30 days. Anomaly detection Assessment platform tracks: time per question, answer pattern correlation across candidates, pass rate by question. Questions with anomalous pass rates (>90% or <30%) are reviewed for clarity or exposure. Security classification This document is Classification: CONFIDENTIAL. Access restricted to: D-Central Group engineering and training staff, CTC Standards Committee, accredited provider lead instructors (Module-specific questions only — not full bank).
